I know that there is no country on planet Earth that takes more measures to ensure that civilian harm or civilian casualties are minimized than the United States of America and this War Department, and that is a fact in human history. Okay, and yet, Mr. Secretary, you eliminated the department's civilian harm reduction staff. You eliminated, for a country that makes great efforts to make sure human casualties are reduced or eliminated, don't you think that goes actually counter to what you just said? Would these children not still be alive if you had not eliminated all of these staff working within CENTCOM? We didn't eliminate them. We made sure that those things that they normally accomplish are at the field level, which they are, and all of these processes are in place at every level. At every single level. Would you not agree that something clearly has failed, whether it's our implementation of AI, whether it's understanding our human dependence on AI? Would you not agree something failed because almost 200 children died in Iran as a result of our bombing? You're insinuating something where an investigation is not complete. Gentlelady's time has expired. Chair now recognizes the gentleman from Georgia, Dr. McCormick. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Gentlemen, I'm happy to have you here today and really excited about the things you've been able to accomplish. Just to summarize, sitting here for a few minutes listening to everybody politicize what we've done, I'm just going to break it down as best as I can and then I'm going to let you answer a couple of questions. First of all, just since the short period of time that the Trump administration has taken over, we've handed over control of Ukraine's destiny to Europe where it belongs—a conglomerate that has a 20-trillion-dollar economy versus a 2-trillion-dollar Russian economy to actually take care of their own country in a very good way, their own continent. We've kept Iran, who has literally talked about raining down fire from heaven and talked about us being the Great Satan, from becoming a nuclear power. We've reshaped the geopolitical space in the Persian Gulf. We've re-established the Monroe Doctrine in the Western Hemisphere. We've basically executed two of the most amazing raids in history, almost flawlessly, that will be studied for a generation at least. We reduced senior administrative billets, thank you. We've defeated Iran with what, under 30 billion dollars spent, with minimal civilian casualties compared to any other war. People forget that in World War II, in one night we lost a hundred thousand civilians, totally innocent, in firebombings just before we dropped the nuclear bombs. War is a horrible thing. But I think we've executed at least as best as possible in a very hard and consequential environment. That's just to sum up a few things, not to mention the recruiting that went from dismal and really something that could have crippled our military to now a surplus just by a change of administration. That's pretty impressive for a short period of time. Thank you, and I commend you for overcoming those hardships. A couple of things that I wanted to get through. One thing is we talked about the Incentivized Savings Act, which is something I wanted to apply to all bureaucracies. When I was in the military as both an operational officer and a logistics officer for my squadrons and my jump places, we got to the end of the fiscal year. If we didn't spend it, what happens? We lose it and we get our budget shrunk. Everybody knows that in the military, and everybody knows that in every bureaucracy in America. One of the things I came up with is, at the end of the fiscal year, if you don't spend it, you don't have anything. You know, we save 10% of our budget for emergencies like we're facing right now. And if you don't spend it, instead of spending it, you actually can save half of it: 49% rolls over to the next year, 49% goes back to debt and deficit, and 2% to reward the people for being good administrators of the money. I don't see any downside. This is the way that we reinvent government and the way we spend money. I wanted your opinion on something like that that I thought was thinking outside the box, because I've seen waste, fraud, and abuse. We're trying to address that. This should be a bipartisan, bicameral approach to actually doing something right as we expand the budget, but also give us some wiggle room for putting money back and being good stewards of the people's money. What do you think about an idea like that? I very much like an idea like that, which creates all the right kinds of incentives. And I would just add, in addition to that, which is the type of thing our department would support, the deals that we're cutting with these private sector companies on munitions ensures they build the plants, they build the assembly lines, which is exactly the right kind of incentive. If you can spend on capital, on facilities, you're going to make sure you make money on those facilities and the munitions that you produce. There's ownership in that stake; there's the right incentive. That's overturning the entire way that the department does business. So I agree with that. We're pointing at the defense contractors that the President has and saying, "You need to do your business differently." This is not a gravy train of the government. You need to be—you're beholden to us, actually. So I think the spirit of that is infused into everything we're doing at the department, and I think it's right in line with what you're talking about: running it more like a business where we have accountability and we're in the right direction. We do have a lot of old programs we need to update. One of the things you talked about is a five- to seven-year period of time for updating. I think almost year-by-year now, we're going to have to look at how we budget things and contract because the technologies are so dramatic, as General Caine pointed out. I know I'm running out of time real quickly, but I want to also revisit the fact that you are still invited down to the University of North Georgia, one of the elite eight schools. We're looking forward to PTing with you. Appreciate you guys being here today. God bless you and God bless our military service members. With that, I yield. Sir, I got to say, this guy over my left shoulder is a University of North Georgia graduate. He didn't want me to tell you that, but he is. The President of the University, Shannon, would be very happy to hear that. Gentleman's time has expired. Chair now recognizes the gentleman from North Carolina, Mr. Davis. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Also thank you Mr. Secretary, Chairman Caine, and Mr. Hegseth. Shout out to Winterville. Thirteen U.S. service members killed, thirteen families forever changed. Thirteen heroes who put the uniform on and paid the ultimate sacrifice. We owe remembrance. To those families, I just want you to know we see you, we stand with you, and we'll never forget. There are Gold Star families who are here today sitting in the room, carrying the Honor and Remember flags. Remember the sacrifice, and when we do so, words can't fully capture it. April the 3rd, F-15E Strike Eagle pilots were rescued because we live by a simple promise: we do not leave anyone behind. The same spirit must be extended to those who are killed in action. That's why I arise again today and raise this question of the importance of passing H.R. 1363, the Honor and Remember Flag Recognition. Mr. Secretary, I raised this with you before, and actually, I come today to thank you for the response from your office and for actually engaging with us on this. I rise again today and bring this up because we must never lose sight of the true cost of war. As we commemorate 250 years in this country, the first call for independence, Halifax County delegates moved forward with the first official action in my district. Our service members are the reason we are here today. They've delivered freedom, and I believe it would be amazing if we could deliver for their families, those who've sacrificed, this flag—something to help them, ideally by Memorial Day, or at least to commemorate their families. I would simply ask if you have any thoughts whatsoever on how we continue to lift and honor those who have been killed serving our country. Well, thank you for what you do on that topic. I think first and foremost, we do it by doing right by the legacy of those that we lose, whether it's those 13 from this conflict or any other conflict. I've been at Dover every single time, as has the Chairman, as has the President and the Vice President when they can, to greet that and respect and honor that. In our department, we established the first-ever Gold Star Task Force, where we've got a Gold Star advocate right in our front office working these issues on a daily basis, which is why it doesn't surprise me that we so quickly got back to you. These are passions, these are deep in our DNA, things that we care about. How you treat a family who's given everything in that moment, not just at Dover but in every step of that process, is a reflection of how we value service. Mr. Secretary, thank you truly, just for time here. I've served in mortuary affairs and I've been to Dover—I deployed to Dover, so thank you there. I want to shift to Army Cadet Command, the senior ROTC program's rebalance optimization plan. North Carolina Central University in particular faces real access and logistical challenges before the cadets. When we think about building the next generation of leaders, I would just ask us to think about how we're putting barriers in front of some, and I would just ask you quickly if you could please take a closer look at the impact on the cadets. I want to shift to another topic and really spend my last moment here as I'm running short: your April memo regarding the non-official personal protection armament on Department of War property. My son is at Fort Benning right now in the infantry. He's training, and ideally, he wants to be an 11 Bravo. I shared the memo with him, and he shared it with his battle buddies to see the response. He's definitely a Second Amendment enthusiast and an advocate, and they're really interested in this. My question is simple: how do you plan to provide guidance on the denials, and how do you plan to track it? That was the intent: to make sure those men and women out there feel like they have their Second Amendment rights even when they're on post. And the burden of proof is on the command as to why they would deny, as opposed to the burden of proof on why they should allow. So the presumption is the same rights they would have outside the gate of a base, they have inside for personal protection purposes. Our bases shouldn't be gun-free zones except for the armory and the MPs. So the burden of proof is something we're going to pay very close attention to. In my last 15 seconds, Chairman Caine, thank you again for the conversation and breakfast. Do you have any final thoughts in terms of any reservations on how we're dealing with munitions in terms of the supplemental and the depletion of munitions? Gentleman's time has expired. Chair now recognizes the gentleman from Louisiana, Mr. Higgins. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. General Caine, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here today. Mr. Secretary, you stated in your opening statement that one of the foundational endeavors that you embrace is to, in your words, "forge a lethal arsenal of freedom." I don't think you could more accurately describe the spirit that you gentlemen are delivering within the Department of War for the men and women that you serve. It's notable for an American like me—an Army veteran, 65 years old. I don't believe in my lifetime I've witnessed this level of rebirth and hardcore focus that I'm witnessing out of the Pentagon right now. And I just commend you for driving that. Please note you have a great deal of support, not just in this body and on this committee, but across the country. Regular Americans that have served or have families that have served have taken note. General, you said in your opening statement that, if called upon, we win our wars around the world. Those are your words. And we're going to make certain through this committee that you have the support, the equipment, and the funding that you need to, if called upon, win wars across the world. But we expect a lot out of you gentlemen. We expect you to liberate and secure the entire Western Hemisphere. One of my colleagues across the aisle stated that in a negative way. Let me flip it to positive: we absolutely do expect you to liberate and secure the entire Western Hemisphere. We expect you to drive Iran to its knees and free the world of the Iranian terrorist threat. We expect you to leverage NATO into an indomitable force. We expect you to set China back on its heels worldwide. We expect you to drive peace and stability through the projection of military power. We expect you to secure the transit of affordable, transportable American energy product as the indisputable cornerstone of economic prosperity for every allied nation. That's a lot of expectations. And what you gentlemen have laid out today as you've answered questions is solid; you've made it very clear that that's the path we're on. And I support you. My Democratic colleagues have made it clear today that, after careful study and thorough research of world history, they've come to the conclusion that soldiers, sailors, airmen, and Marines are far safer if they're never deployed into conflict. You've made it very clear that you take your business seriously, and we support you. I certainly do. Mr. Secretary, in consultation with your staff, and reflective of your goals, your mission, and your stated intentions, in consultation with personnel and readiness, I proposed legislation to codify your guidance that combat fitness standards must be rigorous, with no separate scoring scales, adjusted standards, or accommodations, but there to be only a single, uncompromising standard. This is not to exclude any American from any opportunity, but to ensure we have the best, most capable Americans in the most critical positions. Would you welcome this body making your guidance into law in the NDAA? I would welcome that, Congressman. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, for clarifying that. I have a question about Iran, and I'll give you the final 30 seconds to answer. Was Iran moving precipitously towards nuclear weapons capability, Mr. Secretary? Even after the obliteration of Midnight Hammer, what was clear is that the intention of Iran remained in the pursuit of nuclear weapons. And after as hard as they were hit by Israel, it created an opportunity to get rid of the conventional shield. That's why we talked about missile production, the navy, and the defense industrial base to set them back from blackmailing the region and the world in pursuit of nuclear weapons, because we understood how much they could continue to pursue them. Roger that. Thank you, gentlemen. Mr. Chairman, I yield. Gentleman's time has expired. Chair now recognizes the gentleman from California, Mr. Cisneros. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Thank you and the Ranking Member for bringing us here today for this hearing, which is very important to the nation. I want to thank our witnesses for being here today. Mr. Secretary, the President recently made an announcement that, as activated, the National Guard would start receiving active duty pay for federal missions, and I believe you made similar remarks as well. I'm happy to see that announcement because it brought much-needed attention to this disparity. Whether you agree with the missions or not, when Guardsmen are called up for active duty—and they really have no choice in the matter for homeland missions—often they are receiving inconsistent pay and benefits depending on their status. This issue has persisted for decades. This is why earlier this year, Representative Bergman and I introduced the Duty Status Reform Act, which would address the issue head-on. I believe this is the right time to push this over the finish line. It's something the National Guard Bureau supports, the Council of Governors supports, and just the other week, the Service Personnel Chiefs were all here and they support it, and TAG supports it. So, Mr. Secretary, as a former member of the National Guard, I am asking you here today: would you consider supporting consolidating the 29 duty statuses for the military reserve component into a balanced and equitable system that would also help the National Guard receive retirement points when they are called up for these federal missions? Would you support that? Congressman, thank you for your efforts on this. It makes a ton of sense, and we support these efforts for duty status reform in pursuit of things that work for the department and for the troops, because they deserve the kind of pay and benefits that are commensurate with active duty troops. So we would look forward to working with your office to get at duty status reform. I think now is the right time. Thank you for that. General Caine, I ask you the same question as an individual who has served in the Air National Guard. Would you consider supporting duty status reform that's going to simplify this and help our National Guard troops? Sir, I'll be careful to stay in my lane. My personal—not the Chairman's—opinion, as somebody who has lived the house of pain of 30 different duty statuses, is that I would support it. As the Chairman, I'll leave personnel—and you know this from your prior life—to our civilian leaders who do that, but I do think we owe a better set of options for our Guardsmen and our reservists across the force. Well, thank you both for that. This is something that I think needs to get done, and it's something I've been working on for years now. The National Guard Bureau supports it, and so I hope you will, like you said, work with us to get that done. OMB seems to be our roadblock, so I'm hoping you can push that roadblock down so that we can finally get this done. Next question. Mr. Secretary, I take what we do for our personnel very seriously. Sometimes, I know you have stated right here today, our troops are ready and they will do anything if asked upon them, but I think as leaders sometimes we need to protect them as well. I worry about the USS Ford, which has now been out there over 10 months, and it's probably going to be well over 300 days by the time it gets back to the United States. I just saw a report today that it's going to be headed back here in a few days. You know, these people have gone pretty much all over the world, and they crossed the equator multiple times. But I am interested—I'm sure that was not in the GFMAP when it was first assigned and when they were deployed. And so, what were some of the concerns that the Navy had when you made this modification, when it was brought forward to you that, "Hey, this is what extending them out past 10 months is going to do; this is what it's going to do both on the troops and on the ship"? What concerns did the Navy bring up for this? It's a very fair question, and I can tell you every week I stare at an orders book where I'm looking at troop movements or asset movements, and multiple times that included the Ford. And multiple times the operational requirements, whether it was down in Southcom or up to Centcom, demanded additional assets in real time, which, through a tough decision-making process, led to an extension in consultation with the Navy. Admiral Caudle and his staff, they fairly represented the readiness trade-offs and maintenance trade-offs. Going forward, what trade-offs are we making right here? I mean, really, we have three carriers in the area right now. How many do we really need? It's a great show of force, but do we really need three carriers there, and you've got one ARG there and another one on the way? You know this is going to set back possibly deployments later on. It's going to have ramifications down the road, extending them for this much. And we're not only talking about logistics; you know we have service members that were kept away from their families for almost a year. How many missed the birth of a child before extending them another four months? We need to keep this in consideration when we make these modifications, and I hope these issues are being raised when you do these SDOB books, and that people are actually making these modifications and decisions in real time. Gentleman's time has expired. I yield back. Gentleman's time has expired. Chair now recognizes the gentleman from Virginia, Mr. McGuire. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Thank you to our witnesses today. Secretary Hegseth, I really appreciate your return to the warrior ethos and the priorities of our military. Merit over performance is how you win, and it's how you save lives. Your love of our military comes out in everything you say, so we really support you, your team, and what you're doing. General Caine, your comments about being in the middle and being apolitical also save lives. I think all of the fellow veterans in this room and military guys would agree that if somebody saved your life on the battlefield, you wouldn't care if they were male or female, Black or white, Democrat, Republican, Independent, whatever—we're all Americans, and we're all on the same team. When we put our differences aside and work as a team, we can turn the impossible into the possible. We can be outnumbered and still win. So I want to commend you guys for supporting our troops so they can best survive. Let's see. The border. Nobody talks about the border, Mr. Secretary, as you say, but it's incredible what you guys did in just a matter of weeks. With the missions in Ukraine, Honduras, I don't know of any other military in history that would have been able to successfully do something so complicated, but it talks to the unity and the teamwork of the joint force and how well they work together. I've got a lot of questions, so I'll try to go fast. In this 1.5-trillion-dollar budget, I want to echo what Representative Jackson said about US SOCOM. We are often "deploy, deploy, deploy, deploy," and we seem to be doing more with less, and we don't complain. They won't be that voice, so for us in Congress, we want to be that voice. Let's just take a look at the budget and see if we can help them with that. Also, I want to talk to you guys about solid rocket motors. I know, Secretary of War, that in this budget we've picked one particular company that we've put a lot of money into, and I think it's a great company and they do a great job. But what is your plan to also have other companies come to the table to help us with a shortfall of solid rocket motors? You're right on SOCOM, and we will absolutely work with you and look at that. On solid rocket motors, it's such a critical component. You've got to go with your fastest runner to get caught up as quickly as you can, giving them incentives. But we've also learned on multiple domains that just one option means zero options when you're stuck. You know: one is zero, two is one, one is none. So we need multiple avenues on that approach, and we look forward to finding other companies that can do it as well. Thank you. General Caine, regarding the National Guard, I'm thinking about counter-drone capabilities with FIFA coming up, Super Bowls, and all these things going on. Have you considered—and if you haven't, will you consider—training National Guardsmen, sending them to counter-drone school so they can come back and train law enforcement, so we can increase our ability to protect critical infrastructure? Sir, for sure they may already be doing that. Please let me take that one and come back to you. I know that the Title 32 force, in advance of all the domestic things on the horizon here with America's 250, is looking at a range of capability. So if it's okay, we'll get back to you on that one. Thank you. On Ukraine—whoever can answer this—you know they have 60% enriched uranium, and they say they have enough to build 11 bombs. Even at 60%, is that enough to make a dirty bomb? Is that a yes or no? Does anybody have an answer for that? I wouldn't want to state anything that might be classified, so I wouldn't talk about that in this setting. Fair. And by the way, in World War II, a congressman was in a closed briefing and learned that the reason the Japanese hadn't sunk any U.S. submarines was because they didn't know our submarines could go below 250 feet. So he went out to the media and told them that, and we started losing submarines and losing American sailors. So, a lesson for all of us in Congress to be careful. Thank you for considering that. I would say this: if they had a nuclear bomb, would that kill hundreds of people potentially, thousands of people potentially, or millions of people potentially? History shows us what the lethal potential is of a nuclear weapon, and so you'd have to assume the higher end of that range. And given the radical nature of this regime and the likelihood of them using it, it gives you a sense of the threat. Well, I think the threat is too great to wait, and I have to commend this President, President Trump, for having the fortitude, and the Secretary of War and the joint force for making sure we stop this threat for the world. Also, I'm a big supporter of the AH-64 Apache. Under operations like Absolute Resolve and everything else, it's been very effective in countering drones, so I'm glad that in the NDAA we're keeping that program going. I also just saw that you're going to keep the A-10 program going, and a good friend of mine said, "Long live the Warthog." But are there ways that we could bring some of the Warthogs back, or are there any other capabilities that can replace what the Warthog does? I don't think so. Is there anything that can do what the Warthog does? Gentleman's time has expired. Chair now recognizes the gentleman from Illinois, Mr. Sorensen. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. To General Caine, on behalf of myself and my constituents, we thank you for your service and your leadership to our military men and women. To our service members and the veterans that I see in our committee today, thank you for your support of our great country. Mr. Hegseth, I hear from your officials in our briefings here on the Hill, and because I live in an Army town, I now hear from neighbors at the grocery store who are worried about our munitions stockpiles, which are now down to critical levels. They sure don't want another forever war. So, Mr. Secretary, first of all, talk to us about the need to renew munitions stockpiles, and tell us what happens when munitions stockpiles become depleted? Well, first of all, I would note that as you know, we give weekly briefings on munitions to this committee, so it's not something that's withheld from any of the members here. But it's also a topic that isn't something that should be publicly discussed, as far as any shortfalls that may exist. I would assure you that there are no shortfalls that exist in our ability to execute the missions around the world. The reason we're investing in the defense industrial base with $1.5 trillion dollars is to make sure that the greatest companies in the world, which have been falling behind for decades, are running faster than ever before to give us 2x, 3x, or 4x of the critical munitions—your Patriots, your THAADs, your SM-3s, and every other standard missile. My neighbors work really hard to make sure that we can achieve this. It's jobs in America, it's what our warfighters need, and we're focused on it. So could you tell me why we're firing the people at Joint Munitions Command for no cause other than just telling them that they're overhired or that there's a surplus of employees? At Joint Munitions Command? I'd have to look into the particular circumstances of that. But there have been places where we haven't been moving fast enough with munitions, and when people don't move fast enough in the Department of War, we find someone else who will run fast. I mean, this is Joint Munitions Command. Your department says that you're prioritizing munitions, yet you cut 58% of Joint Munitions Command at Rock Island. But let me move on. Munitions are not built by commands; munitions are built by businesses. No, but they're the ones that are the point of it. We are very good at building commands and bureaucracies, and slapping three- and four-star generals in charge of things that don't move things any faster. So, our job is to unleash the private sector to provide these things. But how are we going to get it done if we don't have the Joint Munitions Command? Let me move on. We also have the Army Sustainment Command in my district. Could you tell me why we're firing folks at the Army Sustainment Command when now is the time that we should be giving our soldiers every piece of sustainment that we can give them? Because, as I've stated, the status quo at this department before the Trump administration was unacceptable, and changes had to be made. Do you think it's acceptable to fire veterans from jobs where they've been—you know, I talked to my neighbors at the arsenal, and they've worked there 20, 30 years on top of their service to this country, and they're doing exemplary work? Great performers will always have a job at the Department of War. When you reestablish merit, sometimes things happen, but we're grateful for all the vets that serve. In fact, we've created a Patriot Pipeline for service members to get into the defense industrial base manufacturing business to consider that as a second career. So, we're leaning into vets and service members as the workforce of the future in our department. And this isn't just me. I mean, they tell me that they feel you're taking a wrecking ball to the civilian workforce. But, you know, I wanted to follow up on Congressman Norcross's questions about why you terminated collective bargaining earlier this month. This affects hundreds of thousands of hardworking DOD employees, so many of whom are veterans who have honorably served our country. You testified that if you work hard and you do the work, you keep your job. But that's not what's happening, and you allege it is being done to maximize efficiency. Explain that. I should, yeah, thank you for asking that question. I'd love to clarify. I fundamentally believe the Department of War should not be subject to collective bargaining, full stop. So people shouldn't be able to fight for good wages and being treated well? I think our department does a great job evaluating and providing pay and benefits for folks across the board. I would love to invite you to the Rock Island Arsenal so you can look these patriots in the eye and tell them that. How does creating chaos for long-term civilian workers actually maximize efficiency? Do you have any comment on that? There are plenty of examples of shipyards and other manufacturing plants where there are restraints that limit the hours, and limit the ability of leaders to ensure we're meeting the goals we're supposed to. I don't think we should live under those constraints. Blah, blah, blah. Okay, okay. Well, you know what? I want to use the rest of my time to address the men and women of our armed forces. Please go forth with the integrity and the morality that you've carried before this administration took us down a path that has allies turning away and American citizens objecting. Do what's right, even if you're asked to do otherwise. Mr. Chairman, I yield back. Gentleman yields back. Chair now recognizes the gentleman from Florida, Mr. Mills. Thank you so much, Mr. Chairman. Mr. Secretary, General Caine, and the Honorable, we'd love to just thank you for this opportunity for coming here and for continuing to push for the warfighter ethos, and for making sure that the realities, regardless of what's said in here, are the realities on the battlefield that we're getting day in and day out. I ask the Chairman for unanimous consent to enter a couple of documents into the record. Without objection, so ordered. I'll be entering into the record my DD-214, published in 2003, indicating my Bronze Star and other wartime medals and decorations. I'd also like to present the document provided by the Department of the Army in 2024 that also authorizes and verifies all medals and awards, as well as a document by Paul Savitzky, a former Special Forces member, that annotates the two explosions that I was involved in in Baghdad. Without objection, so ordered. Mr. Secretary, as you know, you're pretty familiar with the ideas of slander, defamation, and attacks on character. So I apologize for having to take that moment to be able to go ahead and correct the record, but it was necessary. I would also note that my OMPF file, which was FOIA'd by the Department of the Army—or rather, was FOIA'd—was actually released by the Department of the Army the same day as yours was. So, everything is sitting on the actual website itself where people aren't too lazy to look at it. Secretary Hegseth, thank you for your testimony and for the time that you have here today. As the Chairman noted, President Trump's incredible $1.5-trillion-dollar investment into our national defense is set to reverse the years of atrophy that our defense industrial base has faced over time, especially for small, innovative businesses in need of a solid demand signal that our War Department is ready to buy American. I witnessed this myself as I was with you in Merritt Island, Florida, as we went on the arsenal tour. Unfortunately, I didn't get to do the cool things like fly in a jet really quick and buzz around while we were actually doing hard work here. But I wanted to ask: Mr. Secretary, can you detail for us how the Department of War has eased the path for American small businesses to contract with the department? Well, Congressman, thank you for the question. That has been core to our mission: the recognition that we have a problem at the department—previously the Department of Defense and now the Department of War—and that problem is us. That problem is our bureaucracy, our process that is meant to block out new competitors and protect existing primes. There are great primes that provide great products, and we continue to support them. But to build an arsenal of freedom, you need more than just five, more than just six. You need an entire network of small, medium, and large companies contributing to the U.S. manufacturing and supply base, delivering weapons ahead of schedule and under budget, that don't only have defense business but also commercial business. We need something that can move quickly and adapt accordingly because there are multiple options. So, our Acquisitions Transformation speech, memo, and directive we rolled out on that, and the Arsenal of Freedom tour—going out there with the hard hats and saying, "You are part of the workforce, you have the backs of our warriors"—are just changing the entire dynamic to say you shouldn't have to sue the department in order to get a contract with the department. We're opening the doors for business with small business loans, whether it's the SBIR/STTR or DIU or OSC. The Office of Strategic Capital hasn't even been discussed today—twenty billion dollars that gets leveraged into two hundred billion dollars so that companies can invest in ways they otherwise wouldn't have the capital to do, and it's not on our balance sheet. So we've got businessmen, like the Deputy Secretary of War, who should be deemed a national asset for what he understands and how he works. We're opening the aperture of our department in historic ways, and I think—I know—our manufacturing base is going to see the benefit from it. Well, thank you so much. I also want to thank you for your due diligence in making sure that Task Force Audit will come forward and we will see the Department of War for the first time in 2028 with a clean audit. That is something that the previous administration was never able to do, nor many before that. I believe it has been 28 years, if I'm not mistaken, Mr. Secretary, since we've had a clean audit. That is right, and this is not a thing we just announced. We've been doing it for 15 months. Jay has been integrally involved, and we just launched a joint task force audit. We are going to pass an audit as a department by 2028 because of the taxpayer. And this is why we're so honored to have you as our Secretary of War and General Caine. We're honored to have you as well leading our forces. Mr. Secretary, with the last few seconds I have, from ships to bombs to bullets, our industrial base is in dire need of support, even as these defense markers are strengthened. As a former small business owner, I understand how bottlenecks can hold up good contracts and keep them from moving at the speed which we need for our warfighters. Mr. Secretary, can you detail how the department has moved to promote diversity in our defense industrial base? Can you detail the offset? Well, if you will, can we submit that for the record? Every aspect is opening it up to provide as much running room for new entrants as possible. Thank you so much, Mr. Chairman, I appreciate it, and with that, I yield back. Gentleman's time has expired. Chair now recognizes the gentleman from New Mexico, Mr. Vasquez. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you to our witnesses for being here today. You're here before us requesting $1.5 trillion for the Defense Department, the largest one-year increase since World War II. While I recognize that your budget does include increases for pay and quality of life, these increases are outweighed by the rising cost of living and the department's ongoing failures on childcare, education, medical care, and other quality-of-life factors. Just like other families, the soaring costs of gas, groceries, housing, and childcare have left military families underwater. Yet the department continues to put other priorities—like a $25 billion war of choice in Iran, and just lately, photo ops with Kid Rock—ahead of improving the lives of our troops and their families. It's deeply troubling to me that the department received a historic $1 trillion last year, including $9 billion under H.R. 1 for improving troops' quality of life, but the New Mexican service members that I have talked to are still living in barracks that are at capacity, sending their kids to rundown schools, and lacking access to on-base childcare. Here's an example: during my most recent visit to White Sands Missile Range, I saw severely aging on-base infrastructure, specifically an elementary school that is in need of a complete rebuild. Secretary Hegseth, last fall you said that quality of life for our warriors is a critical component of reviving the warrior ethos. In a video you posted to social media last November, you said that the places where our warfighters reside need to be safe, comfortable, and clean. In your opening statement today, you said quality of life is front and center in this budget. But when it comes to our troops' quality of life, I fear that this is mostly talk, because I can tell you the outdated barracks that I saw at White Sands Missile Range were anything but safe, comfortable, and clean, and I haven't heard from service members about any improvements since you've assumed this role. As Secretary of Defense, do you take any accountability for the continued quality-of-life concerns that New Mexico's military service members and families face today under your leadership? Well, I appreciate your attention to the matter of quality of life, which is incredibly important to us and is reflected in this budget. I mean, there are historic amounts for barracks, for family housing, and for family support like child development and youth programs. This budget surpasses anything you've seen from this department in quite some time, including replacing or eliminating all poor and failing barracks. We've seen those photos. We responded to them with a barracks task force with a billion dollars initially, and now we have billions of dollars behind it to make sure they're safe, clean, and quality, but also that where they need to be replaced, they're rebuilt and replaced. The President has charged me with doing that, and we are focused on doing that. Well, thank you, Secretary. And I hope that the next photos you receive, and the next photos and reports that I receive, are of improved conditions, new barracks, and quality-of-life improvements, because this has gone on for far too long. Let me give you another example. At Holloman Air Force Base, the child development centers on base have faced critical staffing issues for many years and just recently gave military families, our airmen and women, just nine days to find new childcare providers. One parent said she felt she had no choice but to resign from her job as a physician assistant when her son lost his spot in on-base care. And one patient describing his experience seeking healthcare at Cannon Air Force Base said, "Good luck if you need specialized care. Referrals take six months or more, and regular appointments are booking a month out." And so, spending a billion dollars a day on a war overseas while leaving our service members out to dry is not America First—it's a betrayal. Secretary Hegseth, I voted for $1 trillion for the defense budget in the committee last year and I've yet to see the results come home. Service members back home have said nothing about progress or improvements underway. Within 30 days, Secretary, would you commit to sending me a detailed list of every dollar that is going to improve the quality of life at bases in my district? We will get you whatever we can, but that doesn't align with what we've seen, which is running as fast as this department ever has on quality-of-life issues. I've been in their shoes, I've seen those barracks, and I've seen them recently. It may not have happened under this administration, but this administration is going to fix it. Ultimately, this budget reflects that kind of priority across the board. Well, thank you, Secretary. I will just suggest that it needs to run faster; it needs to run at the speed of light. It needs to cross time travel—I don't care what it takes—but it has gone on for far too long. I appreciate your attention to this issue, but we have got to get it done, and we cannot be here again next year discussing the improvements that haven't been done despite what's in the budget today. Let me be clear that these issues have persisted for years under both administrations, on both sides of the aisle. The department has received billions of taxpayer dollars as this committee has continued to work in a bipartisan fashion to support our troops and their families. At a time when the cost of living is the top issue for families, including those who serve, the department is in the position to address these costs of housing, childcare, groceries, and other needs. I hope the department chooses to do that. Thank you, Chairman, I yield back. Gentleman's time's expired. Chair now recognizes the gentleman from Texas, Mr. Gooden. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you, Secretary, for being here and for the work you're doing. Over the past decade, Bitcoin has evolved from a fringe asset into a matter of national security. Iran has demanded Bitcoin as a toll for transit through the Strait of Hormuz. North Korean cyber actors have leveraged it in ransomware campaigns, and China is believed to be stockpiling substantial holdings as part of a strategic reserve. Just last week, INDOPACOM Commander Admiral Paparo stated that Bitcoin has direct implications for power projection, and he noted that INDOPACOM is operating a node on the Bitcoin network in furtherance of that mission. Secretary, do you share the opinion that Bitcoin is a tool to project power, and are there any department-wide initiatives to ensure the U.S. secures a strategic advantage in Bitcoin and combats China's digital authoritarianism? I guess my short answer would be yes and yes—I'm a long-time enthusiast of Bitcoin and crypto's potential. A lot of the things we're doing, whether enabling it or defeating it, are classified efforts that are ongoing inside our department, which provide us a lot of leverage in a lot of different scenarios. I appreciate that, and I share your views. I thank you for that. I also wanted an update on our operations in Qatar. How are those going since the Iran war, and how has Qatar been as a partner to work with over these last few months? I think it's fair to say that the Gulf states, to include Qatar, have done a nice job integrating on air defenses specifically, and then providing access, basing, and overflight. Qatar being one of them, the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Bahrain—they've lived the threat of Iran. There are some complicated aspects with Qatar, but ultimately, they've provided the support that we need in the theater. I appreciate that. I also appreciate the job that our men and women are doing there at that base; I realize they're going through a lot. We really appreciate the work you do, and I'll yield back to you, Mr. Chairman. Chair now recognizes the gentlelady from New Hampshire, Ms. Goodlander. I want to thank our witnesses for being here today, and I want to begin by getting right to it since my time is limited on this $1.5-trillion-dollar budget request. Mr. Hegseth, you testified last year, and you testified again today, that you will be accountable for every American taxpayer dollar that comes to the department, where it goes, and why. That's really important since, as it has been pointed out, the department has failed eight audits. The department remains on GAO's high-risk list. The Inspector General, just last year alone, identified billions of dollars that could have been put to better use and questioned costs—that is, spending that raised red flags. So this is a crisis right now in accountability of where the dollars of the American taxpayers, who are in the middle of a cost crisis, where this money is going. I want to ask about your war in Iran. Mr. Hurst, you testified earlier today that $25 billion has been spent to date. I just want to clarify: that is money, taxpayer dollars, that have been spent already in this war? $25 billion reflects the cost of the war, right? So we've already spent the dollars on munitions and things like that? Yes, we're factoring in costs of munitions expended in that total. So how much of that has gone towards munitions? Does that include the munitions that have been spent to date? Yeah, that reflects the munitions that have been spent to date and other operational costs. Can you give us a breakdown of what those costs are? We'll work to get you guys a product that has a detailed breakdown of those costs. Yeah, my concern here is, Mr... Hegseth, you've testified repeatedly that you're going to be accountable for each and every American taxpayer dollar that you spend, and that you're going to be accountable for the rationale. Here we are 60 days into your war of choice in Iran, and you can't give us an answer on a basic breakdown of American taxpayer dollars that have been spent in your war of choice. "Yeah, that's a question for Mr. Hurst." "You, right as you sit here now, you cannot—" "We're happy to give you a breakdown. We'll provide you a product after this hearing." "Well, I would love for you to give us this breakdown now and explain to us: was this unbudgeted costs, or was this something the department planned for in advance, since you were appropriated $1 trillion dollars last year?" "The department deals with contingency operations all the time within its normal budget." "So this was not budgeted for. My point here is, you know—and General Caine, you've testified to this—we all understand that for you to do your jobs, you need Congress to pass budgets. For us to do our jobs, we need you to give us the details and to be accountable to American taxpayers. And so, I gotta say, it's an extraordinary dereliction that as you sit here, you cannot account for billions of dollars that have already been spent in this war." "You're briefed on munitions every week. You and your entire staff have every—" "I'm asking for the American public, no. It's not as if you don't have munitions levels. You do from us every single week, so you know exactly the numbers you're talking about." "Mr. Hegseth, you've testified that American taxpayers need to know where their dollars are going, and as you sit here today, you can't provide us with a basic answer to the most simple question that you should be able to answer to each and every American taxpayer." "You're asking a question to an answer you already have." "No, I'm asking you for a breakdown. Where you said you will be accountable for every taxpayer dollar, you cannot tell us where any of the $25 billion dollars in taxpayer funds that have gone to fund your war of choice in Iran has gone. And I look forward to getting details from our witnesses after this hearing because the American taxpayers need to know." "Mr. Hegseth, can I ask you, do you know what the average cost of a gallon of gas was in this country on February 28th?" "Well, it depends on where you live. If you live in California, it's eight bucks." "It was $2.83. Do you know what the average cost of a gallon of gas is today?" "Much higher in California." "It is $4.23. I gotta say, Mr. Hegseth, you said you've got a crack economic team that's looking at the impacts of this war on the American taxpayer, and you can't answer this basic question. That should shock the conscience of every American." "General Caine, I want to come back to something—a really important principle, and my time is limited. You testified to an important and bedrock principle of American law that our service members follow lawful orders, and lawful orders only. Is that right? Do you agree with the statement, quote, 'The military won't follow unlawful orders'?" "I do." "Mr. Hegseth, do you agree with that statement?" "I do, but I don't understand what you're insinuating at a partisan point." "I'm, I'm not. I'm actually quoting you directly, Mr. Hegseth, from April 12th, 2016, and I appreciate that on the record you've clarified this important principle of American law." The gentlewoman's time has expired. The Chair now recognizes the gentleman from North Carolina, Mr. Harrigan. "Well, thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you, Mr. Secretary and Chairman Caine. It's always a pleasure to have you here. I'm going to follow in line with the chairman's earlier statement that we can do whatever we want with our five minutes, and I'm going to give you all a break from the conflict here and tell you a story." "And it's a story that's important to me. I'm going to start with a proposed citation. It's a rare point of personal privilege for me to do this, but I have an ask of you. I'm going to start with a proposed citation for a Medal of Honor award upgrade from a Silver Star." "Sergeant First Class Thomas J. Grasso distinguished himself by acts of gallantry and intrepidity above and beyond the call of duty between September 29th, 2015, and October 4th, 2015, while serving as a senior communications sergeant, Operational Detachment Alpha 3133, Advanced Operations Base North, Special Operations Task Force Afghanistan in support of Operation Resolute Support." "Sergeant First Class Grasso was part of a small special forces element that seized Kunduz, a provincial capital of over 300,000 civilians, from the Taliban. Outnumbered 60 to 1, the US special forces element faced an overwhelming enemy force of 2,000 confirmed Taliban fighters." "On September 29th, facing a Taliban advance on Kunduz airfield, Sergeant First Class Grasso established a sniper overwatch position in the air traffic control tower. With enemy fire converging from three directions, Sergeant First Class Grasso risked his life to engage a hostile anti-aircraft team, successfully clearing the way for air support." "On September 30th, fighting through relentless ambushes on their subsequent movement into the city, Sergeant First Class Grasso repeatedly dismounted his vehicle while exposed to intense enemy fire to engage insurgents and shield the convoy." "During one encounter while under withering enemy fire, he disregarded his own safety and maneuvered into the kill zone of a complex near ambush, saving the lives of five trapped special forces teammates, one Romanian partner, and their interpreter." "Sergeant First Class Grasso again came under heavy machine gun fire when he dismounted to direct the element to the city compound. To enable the remaining element to safely enter the compound, he effectively returned fire and suppressed the enemy positions." "From October 1st through 4th, Sergeant First Class Grasso anchored the defense of the compound, absorbing the heaviest concentration of enemy fire. He repeatedly subjected himself to heavy machine gun and rocket-propelled grenade fire from distances as close as 15 meters, repelling dozens of enemy attacks." "On October 1st, a rocket-propelled grenade directly hit his position, inflicting a traumatic brain injury. Bleeding, disoriented, and going 60 hours without sleep, he checked on his teammates before returning to his exposed post. From there, he single-handedly neutralized continuous waves of attackers attempting to detonate vehicle-born IEDs, forcing the enemy to request a temporary ceasefire." "During a subsequent rocket-propelled grenade attack near his position, he eliminated a follow-on explosive-filled rickshaw, preventing a perimeter breach. As the fight wore on, the enemy began throwing hand grenades into the compound from an adjacent building." "Sergeant First Class Grasso ran through the blasts to hurl grenades back, then established a clear line of sight from an exposed staircase to enable his command to call in a danger-close F-16 strafing run, with bullets hitting 15 meters from his position." "When partner forces were decisively engaged without sufficient ammunition outside the perimeter, he maneuvered without regard for his own safety under devastating fire to resupply their position and employed accurate sniper fire, destroying another enemy advance." "On October 4th, he unhesitatingly stepped into the enemy's line of sight to destroy the fighter preparing to fire a rocket-propelled grenade at two trapped teammates. Throughout 130 hours of grueling, sleepless combat, Sergeant First Class Grasso's extraordinary heroism, unwavering dedication to his teammates, and complete disregard for his own life ensured the recapture of Kunduz with zero friendly personnel killed in action." "As these severe conditions took a toll on the element, his ability to fight through a traumatic brain injury with indomitable will and lethal precision inspired those around him. Sergeant First Class Thomas J. Grasso's heroism and selflessness are in keeping with the highest traditions of military service and reflect great credit upon himself, his unit, and the United States Army." "I'm asking you to direct the board chair of the HRC Awards and Decorations Branch to board Sergeant First Class Thomas J. Grasso's packet. I know these things to be true inside of this packet because I was there. And I'm here today only because I was one of those five that he rescued out of that near complex ambush." "Thank you for your service to our nation." "Thank you. Congressman, we'll do it, and I'd love to meet the Sergeant First Class." "Thank you." "I thank the gentleman; the gentleman yields. The Chair now recognizes the gentlelady from Maryland, Ms. Elfreth." "Thank you, Mr. Chair. I want to start by thanking Secretary Isom and General Caine for inviting a number of us to the Pentagon last week to talk about the 1.8 million enlisted service members we have, and the challenges and opportunities that we can work on together." "Every base that I go to internationally, and every single major military base in Maryland over the last year, I insist on meeting with service members and hearing, hearing what we all can do better, Mr. Secretary. I want to assure you, I insist on those meetings." "There's a whole lot we could talk about today: the war in Iran, and I too want to thank the families of the 14 service members we have lost in this war so far, both from strikes in the Caribbean and the East Pacific, and the firing of a number of people we all respect on this dais on both sides, 61 days into this war." "But I want to start with those quality of life issues, Mr. Secretary. A number of members of this committee met with survivors of sexual assault at Fort Hood, including enlisted members and family members. You sat here last year and you said, quote, 'I applaud Congress for the efforts that have been ongoing to address sexual assaults, which we do need to drive down to zero, and that funding has not been reduced.'" "I appreciate that, but the budget we have before us is level funded from last year, which you would agree is a realized cut year-over-year. So I want to give you the opportunity to talk about how you're still going to drive those sexual assault numbers down to zero via flat funding." "I'm told we've, we've added 5%, so it's actually not flat, but we remain committed to that. I think you've seen throughout the force reductions that it's not enough—we want more, we want to go to zero—but we remain committed to that." "We're going to hold you accountable for that. Thank you." "I want to move on to Ukraine. I don't normally agree with Mitch McConnell, but as you know, the department is withholding approximately $400 million in appropriated security assistance to Ukraine." "Despite some public belittling, Ukraine has stepped up perhaps more than any other ally for us in the Middle East. They're harnessing the superiority that they have generated from a hard-fought war with Russia, and they are intercepting Iranian drones that threaten US and partner forces in the Middle East." "But Mr. Secretary, I'm very concerned about that $400 million. Can you please let this committee know, the folks watching at home, and the Ukrainian people, when they can expect that $400 million in Ukraine support, passed by Congress bipartisanly last year, to be released and put on contract for its congressionally intended purpose: the defense of Ukraine?" "The department recognizes that $400 million was, was allocated for European capacity building, and as of yesterday, it has been released." "Released and under contract, released as of yesterday? How would you describe it, Jay?" "Not under contract, but released to be put under contract." "Let me, let me ask you a different way, Mr. Hurst. When will it actually be on the ground in Ukraine?" "That depends on what they buy with the money, and we're going to take the advice of the EUCOM Commander as we employ those funds for best use." "Okay, we're going to, we're going to keep on that because it's incredibly important, as we heard from everybody, including the King of England yesterday. I'm going to stay on our allies here." "It's clear that the President didn't account for the Strait of Hormuz being closed, global oil market disruption, and gas prices skyrocketing. Mr. Secretary, gas in my district in Maryland, not California, is $4.11." "The President did not notify allies abroad or Congress before he went to war. He has repeatedly berated and belittled our NATO allies, and I'm not interested in Twitter diplomacy; I'm interested in our actual exit strategy from this war. So I'm actually going to pose it to General Caine, if I could." "Well, the President did understand the risks and had the courage to undertake the endeavor nonetheless, which the American people voted for—that kind of courage and taking on that Iranian nuclear threat." "General Caine, have you, or what have you formally and specifically requested of our NATO allies and European partners for operations in the Strait? Can you tell me? Escort duties, air defense, peace enforcement? Specific requests of our NATO allies?" "Both us and State have reached out through a variety of mechanisms to pulse our international partners, not just in Europe but in the Pacific as well, to see what capabilities could be brought to bear down there. As this is largely a... we don't move much trade through there, I'll defer to Energy, Commerce, and Treasury." "But we've been specific with our allies about what we need?" "We have." "Okay, can I ask you for specificity on another issue that's been brought up today from a military perspective, not a policy perspective? What will it require of our service members to actually eradicate any Iranian nuclear threat? Can you help us understand that?" "There's such a wide range, ma'am, of options in there, all of which are classified. It would be out of my swim lane, but we always deliver the full range of options, the associated risks, and the advice to go do the things that we would be asked to do. There are many." "I can't speak for everybody on this committee, but I know we would like—" "The gentlewoman's time has expired. The Chair now recognizes Mr. Messmer from Indiana for five minutes." "Thank you, Chairman, and thank you, Secretary Hegseth, General Caine, and Comptroller Hurst for being here today. Congratulations to all of you for rolling out the $1.5 trillion dollar defense budget." "I'd like to offer special thanks to Mr. Hurst and his comptroller team for the work that it takes to put, put this budget together. It's a monumental task, and important for our nation and our well-being." "Secretary Hegseth, as you know, the theme of this year's NDAA is expanding the defense industrial base, and one of the key bottlenecks to that expansion is our workforce size. We're not producing enough skilled young workers, and too many regions with strong manufacturing potential remain underutilized." "Would you support Congress working across committees on a national defense strategy workforce, a workforce strategy that promotes technical training, apprenticeships, and proven school-to-industry pipelines?" "Absolutely. I think you'll find our budget fully aligned with the theme of the NDAA. And when it comes to skilled workers, whether it's DODEA schools, WIOA schools, whether it's the Patriot Pipeline or any form of trades, we're investing in that because we need those folks invested in our industrial base." "Excellent. I'd look forward to working with you, working with my team, to ensure those efforts align with the Department of Defense industrial base workforce priorities. So, our office will be reaching out." "Also, Secretary Hegseth, we're in a shipbuilding crisis as the department implements President Trump's vision for a larger fleet. Should the Navy leverage a model of distributed modular shipbuilding that taps industrial strength outside of traditional shipyards—such as in the Midwest, in the Great Lakes, and the Ohio and Mississippi Valley regions—to help expand capacity, ease pressure on overburdened shipyards, and cut down on maintenance backlogs?" "In short, yes. And that includes international options where we can work with partners who have specific capabilities that would speed up the timeline on shipyards that are, that are behind in our own country. But you'll find $65 billion dollars in this budget on shipbuilding, a lot of which builds on those types of capabilities." "Perfect. Thank you. Would you also agree that platforms like unmanned surface vessels, tenders, and other support ships seem well-suited for production outside traditional shipyards?" "Absolutely. And in fact, some of those facilities were precisely the ones we visited on the Arsenal of Freedom tour. USVs, UUVs—where you don't need the same capabilities you would in a traditional shipyard, or you can invest in a new type of shipyard specifically tailored to those more bespoke capabilities that can be scaled for the fight of the future, for sure." "Perfect. Thank you. Secretary Hegseth and General Caine, many from the department have reiterated support for the nuclear sea-launched cruise missile, and I believe President Trump needs more support options in the form of expanded theater nuclear capability." "From your perspective, what specific deterrence problem does SLCM-N solve that current capabilities do not, and what risk do we assume if, if that capability is not fielded?" "I'll take it first. SLCM-N provides... it's part of the multiple layers of nuclear deterrent that any President has to have at their disposal that creates dilemmas for enemies who are either nuclear-capable or not, and ensures the kind of leverage required, defense required, and deterrence required that sort of underwrites everything we do. So more options on that that the adversary can't account for is only good for us." "The only thing I'll add is, as we mentioned a little bit earlier, sir, we continue to modernize the, the full range of the triad. This is another tool in that regional portfolio, so it's a, it's a something I support." "Thank you. As we look beyond the current program, should the department be thinking about a future sea-launched nuclear system with greater survivability, maneuverability, and potential hypersonic capability?" "Yes, and I know we are." "Okay. Thank you." "Secretary Hegseth and General Caine, one of the major bottlenecks in expanding the defense industrial base is test range access. Companies are waiting too long for range time, and that slows fielding at the speed modern conflict demands." "What is the department doing to expand and modernize the testing architecture?" "There is $5.2 billion dollars worth of investment. But I'll tell you, when we sit with industry and I simply ask the question, 'What can we do for you? How do we empower you?', test ranges come up almost every single time, and the timeline limits they create. So we're trying to blow that open and create a lot more options for them." "Okay. Perfect. Thank you." "Secretary Hegseth and General Caine, I'd like to extend an invitation for both of you to come and visit Naval Surface Warfare Center Crane and see the great, great work being done in Southwest Indiana to support the warfighter and ensure our military edge. And with that, I'll close and surrender the rest of my time." The Chair now recognizes Mr. Whiteside from California for five minutes. "Thank you, Madam Chairwoman. Secretary Hegseth, General Caine, Mr. Hurst, thank you for testifying today and for your service to the nation." "Over the past few weeks, military and civilian leaders of many of the DOD components have testified about their FY '27 posture. We appreciate their professionalism and their service to the country, and the time that they've spent to testify before us." "I want to start with Mr. Hurst. Mr. Hurst, you testified at the beginning of this that you said that the estimated cost of the war is about $25 billion dollars. In March, you stated that the first week of the war cost $11 billion dollars." "So is it right to say that the following seven weeks have cost about $14 billion dollars total, or $2 billion per week?" "Yeah, the most intense phase of the conflict was obviously at the beginning. That's when most munitions were used, and that's the basic expense from the war." "Right." "And you also said just now that the $25 billion dollars includes the cost of the munitions deployed. Is that right?" "This question is for the CFO, sir. Yeah. That's correct." "Okay. So I guess I'm confused then. It makes sense that if that's the cost, then the cost to refill those stocks would be no more than $25 billion dollars. Am I right about that?" "When we estimate costs of the conflict, we're using current replacement costs for these weapon systems." "Right, you're using current replacement costs. So it's frankly pretty surprising that the estimate for the supplemental would be $200 billion dollars. You don't have... would you like to explain that if you can?" "That is not, that is not accurate. We don't have an estimate for the cost of a supplemental." "Okay. That's what's publicly reported. So it's good to hear that it's going to be less." "Mr. Hurst, do you have signal on your phone?" "I'm sorry, why is this relevant?" "Do you have signal on your phone? What does this have to do with the budget?" "I'm going to take that as a yes." "General Caine, do you have signal on your phone?" "I do, sir, yes." "Okay, so I want to talk about Signal. Last year, the Secretary and other administration officials discussed sensitive and almost certainly classified information about a strike in Yemen using the unclassified Signal app." "Mr. Hurst, what's the current policy for Signal on official DOD devices?" "I'm the comptroller. I don't do CIO work." "So therefore it is allowed? Is that your statement, that it is now allowed to have Signal on your device?" "Mr. Whiteside, my statement is I run the budget for DOD." "Yeah, okay, well he's got it on his phone, so therefore it must be permitted." "I, I got to go back and look if it's on my official phone. I was talking about my personal phone. I'm not exactly sure, but I'll go back and look, though." "Okay." "Last month, FBI Director Cash Patel made a public announcement that Russian hackers are targeting Signal app users in the government. He specifically stated that they were targeting individuals of high intelligence value, including current and former US government officials." "I hope that it is not official policy that you can have Signal on your official phones, and I would love to get a response from the department on that. We have now asked this question about five separate times and have never gotten a straight answer." "I want to close by talking about helicopters. Mr. Secretary, I want to ask about the Army Apache helicopter that flew over Kid Rock's property on March 28th." "An investigation was launched by the 101st Combat Aviation Brigade command leadership, but before that review was completed, on March 31st, you terminated it via a social media post. Did you personally direct the termination of that review?" "I did." "Okay, now the President said they probably shouldn't have been doing it and that I'd take a look at it. So did you talk to the President before terminating that review?" "Well, I don't relay what I talk about with the President." "Okay, I think unfortunately that means that you did talk to him, which in turn means that you've just confirmed that the President himself overrode the leadership of the 101st Airborne." "Mr. Secretary, you yourself have affirmed repeatedly the need for higher standards and higher accountability at every level of your agency. Commanders rely on established processes to uphold these standards. How does canceling a command-initiated review by the 101st Airborne leadership support a culture of accountability?" "Actually, I think I'll ask it of the chairman." "Sir, back to my earlier comments about the importance in my role of staying in the middle... what you're alluding to is, in my assessment with deep respect for your ask, a partisan question, and I think it would be inappropriate for me to answer that." "Sir, I actually don't think it is. I think it is actually a fundamental principle of accountability in our department, and, and I think it was the wrong decision." "I'm going to close by just saying one thing. Mr. Secretary, you started this hearing by politicizing it, and I want to make one point. Poll after poll shows that the American public does not support this war, and support is dropping every day." "We are all representing our constituents, which is our constitutional duty, and to state that we are politicizing it is an affront to Congress and every member in this house, and I yield back my time." "You are politicizing it, Congressman." The gentleman's time has expired. The Chair now recognizes Mr. Schmidt from Kansas for five minutes. "Thank you, Madam Chair, and I want to thank all three of our witnesses for being here today and answering our questions." "Mr. Secretary, let me start with you. It's a little bit off-topic for today but relevant. We are now more than two months into the funding shutdown for the Department of Homeland Security because folks at this end of the avenue have not yet been able to get to yes." "I hope we're going to get to yes here shortly. I think we've made some progress here today while we've been in this gathering. It's been necessary, as I understand it, for the administration to scrape out the pennies under the couch cushions in order to keep DHS running—and honestly, running not at full speed." "Has your department had any role in contributing to the ongoing operations of DHS while things have not been funded?" "Well, I wouldn't comment on the status of, of DHS. I'll just say the ways that we cooperate with them have continued. I mean, look at the southern... look at the southern border and the 11,000 troops we have there. Task Force Southern Border, ensuring that our border is locked down." "We've worked with law enforcement from day one on DHS to do that, just like we have with local law enforcement across the country. So, obviously we welcome DHS funding, but in the meantime we're going to protect the country, protect the homeland, and that's what we're doing with them." "Do what is necessary." "Very good." "General, let me ask you, or if this is more appropriate for the secretary, I will defer. But we, we've obviously been following with interest all of the developments, and the publicly reported developments, in Operation Epic Fury." "There was some publicly reported activity a while back that I had reason to think might involve some Kansas service personnel. We didn't know from the public reporting; we had simply been trying to find out whether our folks were involved or not." "We finally did get an answer. It took about four weeks, and when we got it, it was for the most part everything we'd read in the press with one exception—one exception." "It's a bit unsatisfactory when we're just trying to do our job and figure out whether our folks are implicated and whether we need to be doing something to help them, their families, or their folks back home." "I know you take seriously the importance of providing that information, but can you just offer some reassurance that you do take those inquiries seriously and that that was perhaps not—" "We do, sir. I'm not exactly sure of the situation you're talking about, but I'll get the teams together, figure out what broke down there, and fix it." "Appreciate it very, very much." "Mr. Secretary, let me come back to you. In talking with Kansans who either have been deployed or in support of folks who've been deployed, I am under the impression that there has been a particularly high, especially early in the operation prior to the ceasefire, particularly high amount of concussive activity, and that we might be well advised to be prepared when folks come home for concussive injuries, for combat-related PTSD, perhaps at levels we aren't expecting—at least those of us watching from the outside of the operation." "Can you say anything either about that in particular, or more generally, about what types of health and post-combat related health issues we ought to be preparing for when folks come home from this operation in particular?" "Well, I know, Congressman, all of that is tracked very closely. As it pertains to this budget, there's $70 million for more research on that. But I would note that as it's tracked and identified, also 90% of those with injuries in this conflict have returned to duty." "So, but that doesn't mean there couldn't still be a lingering effect, which is noted and will be continued to be tracked." "And can I follow on that? I appreciate that answer very much. We're just..." "I suspect particularly to Guard personnel who have been deployed and may, when they come back, return to their civilian roles, they may be in communities far from a military post, they may be in communities that don't have dedicated health care services that may be particularly focused on concussive damage or post-traumatic stress damage. Is there particular thought on things we ought to be doing to prepare to serve that population when those men and women come home?" "I think the biggest one is identifying it up front, doing that testing up front to identify what has occurred, and then having the ability to track those service members." "I know as a front-line leader, that became part of my responsibility as well—tracking those who may be more susceptible to it, whether they have a formal network to address it or not. So, definitely something we care about. TRICARE, ensuring they have increased access in places where there aren't military facilities, is an important part of it." "Thank you, Mr. Secretary. We care as well, and we want to be helpful on that front when our folks come home." "Last question, particular to some folks in Kansas. When you were here last year, Mr. Secretary, I talked about the cooperation between the Veterans Health Agency and DHA and some work we've been trying to do at Irwin Community Hospital at Fort Riley." "This committee, the Congress, and the President enacted into law a provision that causes some cooperation between the two to happen, and we are very excited to report that things are looking very good out at Irwin." "The reason I raise this with you today is that some of the folks who made that happen were the commanding general of the First Infantry, General Roden, who's about to deploy to Europe, I believe in early June; his chief medical officer, who's about to leave for a different assignment; and the Garrison Commander, who's about to leave. Can we get those things signed before they leave?" The Chair now recognizes Mr. Tran from California for five minutes. "Thank you so much, Chairwoman. Thank you, Secretary Hegseth, General Caine, and Mr. Hurst, for being here today." "You know, after I was honorably discharged from the Army Reserve, the GI Bill helped me go to school and ultimately become a lawyer. My focus was representing clients who were wrongfully terminated or faced discrimination and harassment at work." "Mr. Secretary, if I were in the courtroom trying to prove that you discriminate against our military heroes, I wouldn't have to look very far or very hard. In just 16 months, you have fired more than a dozen senior military leaders, and you've delayed or canceled the promotion of even more." "Your chief of staff told the Secretary of the Army that President Trump would not want to stand..." "That's not true, that was totally false reporting..." "I'm not getting to the question yet, Mr. Secretary..." "...you told the Secretary of the Army that the President would not want to stand next to a Black female officer at military events. But you know, despite all of this, I remain very hopeful that you can and will do better for the men and women who have dedicated their lives to protect our great country." "You're here to ask for 1.5 trillion dollars. That's a huge budget, and I want to make sure that you have the ability to manage it. Secretary Hegseth, you would agree with me when I say that we want the most qualified people in every position of our military, correct? That's a question." "Yes." "Thank you. General Caine, you would want the same?" "Yes." "Thank you. You know, I share the bipartisan concern about the firing of General George.
