I know the FA-XX is our next-generation strike fighter, but I would just love to hear your plans and timelines for the future of naval aviation. Well, I appreciate your comments, and I would defer to the chairman, too, on the remaining utility of the F-18s, which are still flying critical missions right now off our carriers and do a fantastic job. They are 30-plus years old, but yes. Yes, correct. Which is why the issue of the FA-XX is important. It's not a matter of if; it's a matter of when. It's a matter of trade-offs in industry, design, and capabilities, ensuring we're not slowing other things down at the behest of others. I think the August timeframe, as I'm sure you're familiar, is what we're looking at. It's a critical capability that we need to fund for the future of naval aviation. I would defer if there is anything you want to add. Sure, I think you covered it. You know, we don't need access basing and overflight off of a CSG and a CVN, and so it gives the secretary and the president a lot of options. I just want to echo the thanks for the joint force, on behalf of the joint force, for all three CSGs that are out there getting after it right now. I share your husband's jealousy, but I just want the force to know how much we appreciate them, especially the Ford. Thank you. Thanks very much, General. And then, Mr. Secretary, just again representing a big Navy district and having a concern for the Navy, could you shed any light on why the Secretary of the Navy was relieved, and what that timeline to replace or have a new Secretary of the Navy appointed looks like? I think that's one of the reasons why we struggle with basing decisions and naval aviation. I wish we would talk more about the contributions of our aviators, but could you shed any light on that issue? Well, as the president stated, we thank the previous secretary for his service. We appreciate a lot of the initiatives that he undertook. Ultimately, it was time for a new leadership and a new direction as far as running fast toward those objectives, so we made a change. Personnel is policy. The sooner you identify issues—especially as we talked about general officers, evaluating whether they're running with the mission they've been given—you've got to make a change if they're not. It is the same with civilians. In this case, we made that change. Is there any timeline to replace or have a new Secretary of the Navy appointed? Well, right now we have an acting secretary, In Hung-Kao, who did a fantastic job as the number two. He'll be acting for now, and I would imagine we'll know in due time. Thank you. And then General Caine, I know you're a VMI graduate, and I know you're watching some of the partisan attacks that have been going on with some of our senior military academies, specifically in Virginia. I was wondering if you had any thoughts or have had any discussions about how we can remove some of our senior military academies from these partisan state politics. I think it requires a further discussion. We had 170 Army ROTC officers that we commissioned out of that school specifically, but we've seen pushes to shut down, defund, or replace their board of visitors. Do you have any thoughts on how we can be more protective of those great schools? Well, Ma'am, you sort of started where I'm going to land on the importance of staying out of politics as the chairman, and not unintentionally or inadvertently stepping into a political matter where I should not. As the secretary said, people are policy, and policies are things that we don't do in the joint force. I appreciate all of the commissioning sources that are out there. The gentlelady's time has expired. The chair now recognizes the gentlelady from Washington, Ms. Strickland. Thank you, Chairman Rogers and Member Smith, and thank you to the witnesses for being here today. This committee is being asked to consider a $1.5 trillion DOD budget request that funds not only weapons and system programs, but the people, leadership, readiness, and trust required to make them effective. When senior leaders are removed abruptly, or merit-based promotions are disrupted without clear explanation, Congress does have the responsibility to understand the rationale and operational consequences. So I'm going to focus on Secretary Hegseth. To start, no one is disputing that senior officers serve at the pleasure of civilian leadership. The issue in front of us is whether the decision to remove General Randy George strengthened the Army or created an avoidable disruption during an active operational period. General George, who also served as commanding general at First Corps at JBLM, has been serving for four decades, including multiple command and operational leadership roles, culminating as Chief of Staff of the Army, responsible for readiness, force modernization, and the welfare of over a million soldiers and civilians. He was removed in the middle of an active conflict involving US operations against Iran when leadership continuity is most critical. Public reporting indicates that General George's removal may have followed disagreements over Army personnel matters, including concerns regarding withheld promotions. So my question, Mr. Secretary, is this: What specific national security risk, mission risk, or leadership concern did General George present that justified removing him in the middle of a conflict, or was he removed because he challenged some decisions being made? As I stated earlier, out of respect to these officers, I don't discuss the nature of the removal. But I would ask an earnest question of you: Where does General George fall in the operational chain of command? So I'm asking you why did you fire him? Don't change the— You talked about an operational moment. Where does General George fall in the operational chain of command? No, it's about national risk, mission risk, and leadership concern. What did he present that justified removing him in the middle of a conflict? Do you know where General George serves on the operational chain of command? So I'm asking you my question one more time, sir. Don't try to flip it on me. Why was he removed? Was he a national security risk, mission risk, or leadership concern? Yes or no? We don't— We don't— okay, that's okay. Don't answer the question, Mr. Hegseth. You also won't answer where he is in the operational chain of command because he isn't in it. He isn't in the operational chain of command. The gentlelady reclaimed her time, Mr. Secretary. You want to tie it to the war, but you don't even know where he is in the chain of command. Mr. Secretary, some respect. It's the gentlelady from Washington's time. Under DODI 1320.04, the Secretary of Defense may withhold or disapprove forwarding a nomination after service recommendations in limited circumstances, including newly identified adverse information, pending investigations, reportable information, questions regarding mental, physical, moral, or professional qualification, incomplete or non-compliant nomination packages, missing certifications, or the need for additional clarification from the military department. Several colonels who were reportedly selected for Brigadier General were removed or withheld after board selection and service certification. Which of these specific grounds applied to each withheld officer, and what objective evidence supported these decisions? Congressman, that was just one of many boards across the services where we will perform similar reviews, and I anticipate other officers will be removed also. Okay. But what were the grounds for those? Again, we don't talk about that out of respect for those officers. We don't talk about the nature of that, and we all serve at the pleasure of the president. We're solving for an institution that needs to be based on merit and professional execution. There's been a lot of gender and demographic engineering that's been going on for reasons other than the focus of exactly what we should be doing on the battlefield. I did not raise gender. I did not raise gender. That's implied in every statement that everybody makes. And so ultimately, as we review all these boards, it has nothing to do with anything other than their performance on the battlefield or their performance in their career. These people are thoroughly vetted, thoroughly researched, and I guess my question is: Is this about loyalty to Trump or is it about what's good for the mission? I understand what you're implying, but it's about the Constitution and our military and its ability to execute. I'm going to make sure the president has the finest officers across the force prepared to deliver. So this committee funds weapons, readiness, recruiting, professional development, and leadership pipelines. But no amount of money can compensate for a force that may lose trust in the fairness of advancement. And this is fairness. We believe in merit; we have always had a merit-based system. That's not the argument. No, we have not. Not under the Biden administration, we did not. It became social engineering, not merit, and we're fixing it quick. No, we didn't. No, we didn't. And when we consider this budget request, we must ensure that taxpayer dollars are matched by sound judgment, credible leadership, and a personnel system worthy of those who serve. I yield back, Mr. Chair. The gentlelady yields back. The Chair now recognizes the gentleman from Georgia, Mr. Scott. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Gentlemen, thank you for being here today. I'm sorry I was late. I was on the floor of the House of Representatives with a voice roll vote, and I did not hear the majority of the testimony. But Secretary Hegseth, I respect you. I do want you to know I disagree with the firing of General George. I've sent you a letter. I look forward to seeing the responses on that. And I just— I mean, it takes 218 votes to get something across the floor of the House of Representatives. With the NDAA and with budget plus-ups and everything else, we're going to lose some Republican votes on the plus-up. We're going to have to have some Democratic votes to do the things that we have to do to fund the Department of Defense. I would encourage everybody to keep that in mind. General Caine, the joint staff plays a unique role in our strategic direction. The integration of services is what gives us a coherent joint force. I have seen where disputes in the Air Force and the Army led to the elimination of systems that we needed, as the department has made attempts to right-size various components. Could you talk about the importance of the joint staff and the value to the joint force? You bet, sir. And I'm incredibly proud of the joint staff. You know, our job is to integrate options, to deliver those options to our national command authority, and work with all the services. The one area of exclusivity we have pursuant to the 2017 NDAA is as the global integrator. Our J-2, J-3, J-5, J-17, and J-8 all do a fantastic job, along with the 1, the 6, and the 4 every day, to really bring the totality of American combat capability together into a range of options and risks that we can then bring to the secretary and the president. They are extraordinary in everything they do, and I appreciate the chance to brag on them a little bit. Well, I have watched as the Air Force was paying for a system that was predominantly used by the Army in the past. I'm talking about JSTARS specifically. It was a ground moving target indicator that the Army used, but it was coming out of the Air Force budget. I think that dispute led to the standing down of a system before another system was ready to go, and that's a dangerous thing. I've spent a fair amount of time in Africa from this committee's work and from HPSCI's work. We lost Airbase 101 and 201. All of you at that table know what happened there. I blame that on the State Department and the way the State Department handled that. How do you intend to handle this? Are we looking at Niger and the potential to get 101 and 201 back? I do believe that an apology would go a long way with that country. As someone who's met with the ministers over there—and interestingly enough, Minister Toumba wanted tractors, he wanted John Deere tractors is what he asked for—where will we base out of if we can't get Niger back? And are we looking at getting 101 and 201 back? Yes, sir. Well, we all want tractors, so I echo his thoughts there. I know General Anderson is doing a lot of work right now, leaning forward and doing a fantastic job along with his deputy commander, General Brennan, on looking at the total requirements across the western flank for access basing and overflight. Bases 101 and 201 are among many things that they're considering. I think the key is: what are they going to be able to do with the resources that they have? We're always in a resource-constrained environment, congressman, in order to put sufficient pressure on the CT adversaries there to hold them at bay. Well, the mil-to-mil relationships are still good. I've been out with some of the ODA teams over there as a civilian and as a member of Congress. It does bother me that our footprint has gotten so much smaller over there, but I do know with drones and other things it makes it much more difficult to have those outposts. I appreciate all of you being here. I am sorry that I have not been here for all of it, but I appreciate your work. The gentleman yields back. The Chair now recognizes the gentleman from New York, Mr. Ryan. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. "Head wounds, heavy bleeding, and then just shrapnel all over. So folks are bleeding from their abdomen, bleeding from arms, bleeding from legs." Secretary Hegseth, do you know who said that? I'm not sure I do. It was one of our soldiers describing the devastating Iranian drone strike at Port Shuaiba on March 1st in Kuwait. As you know, six of our soldiers were killed and over 30 were wounded. Secretary, do you know the range of the Shahed-136 one-way attack drone, approximately? It depends on the variant, but it's got serious distance, several hundred miles. Do you know how far Port Shuaiba is from Iran? There's a reason why we took extreme measures in terms of force protection, because we understood the proximity challenges. I'm going to reclaim my time. Mr. Chairman, reclaim my time. It's well under 100 miles. Before the war started, there was clear, credible intelligence that Shuaiba was high on Iran's target list. Internal analysis said the site was indefensible from aerial attack and should not be used. Yet you sent our soldiers from the 103rd Sustainment Command there anyway. Is that true or false? True or false? Straightforward question. Are you going to give me a chance to answer or just play gotcha? Did you send them there or not? We took proactive measures from the beginning to ensure force protection and defensive posture were maximized across the theater. I'll take that as a yes and reclaim my time. Now let's talk, Mr. Secretary, let's talk about what defenses they had. Prior to the attack, officers on the ground knew our troops were vulnerable. In fact, they requested additional force protection. Did they receive it? Wherever humanly possible, force protection and counter-UAS were always made available. They did not. I'll save you the time. They did not. In fact, when asked to describe the base's defense, one survivor who's come forward from the unit said, quote, "I mean, I would put it in the none category, from a drone defense capability, none." So let's be clear: no counter-drone capability, no counter-rocket systems, no counter-mortar or counter-artillery. Not even the basic overhead protection that you and I had 20 years ago in Iraq. And now, six of our soldiers are dead. The next day, you downplayed the attack. You said it was a squitter that squeaked through fortified defenses. But since then, thankfully, brave survivors have come forward to set the record straight. One of our surviving soldiers told CBS, quote, "Painting a picture that one squeaked through is a falsehood." Another said the unit was, quote, "unprepared to provide any defense for itself. It was not a fortified position." Another survivor said the building's protection was, quote, "about as weak as one gets." Secretary Hegseth, that is obviously in direct contradiction to what you said from the Pentagon podium the next day. So are you saying that these soldiers, our soldiers who survived this horrific attack, are lying? What I'm saying is before the commencement of the conflict, we put in the maximum defensive posture we could. We moved seven— That's a direct contradiction to what they said. ...in this— in this— can I speak or are you just going to monologue falsehoods all over the place? It's not a falsehood. We moved 7,500 troops— I'm reclaiming my time based on the intel. Stop. Based on the intel. Stop. Reclaiming my time. Because you yell doesn't make you right. Just because you yell doesn't make you right. I'm reclaiming my time on behalf of these survivors. You just said what they said is a falsehood. There's a much larger picture at play here that included integrated air defenses, bunkers, and moving people off the "X" to ensure that they were not part of the target. We moved those troops, and all across the theater, thousands of troops off the X and off of their bases because we knew what Iran was going to try to strike, and we knew there could be a tragic moment. There could be a tragic moment where something could get through. Then why, Mr. Secretary, are they dead? Of course, that's the consequence of conflict, and we remember those six every single day. But don't play games with— I'm not playing games, and I want to finish with one more quote from a survivor of the attack, and I want this on the record. Telling the truth is important, and we're not going to learn from these mistakes if we pretend these mistakes didn't happen. Secretary Hegseth, those soldiers, our soldiers, hold the truth. Those soldiers are braver than you are. They are asking for accountability. They deserve accountability, and I'm asking for the same, starting with you. And as I said a year ago, you need to resign immediately. I yield back. The gentleman yields back. The Chair now recognizes the gentleman from Texas, Mr. Fallon. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Secretary Hegseth, I want to tell you something right now. You focused our military once again and finally, and you shaped it into a meritocracy. You've dramatically increased the United States' ability to project power and, when needed, lethality. As a result, our deterrence has never been more evident the world over. So thank you. General Caine, you mentioned in your opening remarks George Marshall. Unfortunately, some of your immediate predecessors were clearly one type of general. They were Democratic generals. One of them, even in this committee, used politically charged rhetoric of the left. And I don't want Democratic generals, and for that matter, I don't want Republican generals either. I want American generals. You, sir, are an American general. You know exactly what we need. We need generals on the Joint Chiefs of Staff who know, understand, respect, and embody their role to serve this Republic, the troops, and the mission. And that is precisely what you are doing. I want to thank you because you do remind me of George C. Marshall. He was a great American, and so are you. Secretary Hegseth, do you know of any nation-state that ever enriched uranium to 60% that then didn't make a nuclear weapon? Well, first of all, I want to second your comments about the chairman, and I agree and appreciate how he does his job. Usually, when a country pursues enrichment at that level, it's pursuing a nuclear weapon. One hundred percent of the countries that have reached 60% enrichment have then produced a nuclear weapon. And is it true that Iran was in the process of building such a swarm of UAVs and a ballistic missile shield conventionally that, once they wanted to "bake the cake" because they had all the ingredients to make the nuclear weapon, it would have been damn near impossible for us or anyone else to prevent them? As I pointed to, the president was wise enough to understand that at its weakest moment, following the 12-day war in Midnight Hammer, now was the time to degrade their conventional capabilities. The umbrella they were building of missiles—long-range, short-range, cruise missiles, and one-way attack drones—was what they were going to use to blackmail the countries around them to not do anything about their nuclear program when they broke out from 60%. We knew that to be their intention, and something had to be done about it, and President Trump had the courage to do it. Well, President Trump—there's been a lot of quotes, people have been mentioning his words, and I wanted to mention some that I thought were interesting. Here's a quote: "I will take no options off the table to prevent Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon." Barack Obama. "Iran will never get a nuclear weapon." Joe Biden. "I will never hesitate to take whatever action necessary and I will never allow Iran to acquire nuclear weapons. All, all options are on the table." Kamala Harris. And this one: "We would totally obliterate Iran. That's a terrible thing to say, but those people who run Iran need to understand that." Hillary Clinton, 2008. So it seems to me that Barack Obama, Hillary Clinton, Joe Biden, Kamala Harris, and Donald Trump had something in common: they weren't going to allow Iran to get a nuclear weapon. Some of them, four of them, were all words, and one of them took action. We're involved in a land war in Asia. I get it. It's complicated. Ninety-three million people, much larger than Iraq, but 80% of their country hates their guts. This is a regime that murdered 40,000 of them and gunned them down in the streets. They're responsible for over 600 American deaths. How long was World War II? Do you have any idea in days? I have it right here in days exactly. I got to multiply by 365, but yeah, it was 1,347 days. Korea was 1,129 days. And our involvement in World War I, which was rather quick, was 585. How long have we been involved in Iran? We are just shy of 60 days. Six-zero days. Two months. And what is the degradation of their Navy right now, Secretary? Their conventional Navy is destroyed. They had three drone aircraft carriers; they have none. They had 11 submarines; they have none. They had different frigate classes; they have none. They may have small speedboats at this point, coast guard type vessels, but their conventional navy is gone. Air Force? Likewise, functionally destroyed. Command and control? It's hard for them—there's a reason why there's a challenge in communicating around a deal, and that is because their command and control is fundamentally broken as far as who's in control and how they communicate. We could make very good arguments that the serious nature of any action in a country like Iran had good things to consider before doing it. There were also very good reasons to do it. The fact of the matter is, ladies and gentlemen, Republicans and Democrats, we're here now. This is an evil regime. We can fundamentally reshape not only the region but the world. They're the largest exporter of terror and ballistic missile technologies. We need, gentlemen, to win. Secretary, General Caine, win. I yield back. The gentleman yields back. The Chair now recognizes the gentleman from Pennsylvania, Mr. Deluzio. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. We all just heard pretty powerful reporting, courtesy of Mr. Ryan, from many of our wounded troops who were injured in the attack in Kuwait. Mr. Secretary, you heard direct quotes from some of them via CBS's reporting. They were willing to talk to the press. They said things like they were unprepared, providing defense for themselves and the unit. They said things like, "We were moved closer to Iran to a deeply unsafe area that was a known target." Your spokesperson, Sean Parnell, in response to that reporting, said it was "not true". Do you agree with that? Are you calling these guys liars? I'm not calling our troops liars, and I don't know if what you're representing is correct or not. I'll take you at your word on that. But all I know is that we took every effort possible at the commencement of this campaign to ensure the defense of our troops, to include moving them off of known bases to places that were not known and where we had intelligence. They were not known, and then those were fortified with bunkers or other fortifications with theater air defenses. Was our concern that something could get through? Let's— I'm going to pause you there. I'm going to give you a chance— Absolutely. But no, you're disparaging me that I don't care— I'm asking you whether you think they're liars or not. That's what I asked. You are disparaging me that I don't care about the passing of our troops. No, I asked if you thought they were liars, Secretary. That's disparaging and smearing in every way. Nobody cares more about the fate of our troops. Nobody cares about the health of our troops. Nobody wants to bring them all home more than I do. Mr. Secretary, Mr. Secretary, I understand, but he controls the time. He controls the time. You get to control your answer and— If we're allowed to answer. Mr. Chairman, thank you. Secretary, my question was clear: whether you thought they were liars or not. You don't seem to want to answer it; we can move on. The other part of what your spokesperson said, and you just reiterated it here, was that, and I'm quoting your spokesperson: "Every possible measure has been taken to safeguard our troops." End quote. You mean to tell us, and let me give you a chance to clarify, that there is not a single additional thing you could have done to protect those troops who are now wounded, six of whom are dead? I'm telling you that, as a department, we did every conceivable thing at my level and every echelon down to ensure the maximum force protection for our troops. We live in a dangerous world and in a dangerous place against a determined enemy that can't have a nuclear weapon, and that requires sacrifice and risk. Tragically, in this case, it meant six Americans lost their lives and others were wounded. We all know that, and we all know that's part of the cost. But it does not mean we didn't care. It doesn't mean we didn't commit. It doesn't mean we didn't do everything possible, which we did. I didn't ask you if you cared, and I'm not saying that. But let's be clear about what was done. There were T-walls there, essentially. There was no overhead protection. The known threat in that environment, and I think we all here understand it, is an overhead drone strike. There was no protection for an overhead drone strike. So I'm struggling to understand how your answer and your spokesperson's answer is that "every possible measure was taken." We can move on. Mr. Chairman, Chairman Caine, I want to ask you something on a different topic. Folks at home know you're the senior military officer in our armed services, but they may not have as much of an understanding of your role in the chain of command. You're outside the operational chain of command, but you are the President's and the Secretary's senior military advisor. I want to ask a very basic thing: what you would expect our commanders in the field, our troops, to do should the enemy lay down their weapons and try to surrender? What would you expect commanders in the field to do? Well, sir, I think I'll go back to something I said earlier, with deep respect for your question, but I think you're trying to take me to a partisan place here and I can't let you do that. General, as always, I respect your desire to avoid it. I'm not trying to put you in a partisan place. No, no, and I know that. But you know, our officers and enlisted service members always follow lawful orders. There's a checklist for them to do that, and I just— I'll stay in the middle. Very good. Understood, General. Secretary, let me give you a chance, and you've been asked about this earlier. You said in, I think, your press conference, essentially, and I'll quote you here: "No quarter, no mercy for our enemies." That's the end of the quote. Is your guidance that you expect our troops in harm's way, if there's a foreign enemy surrendering and laying down their arms, that they're supposed to provide no quarter and kill them? You denied it, but you were indeed trying to take the Chairman to a partisan place. That was General Milley; that was the previous administration, which played politics, and we don't play that way. It's a simple question. I'm asking what you meant. We fight to win, and we ensure the rules of engagement are such that our troops have every authority possible— I'll give you another chance. In saying that, are you trying to tell commanders that that's what you expect? You're in the chain of command. It's a simple question. I understand that. My commanders know exactly what the guidance is with each and every mission, and they know every tool they have in their toolkit to be effective. Secretary, in refusing to answer it, I think you speak volumes. I think that's a dangerous thing. We all know the expectations, we know the law of war, and we know what is lawful. I'd expect our commanders to understand that as well. I yield back, Mr. Chairman. The gentleman yields back. The Chair now recognizes the gentleman from Guam, Mr. Moylan. Thank you, thank you, Mr. Chairman. Secretary Hegseth, thank you for your continued attention to Guam. I really appreciate it. While leading the War Department here, I'd like to remind my colleagues that one of your first official trips as Secretary was to visit Guam. During your confirmation hearing, you identified the island as America's most strategic location globally. This commitment does not go unnoticed, sir. So I thank you, Mr. Secretary, for that. As we continue our buildup for the defense of our nation in Guam and the people of Guam, we have many active-duty personnel, but we also have a lot of Department of War civilian personnel, too. Right now, one of the most consistent questions from my constituents has been asking: when will the Living Quarter Allowance authorization for War Department civilians in Guam be implemented? Recently, your office confirmed that the guidance should be published in early May. Will you work to keep that timeline? And will you work to ensure the Living Quarter Allowance will properly incentivize local hires to stay on Guam? Well, if that's what we stated, we need to stick with it. That's been our pledge. I think it reiterates that the amount we're paying for housing, for quality of life, and for living in certain especially unique locations is usually mismatched. It causes staffing and workforce problems that we need to address in a tailored nature to some of those unique locations like Guam, like Hawaii, and others in Europe. So yes, we are committed to that. And I would just note that in our budget, we've got almost a billion for the Guam defense system also. We're investing in ensuring that that critical location you described is part of our theater umbrella. Excellent, sir. Thank you for that. Another longstanding request I have from the War Department has been the increased support for Guam's power grid. As you know, we use the "one island" approach there, working together with our water, our power, our port, our airport, and our hospitals—it's all working in support together with the military. But one of the important ones for us is Guam's power grid. Most of Guam's grid is owned by the department and is leased to the Guam Power Authority. As the military demands upon the power grid increase, do you see either O&M funds or military construction funds being the best path for the department to support Guam's power grid needs? I would welcome your input first and foremost, as we have throughout. But as was noted, we've got 450 million for small modular reactors, which is a new approach we're taking. We think this makes our bases more resilient. If there's one place the American people count on being able to be prepared in that moment, it's our bases wherever they are, whether CONUS or OCONUS. These reactors are a key part of getting at that. So that would be part of how I'd answer your question. I appreciate that, Mr. Secretary. Also, the Guam Power Authority is the only one in charge of all the transmission lines. So where that power source is, and I know there was some consideration of the small nuclear reactors, the power authority will be the one transmitting for the transmission lines for all the power on the island. So I look forward to working with your office. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Another deep concern for the people of Guam has been the cost of housing, which is often driven up by large numbers of servicemen living on-base in Guam and off-base on Guam. I've been impressed by the recent efforts by Joint Region Marianas to reduce the reliance on existing housing stock in both the north and south of Guam. Can you tell us how the department housing strategy in Guam will work to keep homes affordable to locals, and if increased private sector involvement will help address my constituents' concerns? Well, we've got about four and a half billion for family housing in the FY27 budget. It's something we recognize as a challenge, and in places like that, a unique challenge. There has also been a bureaucratic construction process which has been a huge impediment, and as you can imagine, President Trump has demanded that we do something different and accelerate a different process to deliver those things in more of a private sector, less bureaucratic fashion, and we look forward to employing those. Excellent, sir. And finally, we did have one discussion when we first met on Guam regarding the Medal of Honor possible recipients who are deserving of this. Now, Guam has never had any Medal of Honor recipients, and there are three names that we've— The gentleman's time has expired. Thank you. I would like to point out, you know, once I recognize a member, they have control of that five minutes. If they want to give a speech, they can give a speech. If they want to ask questions, they can ask questions. But if you ask a question, I would hope you give the witness a chance to answer. But if they choose not to, then the witness has to recognize it's their time and cut it off and go back to them. But I want to stick— I want to be able to be respectful of each other and be able to make sure we maximize the use of this time. But everybody has to understand it's the member's five minutes once I recognize them. Does the ranking member want to offer— Yeah, I just want to second that. Yeah, it is the member's time. I do hope members, you know, when you do ask a question, you've got to allow for more than two words. Yes, if they filibuster or don't answer the question, feel free to interrupt. But I hope we can keep this respectful. It's more productive for us, and for the witnesses, and for those watching. So thank you, Mr. Chair. And with that, we will recognize the gentlelady from Hawaii, Ms. Tokuda. Thank you, Mr. Chair. On February 2nd, President Trump said, "We should take over the voting in at least 15 places. The Republicans ought to nationalize the voting," despite the Constitution assigning primary responsibility for elections to the states. Federal law also draws very clear limits. Under 18 US Code Section 592, military officers are prohibited from deploying troops or armed personnel to polling places unless necessary to repel armed enemies of the United States. That is the exception. Not politics, not suspicion, not conspiracy theories. Armed enemies. Secretary Hegseth, do you think American voters are enemies of the United States? A simple yes or no will suffice. I think legal voters— I think everybody who's legal should vote, yes. So American voters are not enemies of the United States. You're agreeing with me. I think America's legal citizens should vote, yes. How many armed enemies of the United States have attempted to interfere in the last election? Can you recall any time when armed enemies of the United States attempted to interfere in the last election or the 2020 election? Well, we just had the King of England here; that was a while ago. But they tried— So 250 years ago. Okay, very good. All right. Well, Secretary Hegseth, if President Trump ordered you to deploy troops to polling places during the midterm elections this fall, which would violate the law I just cited, would you implement his order? Yes or no? What you're trying to insinuate is that the President would give unlawful orders and we would somehow deploy troops as a result. The evidence of our department is that we've worked alongside law enforcement very effectively for 15 months. Los Angeles would have been on fire for the summer had we not come across with our law enforcement friends helping them. I humbly disagree with that, but this is not a hypothetical situation that I'm inferring on your President. In January of this year, he told the New York Times that he regretted not ordering the National Guard to seize voting machines in key states after the 2020 election. The last time I heard from you, the last time we had any kind of armed enemies at any type of voting places was 250 years ago. We're talking 2020. So if the President, again I'm asking you, orders you to break election law, violate the Constitution and the law, would you follow the orders of the President? Yes or no? The President— you'd like to insinuate that the President issues illegal orders, and he doesn't and he has not. It's a simple question. Who would you follow: the President or the Constitution? I will note that in 2024, troops were— that was Joe Biden, by the way. Joe Biden. Troops were deployed to polling locations in 15 states. 2024, Joe Biden, troops deployed to polling locations in 15 states. Explain that one to me. I am asking you a question now. You seem to really like Joe Biden. You've brought his name up more than many other topics today. Answer the question. Who are you beholden to, Mr. Secretary: the President or the Constitution? I very proudly swear an oath to the Constitution. I very proudly swear an oath in Los Angeles— But you can't answer that question. When I asked you who you would follow, you couldn't answer that either. So I think our answer is very clear. Obviously, you have taken a line of loyalty only to the President and not the people of this country. Let's move on now and let's talk about AI. The Maven system, which appears to be the program on record being used in Iran, does the targeting work. We know of about 2,000 people who used US— It's called the Maven system, not the Marvin system. Excuse me, thank you so much for that correction. It does it in a fraction of the time. The intention was to compress the kill chain, and the result was an attack that killed almost 200 children. Would you agree that tragic actions like this prove that when we trade time and humans for safety, people die? The Maven system is a very effective tool, and inside that, we have very prescriptive processes by which we ensure as much precision as humanly possible in very complex battlefield environments. So you think we took every absolute effort to make sure that innocent civilians did not die in this attack? I know that there is no country on planet Earth that takes more measures to ensure that civilian harm or civilian casualties are minimized than the United States of America and this War Department, and that is a fact in human history. Okay, and yet Mr. Secretary, you eliminated the Department's Civilian Harm Reduction staff. You eliminated them. For a country that takes great efforts, makes great efforts to make sure human casualties are reduced or eliminated, don't you think that goes actually counter to what you just said? Would these children not still be alive if you had not eliminated all of this staff working within Centcom? We didn't eliminate them. We made sure that those things they normally accomplish are at the field level where they are, and all of these processes are in place at every level, every single mission. Would you agree that something clearly has failed, whether it's our implementation of AI, or understanding our human dependence on AI? Would you not agree that something failed because almost 200 children died in Iran as a result of our bombing? You're insinuating something where an investigation is not complete. The gentlelady's time has expired. The Chair now recognizes the gentleman from Georgia, Dr. McCormick. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Gentlemen, I'm happy to have you here today and really excited about the things you've been able to accomplish. I'm just going to summarize, just sitting here for a few minutes listening to everybody politicize what we've done. I'm just going to break it down as best as I can and then I'm going to let you answer a couple of questions. First of all, just since the short period of time that the Trump administration has taken over, we've handed over control of Ukraine's destiny to Europe, where it belongs—a conglomerate that has a $20 trillion economy versus a $2 trillion Russian economy to actually take care of their own country in a very good way, their own continent. We've kept Iran, who talked about raining down fire from heaven and talked about us being the great Satan, from becoming a nuclear power. We've reshaped the geopolitical space in the Persian Gulf. We've re-established the Monroe Doctrine in the Western Hemisphere. We've basically executed two of the most amazing raids in history, almost flawless, that will be studied for a generation at least. We've reduced senior administrative billets, thank you. We've defeated Iran with under $30 billion spent, with minimal civilian casualties compared to any other war. People forget that in World War II, in one night we lost 100,000 civilians, totally innocent, in firebombings, just before we dropped the nuclear bombs. War is a horrible thing, but I think we've executed at least as best as possible in a very hard and consequential environment. That's just to sum up a few things, not to mention the recruiting that went from dismal and really something that could have crippled our military to now a surplus, just by a change in administration. That's pretty impressive for a short period of time. Thank you, and I commend you for overcoming those hardships. A couple of things that I want to get through. One of the things we talked about is the Incentivized Saving Act, which is something I wanted to apply to all bureaucracies. But when I was in the military, as both an operational officer and a logistics officer for my squadrons and my gun places, we got to the end of the fiscal year. If we didn't spend it, what happens? We lose it and we get our budget shrunk. Everybody knows that in the military; everybody knows that in every bureaucracy in America. One of the things I came up with is, at the end of the fiscal year, instead of just spending it, you actually can save half of it. 49% rolls over to the next year, 49% goes back to debt and deficit, and 2% is to reward the people for being good administrators of the money. I don't see a downside. This is the way that we reinvent government and the way we spend money. I wanted your opinion on something like that, because I think it is thinking outside the box, and I've seen waste, fraud, and abuse. We're trying to address that. This should be a bipartisan, bicameral approach to actually doing something right as we expand the budget, but also give us some wiggle room for putting money back and being good stewards of the people's money. What do you think about an idea like that? I very much like an idea like that, which creates all the right kinds of incentives. I would just add, in addition to that, which is the type of thing our department would support, the deals that we're cutting with these private sector companies on munitions ensure they build the plants and they build the assembly lines, which is exactly the right kind of incentive. If you can spend on facilities, you're going to make sure you make money on those facilities and the munitions that you produce. There's ownership in that stake. There's the right incentive. We need to overturn the entire way that the department does business. So I agree with that. We're pointing at the defense contractors, as the President has, and saying, "You need to do your business differently." This is not a gravy train of the government. You need to be beholden to us, actually. So I think the spirit of that is infused into everything we're doing at the department, and I think it's right in line with what you're talking about—running it more like a business where we have accountability and we're pointed in the right direction. We do have a lot of old programs we need to update. One of the things you talked about is a five-to-seven-year period of time of updating. I think almost year by year now, we're going to have to look at how we budget things in contracts because the technology is changing so dramatically, as General Caine pointed out. I know I'm running out of time real quick, but I want to also revisit the fact that you are still invited down to the University of North Georgia, one of the elite eight schools. We're looking forward to PTing with you. I appreciate you guys being here today. God bless you, and God bless our military service members. And with that, I yield. Sir, I've got to say this guy over my left shoulder is a University of North Georgia graduate. He doesn't want me to tell any of that, but he is. The President would be very happy—the President of the university, his great friend President Shannon, would be very happy to hear that. Thank you. The gentleman's time has expired. The Chair now recognizes the gentleman from North Carolina, Mr. Davis. Thank you, Mr. Chair. I also thank you, Mr. Secretary, Chairman Caine, and Mr. Herst. Shout out to Winterville. Thirteen US service members killed. Thirteen families forever changed. Thirteen heroes who put the uniform on and paid the ultimate sacrifice. We owe remembrance to those families. I just want you to know we see you, we stand with you, and we'll never forget. There are Gold Star families who are here today sitting in the room, carrying the Honor and Remember flags. We remember the sacrifice, and when we do so, words can't fully capture it. On April 3rd, the F-15E Strike Eagle pilots were rescued because we live by a simple promise: we do not leave anyone behind. That same spirit must be extended to those who are killed in action. That's why I arise again today and raise this question of the importance of passing H.R. 1363, the Honor and Remember Flag Recognition Act. Mr. Secretary, I raised this with you before, and actually, I come today to thank you for the response from your office and for actually engaging with us on this. I rise again today and bring this up because we must never lose sight of the true cost of war. As we commemorate 250 years in this country, the first call for independence, the Halifax County delegates moved forward with the first official action in my district. Our service members are the reason we are here today. They've delivered freedom. I believe it would be amazing if we could deliver for their families—those who've made the ultimate sacrifice—this flag, something to help them, ideally by Memorial Day, or at least to commemorate their families. I would simply ask if you have any thoughts whatsoever on how we continue to lift up and honor those who have been killed serving our country. Well, thank you for what you do on that topic. I think, first and foremost, we do it by doing right by the legacy of those that we lose, whether it's those 13 from this conflict or any other conflict. I've been at Dover every single time, as has the Chairman, as has the President and the Vice President when they can, to greet that and respect and honor that. In our department, we've established the first-ever Gold Star Task Force, where we've got a Gold Star advocate right in our front office working these issues on a daily basis, which is why it wouldn't surprise me that we so quickly got back to you. These are passions, these are deep in our DNA, things that we care about. How you treat a family who's given everything in that moment, not just at Dover but in every step of that process, is a reflection of how we feel about it. Mr. Secretary, thank you truly, just for the time here. I served mortuary and I've been to Dover; I deployed to Dover, so thank you there. I want to shift to Army Cadet Command, the senior ROTC programs' rebalance optimization plan. North Carolina Central University, in particular, has real access and logistical challenges that will be before the cadets. And when we think about building the next generation of leaders, I would just ask us to think about how we're putting barriers in front of some, and I would just ask quickly if you could please take a closer look at the impact on the cadets. I want to shift to another topic and really spend the last moment here as I'm running short: your memo, the April memo, on the non-official personal protection armor on Department of War property. My son is at Fort Benning right now, infantry, he's training—ideally he wants to be an 11 Bravo—and I shared the memo with him and he's shared it with his battle buddies to see the response. He's definitely Second Amendment enthused and an advocate, and they're really interested, you know, in this. My question is simple: how do you plan to provide any guidance on the denials, and how do you plan to track it? That was the intent—to make sure those men and women out there feel like they have their Second Amendment rights even when they're on post. And the burden of proof is on the command as to why they would deny, as opposed to the burden of proof on why they should allow. So the presumption is the same rights they would have outside the gate of a base, they have inside for personal protection purposes. Our bases shouldn't be gun-free zones except for the armory and the MPs. So the burden of proof is something we're going to pay very close attention to. In my last 15 seconds, Chairman Caine, thank you again for the conversation brought up. Do you have any final thoughts in terms of any reservations of how we're dealing with munitions, in terms of a supplemental on the depletion of munitions? The gentleman's time has expired. The Chair now recognizes the gentleman from Louisiana, Mr. Higgins. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. General Caine, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here today. Mr. Secretary, you stated in your opening statement that one of the foundational endeavors that you embrace is, in your words, to "forge a lethal arsenal of freedom." I don't think the spirit that you gentlemen are delivering within the Department of War and to the men and women that you serve could be more accurately described. It is notable for an American like me—an Army veteran, 65 years old. I don't believe in my lifetime I've witnessed this level of rebirth and hardcore focus that I'm witnessing out of the Pentagon right now. And I just commend you for driving that. And please know that you have a great deal of support, not just in this body and on this committee, but across the country. Regular Americans that have served, or have families that have served, have taken note. General, you said in your opening statement that, if called upon, we win our wars around the world. Those were your words. And we're going to make certain through this committee that you have the support, the equipment, and the funding that you need to, if called upon, win wars across the world. But we expect a lot out of you gentlemen. We expect you to liberate and secure the entire Western Hemisphere. One of my colleagues across the aisle stated that in a negative way; let me flip it to a positive: we absolutely do expect you to liberate and secure the entire Western Hemisphere. We expect you to drive Iran to its knees and free the world of the Iranian terrorist threat. We expect you to leverage NATO into an indomitable force. We expect you to set China back on its heels worldwide. We expect you to drive peace and stability through the projection of military power. We expect you to secure the transit of affordable, transportable American energy products as the indisputable cornerstone of economic prosperity for every allied nation. That's a lot of expectations. And what you gentlemen have laid out today as you've answered questions is solid. You've made it very clear that that's the pathway we're on, and I support you. My Democrat colleagues have made it clear today that, after careful study and thorough research of world history, they've come to the conclusion that soldiers, sailors, airmen, and marines are far safer if they're never deployed into conflict. You've made it very clear that you take your business seriously, and we support you. I certainly do. Mr. Secretary, in consultation with your staff, reflective of your goals, your mission, and your stated intentions—specifically, in consultation with your staff in Personnel and Readiness—I proposed legislation to codify your guidance that combat fitness standards must be rigorous, with no separate scoring scales, adjusted standards, or accommodations. There is to be only a single, uncompromising standard—not to exclude any American from any opportunity, but to ensure we have the best, most capable Americans in the most critical positions. Would you welcome this body making your guidance into law in the NDAA? I would welcome that, Congressman. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, for clarifying that. I have a question about Iran, and I'll give you the final 30 seconds to answer: was Iran moving precipitously towards nuclear weapons capability, Mr. Secretary? Even after the obliteration of Midnight Hammer, what was clear is that the intention of Iran remained in the pursuit of nuclear weapons. And after, as hard as they were hit by Israel, it created an opportunity to get rid of the conventional shield. That's why we talked about missile production, the Navy, and the defense industrial base—to set them back from blackmailing the region and the world in pursuit of nuclear weapons, because we understood how much they could continue to pursue them. Roger that. Thank you, gentlemen. Mr. Chairman, I yield. The gentleman's time has expired. The Chair now recognizes the gentleman from California, Mr. Cisneros. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Thank you and the ranking member for bringing us here today for this hearing. It is very important to the nation. I want to thank our witnesses for all being here today. Mr. Secretary, the President recently made an announcement that the state-activated National Guard would start receiving active duty pay for federal missions, and I believe you kind of made similar remarks as well. I'm happy to see that announcement because it brought much-needed attention to this disparity. Whether you agree with the missions or not, when guardsmen are called up for active duty—and they really have no choice in the matter for homeland missions—often they are receiving inconsistent pay and benefits depending on their status. This issue has persisted for decades. This is why earlier this year Rep. Bergman and I introduced the Duty Status Reform Act, which would address the issue head-on. I believe this is the right time to push this over the finish line. It's something the National Guard Bureau supports, the Council of Governors supports, and just the other week, the service personnel chiefs were all here and they support it. The TAGs support it. So, Mr. Secretary, as a former member of the National Guard, I am asking you here today: would you consider supporting consolidating the 29 duty statuses for the military reserve component into a balanced and equitable system that would also help the National Guard receive retirement points when they are called up for these federal missions? Would you support that? Well, Congressman, thank you for your efforts on this. It makes a ton of sense, and we support these efforts for duty status reform in pursuit of things that work for the department and for the troops, because they deserve the kind of pay and benefits that are commensurate with active duty troops. So we would look forward to working with your office to get at duty status reform. I think now is the right time. Thank you for that. General Caine, I ask you the same question as an individual who's served in the Air National Guard. Would you consider supporting duty status reform that's going to simplify this and help our National Guard troops? Sir, I'll be careful to stay in my lane. My personal opinion, not the Chairman's opinion, as somebody who's lived the house of pain of 30 different duty statuses, is that I would support it. As the Chairman, I'll leave personnel—and you know this from your prior life—to our civilian leaders who do that. But I do think we owe a better set of options for our guardsmen and our reservists across the force. Well, thank you both for that. This is something that I think needs to get done and something I've been working on for years now. The National Guard Bureau supports it, and so I hope you will, as you said, work with us to get that done. OMB seems to be our roadblock, so I'm hoping you can kind of push that roadblock down so that we can finally get this done. For my next question: Mr. Secretary, I take what we do for our personnel very seriously. Sometimes, I know you have stated right here today, our troops are ready and they will do anything if asked of them, but I think as leaders sometimes we need to protect them as well. I worry about the USS Ford, which has now been out there over 10 months. It's probably going to be well over 300 days by the time it gets back to the United States. I just saw a report today that it's going to be headed back here in a few days. You know, these people have gone pretty much all over the world; they crossed the equator multiple times. But I am interested—I'm sure that was not in the GFMOP when it was first assigned and when they were deployed. And so, what were some of the concerns that the Navy had when you made this modification, when it was brought forward to you that extending them out past 10 months is going to have impacts both on the troops and on the ship? What concerns did the Navy bring up for this? It's a very fair question, and I can tell you every week I stare at an orders book where I'm looking at troop movements or asset movements, and multiple times that included the Ford. Multiple times, the operational requirements, whether it was down in Southcom or up to Centcom, demanded additional assets in real time, which, through a tough decision-making process, led to an extension. In consultation with the Navy, Admiral Caudle and his staff fairly represented readiness tradeoffs and maintenance tradeoffs, which are a real thing going forward. What are the concerns? What tradeoffs are we making right here? I mean, really, we have three carriers in the area right now—how many do we really need? It's a great show of force, but do we really need three carriers there, especially when you have one ARG there and another one on the way? You know this is going to set back possibly deployments later on. It's going to have ramifications down the road, extending them for this much. And I am not only talking about logistics. We had service members that were kept away from their families for almost a year. How many missed the birth of a child before extending them another four months? We need to keep this in consideration when we make these modifications, and I hope these issues are being raised when you do these SDOB books, right? And people are actually making these decisions... The gentleman's time has expired. The gentleman yields back. The Chair recognizes the gentleman from Virginia, Mr. McGuire. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Thank you to our witnesses today. Secretary Hegseth, I really appreciate your return to the warrior ethos and the priorities of our military. Merit over performance is how you win, and it's how you save lives. Your love of our military comes out in everything you say. So we really support you, your team, and what you're doing. General Caine, your comments about being in the middle and being apolitical also save lives. I think all of the fellow veterans in this room, military guys, would agree that if somebody saves your life on the battlefield, you wouldn't care if they were male or female, black or white, Democrat, Republican, independent, whatever. We're all Americans, and we're all on the same team. When we put our differences aside and work as a team, we can turn the impossible into the possible. We can be outnumbered and still win. So I want to commend you guys for supporting our troops so they can best survive. Let's see, the border. Nobody talks about the border, Mr. Secretary, as you say, but it's incredible what you guys did in just a matter of weeks. The missions in Ukraine, Honduras... I don't know any other military in history that would have been able to successfully do something so complicated, but it speaks to the unity and the teamwork of the joint force and how well they work together. I've got a lot of questions; I'll try to go fast. On the $1.5 trillion budget: in that budget, I want to echo what Rep. Jackson said about US SOCOM. We are often deploy, deploy, deploy, deploy, and we seem to be doing more with less, but we don't complain and they won't be that voice. So for us in Congress, we want to be that voice. Let's just take a look at the budget and see if we can help them with that. Also, I want to talk to you guys about solid rocket motors. I know in this budget we've picked one particular company that we put a lot of money into, and I think it's a great company and they do a great job. But what is your plan to also have other companies come to the table to help us with a shortfall with solid rocket motors? You're right on SOCOM, and we will absolutely work with you and look at that. On solid rocket motors, it's such a critical component. You've got to go with your fastest runner to get caught up as quickly as you can, giving them incentives. But we've also learned on multiple domains that just one option means zero options when you're stuck, you know. One is zero, two is one, one is none. So we need multiple avenues on that approach, and we look forward to finding other companies that can do it as well. Thank you. General Caine, National Guard—I'm thinking about counter-drone with FIFA coming up, Super Bowls, and these things going on. Have you considered, and if you haven't, will you consider training National Guardsmen, sending them to counter-drone school so they can come back and train law enforcement so we can increase our ability to protect critical infrastructure? Sir, for sure they may already be doing that. Please let me take that one and come back to you. I know the Title 32 force, in advance of all the domestic things that are on the horizon here with America's 250, are looking at a range of capabilities. So, if that is okay, we'll get back to you on that. Thank you. On Ukraine, whoever could answer this—you know they have 60% enriched uranium. They say they have enough to build 11 bombs. Even at 60%, is that enough to make a dirty bomb? Is that a yes or no? Does anybody have an answer for that? I mean, I wouldn't want to state anything that might be classified, so I wouldn't talk about that in this setting. Fair enough. And by the way, in World War II, a congressman was in a closed briefing and learned that the reason the Japanese hadn't sunk any US submarines is they didn't know our submarines could go below 250 feet. So he went out to the media and told them that, and we started losing submarines and losing American sailors. So, a lesson for all of us in Congress: be careful how we... So thank you for considering that. I'll say this: if they had a nuclear bomb, would that kill hundreds of people potentially, thousands of people potentially, or millions of people potentially? History shows us what the lethal potential is of a nuclear weapon. And so you'd have to assume the higher end of that range, and given the radical nature of this regime and the likelihood of them to use it, it gives you a sense of the threat. Well, I think the threat is too great to wait, and I have to commend this President, President Trump, for having the fortitude, and the Secretary of War and the joint force for making sure we stop this threat for the world. Also, I'm a big supporter of the AH-64 Apache. I understand in operations like Absolute Resolve and everything else, it's been very effective in countering drones, so I'm glad that in the NDAA we're keeping that program going. And I just saw that you're going to keep the A-10 program going, and a good friend of mine said, "Long live the Warthog." But are there ways that we could bring some of the Warthogs back, and/or are there any other capabilities that can replace the Warthog? I don't think so. Is there anything that can do what the Warthog does? The gentleman's time has expired. The Chair now recognizes the gentleman from Illinois, Mr. Sorensen. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. To General Caine, on behalf of myself and my constituents, we thank you for your service and your leadership to our military men and women, to our service members, and to the veterans that I see in our committee today. Thank you for your support of our great country. Mr. Hegseth, I hear from your officials in our briefings here on the Hill, and because I live in an Army town, I now hear from neighbors at the grocery store who are worried about our munitions stockpiles that are now down to critical levels, and they sure don't want another forever war. So Mr. Secretary, first of all, talk to us about the need to renew munitions stockpiles and tell us what happens when munitions stockpiles become depleted. First of all, I would note that as you know, we give weekly briefings on munitions to this committee, so it's not... ...something that's withheld from any of the members here. We appreciate that. And—but it's also a topic that isn't something that should be publicly discussed, as far as any shortfalls that may exist. But I would assure you that there are not shortfalls that exist in our ability to execute the missions around the world. And the reason we're investing in the defense industrial base with $1.5 trillion is to make sure the greatest companies in the world, which have been falling behind for decades, are running faster than ever before to give us 2x, 3x, 4x of the critical munitions: your Patriots, your THAADs, your SM-3s, every other Standard Missile—you name it. And my neighbors work really hard to make sure that we can—it's jobs in America, it's what our warfighters need, we're focused on that. So, could you tell me why we're firing the people at Joint Munitions Command for no cause other than just telling them that they're overhired or they're surplus employees? I would have to look into the particular circumstances of that, but there have been places where we haven't been moving fast enough with the munitions. And when people don't move fast enough in the Department of War, we find someone else that will run fast. I mean, this is Joint Munitions Command. Your department says that you're prioritizing munitions, yet you cut 58% of Joint Munitions Command at Rock Island. But let me move on. My district is also... But munitions are not built by commands. Munitions are built by businesses. And we are very good at building commands and bureaucracies and slapping three and four-star generals in charge of things that don't move things any faster. So our job is to unleash the private sector to provide these things. But how are we going to get it done if we don't have Joint Munitions Command? But let me move on. We also have Army Sustainment Command in my district. Could you tell me why we're firing folks at Army Sustainment Command when now is the time that we should be giving our soldiers every piece of sustainment that we can give them? Because, as I've stated, the status quo at this department before the Trump administration was unacceptable, and changes had to be made. Do you think it's acceptable to fire veterans from jobs where they've been—I talked to my neighbors at the Arsenal, and they've worked there 20, 30 years on top of their service to this country, and they're doing an exemplary job. Great performers will always have a job at the Department of War, and when you reestablish merit, sometimes things happen. But we're grateful for all the vets that serve, and in fact, we've created a Patriot pipeline for service members to get into the defense industrial base manufacturing business to consider that as a second career. So we're leaning into vets and service members as the workforce of the future in our department. And this isn't me; I mean, they tell me that they feel you're taking a wrecking ball to this civilian workforce. But I want to go and follow up on Congressman Norcross's questions about why you terminated collective bargaining earlier this month. This affects hundreds of thousands of hardworking DoD employees, so many of whom are veterans who have honorably served our country. You testified that if you work hard, you do the work, you keep your job. But that's not what's happening. And you allege it is being done to maximize efficiency. Explain that. I should—yeah, I thank you for asking that question. I'd love to clarify. I fundamentally believe the Department of War should not be subject to collective bargaining, full stop. So people shouldn't be able to fight for good wages and being treated well? I think our department does a great job of evaluating and providing pay and benefits for folks across the spectrum. I would love to invite you to the Rock Island Arsenal so you can look these patriots in the eyes and tell them that. How does creating chaos for long-term civilian workers actually maximize efficiency? Do you have any comment on that? There are plenty of examples of shipyards and other manufacturing plants where there are restrictions that limit the hours, limit the ability of leaders to ensure we're providing—meeting the goals we're supposed to, and I don't think we should live to those constraints. Blah, blah, blah. Okay, okay. Well, you know what? I want to use the rest of my time to speak to the men and women of our armed forces. Please go forth with the integrity and the morality that you've carried before this administration took us down a path that has allies turning away and American citizens objecting. Do what's right, even if you're asked to do otherwise. Mr. Chairman, I yield back. The gentleman yields back. The Chair now recognizes the gentleman from Florida, Mr. Mills. Thank you so much, Mr. Chairman. Mr. Secretary, General Caine, and the Honorable—we'd love to just thank you for this opportunity for coming here and for continuing to push forward the warfighter ethos, for making sure that the realities, regardless of what's said in here, are the realities on the battlefield that we're getting day in and day out. I ask the Chairman for unanimous consent to enter a couple of documents into the record. Without objection, so ordered. I'll be entering into the record my DD-214 published in 2003, indicating my Bronze Star and other wartime medals and decorations. I'd also like to present the document provided by the Department of the Army in 2024 that also authorizes, vets, and verifies all medals and/or awards, as well as a document by Paul Savitzky, former Special Forces member, that annotates the two explosions that I was involved in in Baghdad. Without objection, so ordered. Mr. Secretary, as you know, you're pretty familiar with the ideas of slander, defamation, and attacks on character, so I apologize for having to take that moment to be able to go ahead and correct the record, but it was necessary. I would also note that my OMPF file that was FOIAed by the Department of the Army, or was FOIAed, was actually released by the Department of the Army the same day as yours was. So everything is sitting on the actual website itself if people aren't too lazy to look at it. Secretary Hegseth, thank you for your testimony and for the time that you have here today. As the Chairman noted, President Trump's incredible $1.5 trillion investment into our national defense is set to reverse the years of atrophy that our defense industrial base has faced over time, especially for small, innovative businesses in need of a solid demand signal that our War Department is ready to buy American. I witnessed this myself as I was with you in Merritt Island, Florida, as we went on the Arsenal tour. Unfortunately, I didn't get to do the cool things, like fly in a jet really quick and buzz around, while we were all out actually doing hard work here. But I wanted to just ask, Mr. Secretary, can you detail for us how the Department of War has eased the path for American small businesses to contract with the department? Well, Congressman, thank you for the question. That has been core to our mission: the recognition that we have a problem at the department, previously the Department of Defense and now the Department of War, and that problem is us. That problem is our bureaucracy, our process that is meant to block out new competitors and protect existing primes. There are great primes that provide great products, and we continue to support them. But to build an arsenal of freedom, you need more than just five or more than just six; you need an entire network of small, medium, and large companies contributing to the US manufacturing and supply base, delivering weapons ahead of schedule and under budget, that don't only have defense business but also commercial business. And when we need something, it can move quickly and adapt accordingly because there are multiple options. So our acquisitions transformation speech, memo, and directive we rolled out on that, the Arsenal of Freedom tour going out there with the hard hats and saying, "You are part of the workforce, you have the backs of our warriors"—just changing the entire dynamic to say you shouldn't have to sue the department in order to get a contract with the department. We're opening the doors with small business loans, whether it's SBIR, STTR, DIU, or OSC—the Office of Strategic Capital hasn't even been discussed today—$20 billion that gets leveraged into $200 billion so that companies can invest in ways they otherwise wouldn't have the capital to do, and it's not on our balance sheet. So we've got businessmen like the Deputy Secretary of War, who should be deemed a national asset for what he understands and how he works. We're opening the aperture of our department in historic ways, and I think our—I know our manufacturing base is going to see the benefit from it. Well, thank you so much. I also want to thank you for your due diligence in making sure that task force audit will come forward, and we will see the Department of War for the first time in 2028 with a clean audit—something that the previous administration was never able to do, and many before that. I believe it was 28 years, if I'm not mistaken, Mr. Secretary, since we've had a clean audit. That is right, and this is not a thing we just announced. We've been doing it for 15 months; Jay's been integrally involved. We just launched a joint task force audit, and we are going to pass an audit as a department by 2028 because the taxpayers deserve it. And this is why we're so honored to have you as our Secretary of War. And General Caine, we are honored to have you as well leading our forces. Mr. Secretary, with the last few seconds I have, from ships to bombs to bullets, our industrial base is in dire need of support, even as these defense markers are strengthened. As a former small business owner, I understand how bottlenecks can hold up good contracts and prevent moving at the speed which we need for our warfighters. Mr. Secretary, can you detail how the department has moved to promote diversity in our defense industrial base? I think we can detail it in 12 seconds. Well, if you will, can we submit that for the record? We'll take any update. Sure, every aspect is opening it up to provide as much running room for new entrants as possible. Thank you so much, Mr. Chairman, I appreciate it, and with that, I yield back. The gentleman's time has expired. The Chair now recognizes the gentleman from New Mexico, Mr. Vasquez. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you to our witnesses for being here today. You're here before us requesting $1.5 trillion for the Defense Department, the largest one-year increase since World War II. While I will recognize that your budget does include increases for pay and quality of life, these increases are outweighed by the rising cost of living and the department's ongoing failures on childcare, education, medical care, and other quality-of-life factors. Just like other families, the soaring cost of gas, groceries, housing, and childcare have left military families underwater. Yet the department continues to put other priorities first, like a $25 billion war of choice in Iran, and just lately, photo ops with Kid Rock, ahead of improving the lives of our troops and their families. It's deeply troubling to me that the department received a historic $1 trillion last year, including $9 billion under HR1 for improving troops' quality of life, but the New Mexican service members that I have talked to are still living in barracks that are at capacity, sending their kids to run-down schools, and lacking access to on-base childcare. Here's an example. During my most recent visit to White Sands Missile Range, I saw severely aging on-base infrastructure, specifically an elementary school that is in need of a complete rebuild. Secretary Hegseth, last fall you said that quality of life for our warriors is a critical component of reviving the warrior ethos. In a video you posted to social media last November, you said that the places where our warfighters reside need to be safe, comfortable, and clean. And in your opening statement today, you said quality of life is front and center in this budget. But when it comes to our troops' quality of life, I fear that this is mostly talk. Because I can tell you, the outdated barracks that I saw at White Sands Missile Range were anything but safe, comfortable, and clean, and I haven't heard from service members about any improvements since you've assumed this role. As Secretary of Defense, do you take any accountability for the continued quality-of-life concerns that New Mexico's military service members and families face today under your leadership? Well, I appreciate your attention to the matter of quality of life, which is incredibly important to us and it's reflected in this budget. I mean, historic amounts in barracks, in family housing, in family support like child development and youth programs—this budget surpasses anything you've seen from this department in quite some time, to include replacing or eliminating all poor and failing barracks. So we've seen those photos, and we responded to them with a barracks task force with a billion dollars initially, and now we have billions of dollars behind it to make sure they're safe, clean, and high-quality.
