Basically, a humanoid robot is designed to do the tasks that humans can do. It's estimated that almost two million manufacturing jobs in the US alone might go unfilled by 2033. But if humanoids are going to be part of closing that gap, they need to actually, well, move and problem-solve like a human, safely alongside people. And right now that’s only happening in a limited number of very controlled pilot tests run by some very big companies. For example, BMW is currently running a pilot and is reporting early data. Other big names testing these robots include Amazon and Hyundai. These trials are crucial when it comes to figuring out where humanoids work and where they don’t. They also provide, yes, data to fuel development while things like technology and regulation catch up. We started with pilots in humanoids about a year ago. My company is one of many testing out third-party humanoid robots in their warehouses. We have been working with robots for the last 10 years and we are at the forefront of piloting humanoids in our warehouse operations. It really is fascinating to see the new technology take foot in production in the warehouse environment. GXO has found some cases that humanoids might be useful for, like say, working in a freezer. We're able to deploy the humanoids in that environment where they can really work for an entire battery life, completing tasks and being directed by somebody who is outside of the freezer. This allows us to get work completed without putting our teammates in an uncomfortable situation working in that cold environment for a long time. But there's still a lot they're not great at. The humanoids so far are not perfect. They damage product, they drop product, and they may put product back in the wrong location. It is so important that our teammates are working alongside of them to do that. There is corrective action and audits done to make sure that the activity done by the humanoid is done accurately. Humanoids still face a number of engineering challenges that can create serious bottlenecks in a warehouse. They're slow, for one, they have limited battery life, and picking up a box or handling an object, things that we take for granted, require dexterity. It's something humanoids, which move using motors and actuators, have a hard time replicating. We do not see humanoids in the next couple years taking away many other tasks that our teammates perform. The tasks are too complex and too important from a quality perspective to go entirely to humanoids. And it's not really understood yet what the return on investment is going to be based on the finalized technologies that are coming out. In manufacturing you have a very fixed production process. You can shield off human beings and you can let the humanoid robot perform. While in a hospital setting, it's a semi-structured environment. There are protocols in how processes should be done and how patients should be cared for, but still there are so many uncertainties that can arise. In fact, it's about life and death type of situations and the costs of making a mistake are very, very high. So will humanoids one day overcome their technical challenges, recoup investments, and become indispensable companions? Folding our laundry, watering our plants, and building our cars? Or just be a novelty giving way to robots of other shapes and sizes? I've been in this field for 40 years and it is fascinating to me how much it has had a resurgence again and again over the years. I’m thrilled about that. I'm excited because I feel that this field's got many years of questions and research that need to be done. So in some sense I don't want to sound discouraging. I'm very actually optimistic about the future, but I think it's important again to just be realistic about the timeframe. In the meantime, I'll leave you with this: A humanoid robot, waving to you from a balcony, as a drone pulls slowly and dramatically away. That feels like a decent way to end. For now.

Inside Wealth: Thiel Capital Managing Director Jack Selby on investment opportunities

CNBC Television(3.4M)·24:50·3.1K views·2026-04-29
Jack SelbyManaging Director, Thiel Capital Robert FrankWealth Editor, CNBC
TL;DRWhile AI is a revolutionary technology, it is forming a massive, capital-intensive bubble where potential losses could be orders of magnitude larger than the dot-com era due to high infrastructure costs and underpriced Middle Eastern geopolitical risks.
TL;DR While AI is a revolutionary technology, it is forming a massive, capital-intensive bubble where potential losses could be orders of magnitude larger than the dot-com era due to high infrastructure costs and underpriced Middle Eastern geopolitical risks. Scale of...

TL;DR

While AI is a revolutionary technology, it is forming a massive, capital-intensive bubble where potential losses could be orders of magnitude larger than the dot-com era due to high infrastructure costs and underpriced Middle Eastern geopolitical risks.

The AI Bubble and Capital Intensity

  • Scale of Potential Losses: While AI is a transformative technology, Selby warns it is also an "exceptional bubble." Unlike the dot-com era where companies like Webvan failed with relatively small losses, the AI bust will likely involve "two or three more zeros" in terms of incinerated wealth.
  • CapEx Intensity: The primary risk factor is the physical requirement for energy, chips, and data centers.
      • Depreciation Risk: Markets currently calculate a six-year depreciation curve for AI chips; Selby estimates the actual curve is closer to three years due to high-intensity usage.
      • Infrastructure Fragility: If the "math" on data center ROI fails, it could wipe out tens or hundreds of billions in capital expenditures.
  • Survivorship: While publicly traded giants (Google, Amazon) are safe from bankruptcy, Selby anticipates a "browser war" style culling among private AI firms. He predicts OpenAI will survive, though potentially as part of Microsoft.

Geopolitical Risks and the Middle East

  • Underestimated Volatility: Selby argues that global markets have failed to price in the risk of prolonged conflict in the Middle East.
  • Infrastructure Spend: Approximately 25% of all projected AI infrastructure spend over the next five years is sourced from the Middle East.
      • Force Majeure: Ongoing conflict could lead to the cancellation or postponement of massive data center projects.
      • Secondary Market Impact: Highly leveraged companies involved in these projects (citing Oracle as an example of a high debt-ratio firm) could face significant credit and margin pressure if these contracts are stalled.

The "Culling" of Venture Capital

  • Performance Crisis: Selby estimates that two-thirds of venture capital firms currently have "no right to be in business."
      • Lack of DPI: Investors (LPs) are increasingly frustrated by the lack of "Distributed to Paid-In Capital" (actual cash returns).
      • Slow Failure: Selby notes the irony that VC managers advise startups to "fail fast" while they themselves "fail as slowly as humanly possible" to continue collecting fees.
  • Family Office "Tourist" Capital: Frustrated by poor VC returns, many family offices have moved toward direct investing.
      • The "Cocktail Party" Factor: Selby critiques family offices that invest in opaque Late-stage SPVs (Special Purpose Vehicles) for social status rather than conviction.
      • Public vs. Private: He argues that for most investors, buying liquid stocks like Google is a superior way to "scratch the AI itch" compared to high-fee, low-information private SPVs.

Regional Arbitrage and Founder Talent

  • The Coastal Bubble: Currently, over 90% of VC investment is concentrated in California, New York, and Massachusetts—a record high driven by the AI boom.
  • The Arizona Strategy: Through his fund Copper Sky, Selby targets the "other 47 states" to find companies at "far, far less expensive" valuations.
      • Example: He cites Carvana as proof that billion-dollar tech companies can thrive in the Southwest rather than just Palo Alto or Cambridge.
  • The "Talent Scout" Model: Reflecting on Peter Thiel’s success, Selby describes the best VCs as talent scouts (akin to Hollywood agents) who look for "orthogonal talent" and "that twinkle in the eye" rather than specific sectors.

Macroeconomic Outlook: The "K-Shaped" Economy

  • Inflation Persistence: Selby believes inflation is "here to stay" and will widen the existing wealth gap.
  • The Four Seasons vs. Holiday Inn: In an inflationary environment, businesses serving the top of the "K" (wealthy consumers) have inelastic demand and high pricing power, whereas mid-tier businesses (like Holiday Inn) struggle with rising input costs they cannot pass on.
  • AI and Labor: He views AI as a "J-curve"—painful in the short term as it disrupts middle-class jobs, with the potential for long-term recovery that remains uncertain.

***

"The AI bubble when it goes bust will be at least one more zero, probably two, and three more zeros at times of money that literally goes to zero... because when the losers lose, they will wipe out all of that CapEx and investment." — Jack Selby
"Venture capital managers tell their poorly performing companies to fail fast, but they themselves... fail as slowly as humanly possible. There’s a massive disconnect between the two." — Jack Selby
"If you have an insatiable AI itch that you have to scratch, why wouldn't you just buy Google? Why would you sign up for a ride in an opaque SPV with fees on fees on fees with no information rights?" — Jack Selby
He was one of the founders of PayPal. He runs Copper Sky, the Arizona VC fund, and he helps run Thiel Capital, the family office of tech billionaire Peter Thiel. Jack Selby is a triple threat of investing. I sat down with him at Riverwood Capital's LatAm Tech Forum in Miami. Jack, thanks so much for joining us today. So, you said recently that AI had the potential of being the largest bubble of private tech investing ever. We could see hundreds of billions of dollars of tech wealth or investment wealth incinerated. Do you still think that, and why? Thanks for having me, Robert. I think AI is probably the most revolutionary technology that we have seen in our lifetime, so I just want to disclaim that up front. And that's a lot, because you were an early investor in Facebook and you were one of the guys who helped start PayPal. You've been on the forefront of a lot of internet revolutions. You think this is bigger than any of that? That's right. I'm not a Luddite. AI is a real thing. It's very positive, but it can also be two things at the same time. It can be a revolutionary technology and also it can be an exceptional bubble. And I think that's what's going on. So, there'll be extreme winners in the AI movement that's going on now and there'll also be some real losers. Those losers will be orders of magnitude larger than any of the losers that we've seen before. For example, you were referencing our dot-com era experience with PayPal. There were Webvan, there were other companies that went bust. The AI bubble when it goes bust will be at least one more zero, probably two and three more zeros, at times of money that literally goes to zero. Why is that? Why do you think that many of these companies won't make it? It's a function of the fact that AI is so CapEx intensive. So, if you're building Pets.com, you don't need a lot of CapEx to build that business. Whereas today, you need energy, you need data centers, and you need chips. It's much more capital intensive, so when the winners win, they'll win, but when the losers lose, they will wipe out all of that CapEx and investment. That's tens, if not hundreds of billions of dollars. But unlike the dot-com era, we do have a lot of AI startups, but most of the capital is going toward the big five, six, or seven, whatever you want to call them. Are you saying that some of those companies—we're talking about Anthropic, we're talking about OpenAI, ChatGPT, we're talking about Amazon, we're talking about Alphabet, we're talking about these huge compute companies—I mean, you're not saying any of those are going to fail, right? None of the publicly traded companies are obviously going to go bust. Google's not going bust anytime soon. But in terms of some of the private companies, the best analogy I can give you, and it's far from perfect, is to think back to the dot-com era. Not to date myself again, but think about all the different browser companies that started up back then. So, you had Infoseek and you had Ask Jeeves and you had all these different companies and how many of those actually survived? There was a little company called Google that came along and leapfrogged a company that had been kind of the mainstay and the incumbent, which was Yahoo. So, Yahoo still exists today, but Google is what it is today and all those others are gone. So what if that is analogous? What are the equivalents of those companies that might be gone today? Well, do you think there's any risk that OpenAI doesn't make it, doesn't become one of the big survivors and winners of this game? I think OpenAI will absolutely survive. Now, whether they get tucked back into Microsoft at some point down the road, that's an open question. And what about on the chip space? We've seen Nvidia, we've seen a lot of the chip companies, the sort of "picks and shovel" thesis about AI. Do you think that's valid? Do you think that will continue for a while, or do you think that phase of the revolution is going to end at some point, and when? So it's another answer where there's a bit of a yes or no. I think the railroad analogy of how we're laying tracks for the future and it's value-add no matter what—I don't think that analogy works very well. You're building these huge data centers all over the place and if there is a bump in the road, there is a problem. A lot of these data centers are filled with chips that are running 24/7, 365 as if they're in sixth gear if they're an automobile. They're running so quickly that one of the questions that's come up a lot in the financial press is: what is the depreciation curve on these chips? Previously, they'd estimated that it was six years on average. It's probably somewhat closer to half of that. And so if these chips are depreciating twice as fast as what people are otherwise calculating, then what do you do? So you have to buy new chips or your chips are half as valuable, and that means that some of these data centers may not survive, or at least the math may not be as good as what it was originally thought. So you think the depreciation could be more like three years instead of six? I think the depreciation curve is far, far, far more... it's a faster depreciation curve than what's being depicted. Right. Let's talk about SpaceX, because you were an early investor in SpaceX. I believe you sold out of most or all your position in 2022. They're about to come public with a valuation they're talking about of certainly more than one and a quarter trillion dollars. Do you regret selling? What do you think of that as a company right now? Well, listen, I was extremely lucky and fortunate to get into SpaceX early. So I have absolutely nothing to complain about. You did well, even selling in 2022, you did well. I have nothing to complain about. Peter got a lot of flak when he sold out of Facebook back in, I think, 2010, but he was literally Mark Zuckerberg's first check. I wasn't Elon's first check into SpaceX, but everything was fine. So don't cry for me, certainly. SpaceX is truly a once-in-a-lifetime type company. We may not see another one like it for the rest of our lives. It's exceptional. So I'm just really excited to see what happens when it goes public. Do you think, would you invest in it now, and why not? Do you think the valuation is just... you're not going to get that early gain that you got and so therefore you're moving on to the next company? Or do you think it's still a good investment for retail investors? So, my comment would be that I don't have anything specific to say about SpaceX because I just don't look at late-stage private companies as an investor. That's not what I do. So, it's no offense to SpaceX; it very well could be an amazing investment, but it's just not what I do for a living. Great. And you do a lot of things for a living right now. You're a managing director of Thiel Capital, which is Peter Thiel's family office. You also are the GP of Copper Sky and you're raising a new fund there. Let's talk about Peter Thiel. I know that that's not your primary responsibility; there are a lot of other people that manage that fund for him and that work with him. But broadly speaking, if we look at what he's been investing in with his own private capital, it seems like he likes biotech, he likes drone companies—particularly those in Europe—he's investing a lot in those, and he seems to like, just broadly as does everybody, AI. So, as a guy who knows Peter, what do you think he'd like to do or see as exciting right now as personal investments? Sure. So, I'm not a spokesman for Peter, so I have to disclaim that up front. And I've had the good fortune of working with Peter now for 28 years. So, I know him pretty well. I think one thing that Peter is really, really good at, and it's the reason why Founders Fund is named Founders Fund, is that he is really good at identifying rockstar young entrepreneurs. And he's sector-agnostic about that. So, whether it's drones or AI or any of the different sectors you just talked about, I think there's a degree to which Peter thinks about things from an agnostic perspective and he's just looking for true orthogonal talent. How, what does he look for? What do you see as the common qualities that he tends to like when he finds a founder? He's like, "I don't care what this guy does, this is a founder I want to invest in." Well, again, not speaking for Peter, but I think one of the aspects of venture capital is that you can skin the cat many different ways. I think one of the ways though that Peter's been very good at with the Founders Fund team in particular is—and the reason why the firm is named such is—that the job is almost like you're a talent scout. You work for CAA and you sit across from aspiring, amazing young entrepreneurs all day long and you put in your 10,000 hours. If you do that, you develop almost like an innate sense of who actually has that twinkle in their eye to become the next Elon and who doesn't. And if you do enough of those reps, you get that sense. I think Peter has as good of a sense arguably as anyone in the world. So you think it's not looking at, let's say, "this guy has started and failed three times, I like to find founders who've failed three times," or "this guy has done X." You think it's more instinctual when he meets somebody, that you just know when you meet them? That's right. I was doing an event a couple weeks ago and a fellow panelist made a comment that I strongly disagreed with. His comment was basically that AI is going to replace the job of the venture capitalist. And so my pushback isn't trying to be defensive—everyone's entitled to their own opinion—but I think, when is AI going to paint a Picasso? When is AI going to write an Oscar-worthy script? I would argue that's a long time in the future. So similarly, I think what venture capital does, just like any other business, is that you sit across from other human beings and you make an assessment: do you want to do business with that person? Venture capital, I know it has a sexy reputation, but it's no different than any other business in that regard. I want to ask you what you're investing in now personally. You've got a second fund at Copper Sky; the first one was I think about $100 million, the second one is $300 million. What's the focus of that second fund? The first one was quite local in terms of you were going to be in Arizona investing locally. What's the second fund and where do you see the biggest opportunities right now for that fund? So, the first fund we focused on the Southwest area of the United States, Arizona in particular, where I've lived since our PayPal days. And so the idea is, one of the biggest phenomena that's going on in venture capital today is that investment last year—the numbers aren't in yet—but probably 90% plus of all venture capital investment went to California, New York, and Massachusetts. That is an all-time high. The previous high was in 2021 and it was just above 60%. So it's a record, record high and it's highly, highly correlated to the AI phenomenon. So the good news, glass half full, is if you get outside of those three states and go to the other 47 states, the deals, the investment opportunities are far, far, far less expensive. And that's what we do. We're basically an arbitrage non-coastal, finding great deals that are at reasonable prices, coach them up, and then if they want to go to the coast, we can help them do that given our background. So, it's not that you just dislike Democratic blue states, because those are all Democratic blue states. I love Democratic blue states; I hate expensive deals. But the best companies in AI might just be in California, New York, and Massachusetts. Are you missing out because geographically you're drawing what in a digital age is sort of an arbitrary line? Sure. So, is the next OpenAI or Anthropic going to come out of Phoenix? Probably not. But can you tell me what the largest tech company is that's come out of Arizona? I won't put you on the spot; it's Carvana. Carvana is a perfectly reasonable technology company worth billions and billions of dollars that has come out of Arizona. And it could come out of Kansas and it could come out of Minnesota. So, focusing on these companies that are not necessarily going to be domain-specific to world-class institutions like Stanford and Palo Alto or MIT and Cambridge, you can still build real companies in these other interior middle markets. Now, in your role with Thiel Capital as well as somebody who's investing your own capital, you're very close to the family office community and the private capital community. How do you feel family offices right now are reacting to what's happening in the Middle East? What are some themes you've heard? Every family office is different, we know that, but broadly speaking, what's the vibe right now among family offices? Yeah, it's interesting, I was actually there when the war broke out. And so this question hits home probably more closely than it would for most. Where, where were you? I was in the UAE whenever it started. And so it's a very real situation. How did you get out? It was a very complicated, poor man's James Bond escape for me to get out, but I got out. Everything is fine. I have a ton of friends there. It's a place that I've been going to since PayPal days. And so it's surprising to me that markets don't seem to grasp that this is a very real situation. It's a very volatile situation and I hope and I pray that it goes back to some semblance of normalcy soon. But it seems to me that markets are underpricing this volatility and the risk, unfortunately, that it may extend and prolong for a good, good period of time. What makes you think that? Because typically, geopolitical events like the war in Ukraine or the previous war in Iraq, the markets take an initial dip and then we find that over time, it could be six months, 12 months, could be three years, they see through it. And that's what people are saying about this market, coupled with the fact that this market is very tech-dominated, which is unaffected by the Middle East. So why do you think they're underestimating the risk if history tells us these aren't real risks to stock markets? So, markets are usually pretty smart, and I think in this case they still have some catching up to do. Let me give you a specific point. So, I was doing some research on this, and roughly a quarter of AI infrastructure spend from today for the next five years will be coming from the Middle East. Through the sovereign wealth funds and through the capital from all the entities related in the region. A quarter. A quarter. A quarter, so that's the starting point. And then my second point is that roughly half of that spend would be put in the ground, proposed to be put in the ground for data centers in the Middle East itself, and the other half would go to the US and elsewhere. So what would happen, God forbid, if this situation in the Middle East continues, prolongs, and what happens if it gets worse? So, there've already been examples of force majeure of contracts being cancelled throughout that region because of different facilities being hit. So what happens if some of those facilities that get hit start to be data centers? What happens if those—which we've already seen a couple data centers, we've already seen Amazon get hit and you've had a couple others as well. So if some of these data centers start getting postponed or cancelled because of force majeure, what is the impact on the market? And then think about, what is the second-order effect of these financial investments that are undoubtedly tied legally to these CapEx investments? What happens if those get postponed or force majeure cancelled? What is the impact on the market then? Do you think that's bigger than DeepSeek? Do you think that's bigger than the SaaSocalypse? Some of that half of that quarter capital is in the Middle East, the other half everywhere around the world. You think that gush of cash that we've seen from the Middle East could slow? Yeah, the headline would be I think markets have under-appreciated how important the Middle East region is for CapEx spending as it relates to AI and AI infrastructure. And if the Middle East starts taking some of these projects offline or cancelling some of these projects, the impact on the market could be much, much, much larger than what is currently priced in. Where do you think that would first show up? What stocks or what parts of the economy would that show up in? Well, kind of like with private credit, this is not what I do for a living, but just as an amateur I would suggest, you know, Oracle has a very high debt ratio and you have all this kind of circular trading, or what's been alleged as circular trading. So, if you have highly indebted companies that all of a sudden you have projects that are getting postponed or cancelled, their margin for error in terms of their ability to make their payments and keep their credit rating would presumably be relatively slight or thin. And so I think there's a degree of tension and fragility there that if, again, some of these contracts start getting postponed or cancelled, there could be real market impact. And you think the market, whether or not that happens, you don't think the market has priced in that risk? I do not. I think the market is so fixated on all the trees growing in the sky, Goldilocks-type scenarios with AI, which again I'm not a Luddite and I appreciate that optimism as a venture capitalist, but I don't think the risk and the downside is sufficiently priced in. So let's go back to the family offices for a while because again, you know that space. You're a manager in that space, you raise money in that space. Family offices have grown in size and number exponentially over the past 5-10 years. What do you think is the biggest problem that family offices face right now? So I think, in my world, I think family offices and most allocators are very frustrated with venture capital, with venture capitalists. Because we have done as an asset category a poor job of returning our client's money to them in the form of DPI. And so they are understandably very, very frustrated with people like ourselves because they've been waiting on getting their money back for a very long time. And so because of that, a lot of them have said, "Okay, well, I'm going to start doing this job myself." Yes, direct investing is bigger and bigger. And I'm going to become a direct investor. And I think it's unfair when people in my seat criticize them for kind of becoming tourist capital by going direct. Because why shouldn't they? Because they're so frustrated with people like ourselves to have not been returning our capital. So why shouldn't they try themselves, because they couldn't do any worse than a lot of what we've been doing in terms of making investments, not giving money back, having marks on paper, but a lot of their VC fund managers that do it for a living have been doing the same thing. So I think they get a bit of a bad rap in that regard. But I also think there are a lot of VC managers that are frankly not very good. And there needs to be kind of a Darwinian culling of the herd, and that culling has taken in my opinion much, much longer than I would have thought. There's an irony in the sense that VC managers tell their poorly performing companies to fail fast, but they themselves, when they're not performing, fail as slowly as humanly possible. That's true. That's true. And there's a massive disconnect between the two. So I do think there's justifiably a frustration with our family office friends with their venture capitalist investments. What percentage of venture capital firms do you think have no right to be in business? Two-thirds. Two-thirds. Two-thirds? Two-thirds. Well, if you think about it, after the financial crisis, interest rates were zero for 13 plus years and private tech investors, family offices included, associated tech with growth, with yield. So they assumed that if they put more money into venture capital—which by the way is a very, very small asset class. So private equity is a massive asset class, venture capital is a very small subset, and good venture capital, which is the only part you want to invest in, is tiny. But all this money got stuffed into it and guess what? When you stuff a ton of money into an asset class that otherwise cannot sustain it, returns go down. And that's a bad combination and that's where we are, but it's just taken so long for this cathartic process, this culling of the herd, to take place. And you mentioned that because of that performance, family offices have gone to direct investing themselves in a huge way. How have they done? How are family offices at direct, at being direct investors? So, in non-fairness to them, it takes you a very long time to learn that you're not a very good direct private investor. So there's a reason why people do it full-time for a living, and so you're investing... what is crazy to me is that so many of these family offices are investing in these SPV products that are totally opaque, they're extended duration, you have no information rights, and you're basically just taking a punt. And it kind of, it's symbolic in a way of this world that we live in where you have all these betting markets that are out there now. This is essentially in my mind just another version of that—taking a punt—which by the way, there's nothing wrong with that, but it's not the same thing as investing. Investing is about conviction and long-term and not flipping a coin, and these late-stage tech companies through these SPVs in my opinion are much more about trading than they are long-term investing. I mean, what we hear from family offices is that they do direct investing when the principal has an expertise in that field. They may have cashed out of their company 20 years ago, but they still know food packaging or trucking or hotels, whatever it might be, so therefore they're experts. Or number two, they have a good friend who's also in that company and it's a club deal and they trust those people. I'm not sure about that. I think when these fancy people go to their cocktail parties in Manhattan, they have to have something interesting to talk about. And this, and all of their friends are talking about some version of this, so they have to have something to add to the conversation, so therefore they do the same thing. I think. You really, you really think it's sort of... that's part of why I'm not a... this is not a joke. I guarantee you there's a degree to which what this is the dynamic that's going on. Because the Greek shipping magnate that lives in Manhattan knows nothing about rocketry. He knows a lot about ships. So why is he investing in SpaceX? Because his buddy is an expert on rocket ships? Probably not; he just wants to have something fun to talk about at the fancy cocktail party where he meets all of his buddies on Friday night. Whereas if they just say, "Look, I invest in Nvidia stock," anybody can do that—that's not special, that's not cool. That's a good point, because if you have an insatiable AI itch that you have to scratch, why wouldn't you just buy Google? Why would you sign up for a ride in an opaque SPV with fees on fees on fees with no information rights? Versus you literally just click a button and you're into Google, and if you want to get out of Google, you click another button. You can be in and out in milliseconds. So the peer pressure, for lack of a better phrase, I think—I know it sounds like a joke, but I honestly don't think—I think there's an animal spirits component to it where people want to be able to talk as if they're into this shiny new toy, which are these late-stage tech companies. I want to ask you about inflation, because earlier this year when I was talking to a lot of family offices at the conferences, there was a lot more money being put toward inflation bets. In other words, real estate, infrastructure—the idea, I think it was pegged more to tariffs obviously, we have a whole other catalyst now, but the idea was the family offices seemed to believe that inflation was going to stick around longer and remain elevated. And that wasn't the prevailing wisdom on Wall Street, if you look at yields were starting to come down, the Fed was expected to cut, to have a lot of interest rate cuts this year. Do you think that thesis in the family office world is still true? And what, and what is your view of inflation this year and next year? So I think, I think we've all come around to the idea that inflation unfortunately is definitely not transitory. It's here to stay. And I think it absolutely plays into kind of the K-economy dynamic. So, if you're a business that's selling into the top part of the K, you have exceptional pricing power. And conversely, if your business is predicated on inputs that are selling in or related to the bottom of the K, it's very problematic. So in other words, if I had to be very succinct, it's very good to be an owner and operator of the Four Seasons and far, far less so of the Holiday Inn. Because those inflationary pressures, you can only price your hotel room at the Holiday Inn so high, versus with the Four Seasons you have almost inelastic demand to raise your prices for that hotel room. And so you can apply that across all industries, whether you're in the Four Seasons business or the Holiday Inn business. Right, and even beyond the consumer economy, what other areas do you think investors might look toward as a hedge against inflation? Yeah, it's difficult. So if you were long the selling into the top of the K because that demand curve—because I think the wealth gap that already exists, because we're in kind of later innings of capitalism—that wealth gap I think is just getting wider. It's getting more broad. Do you think AI is going to make that more extreme? Well, that's a question I ask literally all the time. It's a rhetorical question, so I think yes. And so, and we live at a time where our friends in DC, you could maybe not too controversially argue, are not doing a great job. Or we in a live in a very divided country, and so the middle class that was such an important component of our society—if they are not being benefited by our friends in DC and AI is making this wealth gap wider, that is very problematic for all of us. And you see that continuing that K-shape, perhaps even accelerated by AI? I think so, and I think the doomers on the AI front—I think that's very overdone. I think again... you don't think there's going to be massive catastrophic job loss. I think again there's a degree to which it's both. And so there's a degree I believe where AI, there's a J-curve and we're going down the J-curve, so it's a bit painful. So we're going through this adjustment curve and then we start going up on the J bit of the curve, then I think it will get quite a bit better. But in this transitional phase, it can feel one way directionally on the downside which is painful, but I think once we start to come back up, I think that will help compensate for it. But in the meantime, it can be difficult. Great. Jack, thanks so much for joining us today. Thank you for having me. Thank you.

LIVE: Fed Chairman Jerome Powell Holds Press Briefing After Warsh Advances Out Of Committee

Forbes Breaking News(5.5M)·72:26·3.9K views·2026-04-29
Jerome PowellChairman, Federal Reserve Board
TL;DR> "These legal actions by the administration are unprecedented in our 113-year history...
TL;DR: The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) held interest rates steady at 3.5%–3.75% amid elevated inflation and geopolitical uncertainty, while outgoing Chair Jerome Powell confirmed he will remain on the Board as a Governor to safeguard the Fed’s independence against...

TL;DR: The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) held interest rates steady at 3.5%–3.75% amid elevated inflation and geopolitical uncertainty, while outgoing Chair Jerome Powell confirmed he will remain on the Board as a Governor to safeguard the Fed’s independence against unprecedented "legal assaults" and political pressure.

Monetary Policy and Rate Decision

  • Interest Rate Hold: The committee maintained the target range for the federal funds rate at 3-1/2 to 3-3/4 percent.
      • Policy Stance: Powell characterized the current stance as "appropriate," sitting at the high end of neutral or "mildly restrictive."
  • Evolving Guidance: There was a "vigorous discussion" regarding the Fed's "easing bias" (the inclination toward cutting rates).
      • Internal Dissent: Three members dissented, favoring a move to a "neutral" stance (where a hike is as likely as a cut) due to stalled progress on inflation.
      • Decision to Wait: Powell opted to maintain current language, arguing there is no "rush" to change guidance given the high degree of uncertainty over the next 60 days.
  • Meeting-by-Meeting Basis: Future adjustments will depend on incoming data; notably, the Fed wants to see the "back side" of the current energy shock before considering rate cuts.

Inflation and External Shocks

  • Current Data: Total PCE inflation rose to 3.5% (12 months ending in March), while Core PCE (excluding food and energy) stands at 3.2%.
  • Geopolitical Pressure: The conflict in the Middle East has pushed Brent crude toward $120 a barrel.
      • Oil Impact: Powell noted that while the Fed typically "looks through" energy shocks, the duration of the current corridor closures remains "unknowable" and may bleed into core inflation (e.g., airfares).
  • Tariff Pass-Through: Powell attributed a portion of core inflation to recent tariffs, which he views as "one-time price increases." The Fed expects this impact to subside within the next two quarters.

Economic Outlook and Labor Market

  • Economic Resilience: The U.S. economy continues to expand at a "solid pace" (2% or better), driven by resilient consumer spending and "insatiable demand" for business investment in data centers.
  • Labor Market Dynamics: The unemployment rate remains low at 4.3%, but other indicators show a softening trend.
      • Hiring Stagnation: Job gains are low, and there is effectively "no new net job creation."
      • Labor Supply: Slower job growth is partially attributed to a decline in labor force growth due to lower immigration and participation rates.
  • Housing Sector: This remains the primary area of economic weakness in contrast to the broader expansion.

Leadership Transition and Fed Independence

  • Powell’s New Role: Following the end of his term as Chair on May 15, Powell will remain on the Board as a Governor for an undetermined period. He intends to keep a "low profile" to avoid being a "shadow chair" to successor Kevin Warsh.
  • Protecting the Institution: Powell explicitly stated his decision to stay is a response to "unprecedented" legal and political attacks from the administration.
      • Legal Finality: He will not leave the Board until the DOJ criminal investigation is "well and truly over" with full transparency and finality.
  • Transition to Warsh: Powell congratulated Kevin Warsh on advancing out of the Senate Banking Committee and expressed confidence in Warsh's ability to maintain Fed independence and build committee consensus.
"These legal actions by the administration are unprecedented in our 113-year history... I worry that these attacks are battering the institution and putting at risk the thing that really matters to the public, which is the ability to conduct monetary policy without taking into consideration political factors." — Jerome Powell
"We think our policy stance is in a good place for us to hold and wait developments... [The Fed is] an institution full of human beings who work super hard to get things right for the benefit of the public. We're all human, don't expect perfection, but do expect us to make decisions without political considerations." — Jerome Powell
"I’ve said that I will not leave the Board until this investigation is well and truly over, with transparency and finality, and I stand by that." — Jerome Powell
Good afternoon. My colleagues and I remain squarely focused on achieving our dual mandate goals of maximum employment and stable prices for the benefit of the American people. The U.S. economy has been expanding at a solid pace. While job gains have remained low, the unemployment rate has been little changed in recent months. Inflation has moved up and is elevated, in part reflecting the recent increase in global energy prices. Today, the FOMC decided to leave our policy rate unchanged. We see the current stance of monetary policy as appropriate to promote progress toward our maximum employment and 2 percent inflation goals. Developments in the Middle East are contributing to a high level of uncertainty about the economic outlook, and we will remain attentive to risks to both sides of our dual mandate. I'll have more to say about monetary policy after briefly reviewing economic developments. Recent indicators suggest that economic activity has been expanding at a solid pace. Consumer spending has been resilient, and business fixed investment has continued to expand at a brisk pace. In contrast, activity in the housing sector has remained weak. In the labor market, the unemployment rate was 4.3 percent in March and has changed little in recent months. Job gains have remained low. A good part of the slowing in the pace of job growth over the past year reflects a decline in the growth of the labor force due to lower immigration and labor force participation, though labor demand has clearly softened as well. Other indicators, including job openings, layoffs, hiring, and nominal wage growth, generally show little change in recent months. Inflation has moved up recently and is elevated relative to our 2 percent longer-run goal. Estimates based on the Consumer Price Index and other data indicate that total PCE prices rose 3.5 percent over the 12 months ending in March. This was boosted by the significant rise in global oil prices that has resulted from the conflict in the Middle East. Excluding the volatile food and energy categories, core PCE prices rose 3.2 percent over the 12 months ending in March. This relatively high rate largely reflects the effects of tariffs on prices in the goods sector. Near-term measures of inflation expectations have risen this year, likely because of the substantial rise in oil prices. Most measures of longer-term expectations remain consistent with our 2 percent inflation goal. Our monetary policy actions are guided by our dual mandate to promote maximum employment and stable prices for the American people. At today's meeting, the committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 3-1/2 to 3-3/4 percent. The economic outlook remains highly uncertain, and the conflict in the Middle East has added to this uncertainty. In the near term, higher energy prices will push up overall inflation. Beyond that, the scope and duration of potential effects on the economy remain unclear, as does the future course of the conflict itself. We will continue to monitor the risks to both sides of our dual mandate. We are well positioned to determine the extent and timing of additional adjustments to our policy rate based on the incoming data, the evolving outlook, and the balance of risks. Monetary policy is not on a preset course, and we will make our decisions on a meeting-by-meeting basis. This is my last press conference as Chair, and I will close with a few thoughts. First, I want to congratulate Kevin Warsh on his advancement out of the Senate Banking Committee this morning. This is an important step forward, and I wish him well as that process continues. The Federal Reserve exists for one fundamental purpose: to foster the economic conditions in which American families and businesses can thrive—stable prices, a strong job market, and a financial system they can depend on. Every decision we make, whether about interest rates or regulatory and supervisory matters or other issues, is made in service of that purpose. Our decisions reflect the collective judgment of the Board of Governors and the Federal Open Market Committee, colleagues who demonstrate analytical rigor, principled judgment, and a genuine commitment to the public interest. Our collaborative and deliberative process has long reflected a shared commitment to finding common ground in service to our mission. This institution is resilient, capable, and staffed by professionals of extraordinary talent and exceptional dedication. It has been a privilege to serve alongside so many great public servants at the Board of Governors and around the Federal Reserve System. The Fed's work is only as effective as the public's understanding of it, and you, the press, are essential to keeping the public informed about what we do and why. The people we serve benefit from your careful reporting. I welcomed the announcement last Friday by the U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia that she had closed the criminal investigation. She also noted, however, that she would not hesitate to restart the investigation. Over the weekend, the Department of Justice provided assurances that they will not reopen the investigation unless there's a criminal referral from the Fed's Inspector General. And absent such a referral, if they do appeal the recent court decision, they would not seek as part of that appeal to restart the investigation or send new subpoenas. I've said that I will not leave the Board until this investigation is well and truly over, with transparency and finality, and I stand by that. I'm encouraged by recent developments, and I'm watching the remaining steps in this process carefully. My decisions on these matters will continue to be guided entirely by what I believe is in the best interest of the institution and the people we serve. After my term as Chair ends on May 15, I will continue to serve as a Governor for a period of time to be determined. I plan to keep a low profile as a Governor. There's only ever one Chair of the Federal Reserve Board. When Kevin Warsh is confirmed and sworn in, he will be that Chair. Once sworn in as Board Chair, his new colleagues will elect him to chair the FOMC as well. As I regularly point out from this podium, our success in delivering our goals matters for all Americans. I'm confident that the Fed will continue to do its work with objectivity, integrity, and a deep commitment to serve the American people. Thank you, and I look forward to your questions. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Appreciate the kind words about the press, often doesn't come from the podium in different places, but appreciate that. Can you talk about what has gone into your decision to remain on the Board, what kind of criteria are you weighing and how long might you stay? Thank you. Sure. So, you know, my concern is really about the series of legal attacks on the Fed, which threaten our ability to conduct monetary policy without considering political factors. And I want to note here, this has nothing whatever to do with verbal criticism by elected officials. I've never suggested that such verbal criticism is a problem, and neither has anyone else here. But these legal actions by the administration are unprecedented in our 113-year history, and there are ongoing threats of additional such actions. I worry that these attacks are battering the institution and putting at risk the thing that really matters to the public, which is the ability to conduct monetary policy without taking into consideration political factors. It is so important for our economy, for the people that we serve, that they can depend over time on a central bank that operates that way, free of political influence. It's part of the absolute foundation of this amazing economy that we have. It's just one of the many reasons why the U.S. economy is the envy of the world. That piece of institutional architecture separates successful countries from unsuccessful countries. It is extremely important, not for the people who work at the Fed at any given time, but for the people that we serve, that the Fed remain able to conduct monetary policy in a way that doesn't get pulled into politics, trying to help or hurt any particular politician or political party. It's critical for the people that we serve. In terms of when I would leave, I will leave when I think it's appropriate to do so. Was that all your questions, or was there... Well, I just have a follow-up, which is, what would you say to the criticism that by remaining on the Board you're actually taking a political act and denying President Trump the majority of the Board, which as President he would have if you left? I don't see that at all. And as I mentioned, you know, I'm literally staying because of the actions that have been taken. I had long planned to be retiring, and you know, the things that have happened in really in the last three months, I think left me no choice but to stay until they're... till I see them through at least that long. You know, in addition, I don't see how this will interfere. My attention is not to interfere. You know, I was a Governor for almost six years, and the tradition is at the Fed that Governors who understand how difficult the role of Chair is, and as a... as a soon-to-be former Chair, I do understand how hard it is to get consensus with 19 strong-minded people. You work with the Chair, you try to... you try to be heard, but also collaborate with the Chair and try to support the Chair when you can. When you can't, you can't. And I think that's the attitude that people generally take, and that's the attitude that I'll take. Chair Powell, if I could ask about the inflation outlook. In March you described the standard practice of looking through energy shocks as conditional on inflation expectations staying anchored. Since that meeting there has been very little progress reopening key energy trade corridors. Can you help us understand how the inflation outlook has changed in the intervening period, beginning with the prospects for tariff pass-through resolving on the timeline that you had outlined in March, before getting to the energy shock that is now on top? So, you know, I would look at it this way. For a long time we've been working on the... on the hypothesis, really, that tariff... tariffs would lead to a one-time price increase and that that would go away over time. In other words, there would be no further change, so measured inflation wouldn't reflect that higher level going up more and more. And it's time for that to happen. You know, we really do expect that to be happening in the next two quarters. So we'll be watching very carefully to see that what we've thought all along would happen... that's kind of a critical part of the forecast. We need to really see that. With... with energy it's so hard to say. I mentioned, you know, in... you know sort of the textbook you you would look through an oil shock because they tend to be short-lived and they tend to revert and monetary policy works with long and variable lags so you you wouldn't necessarily react right away. I think that is all the more true given that we're several years above 2 percent inflation and that we're already looking through the tariff shock. So, I think we're going to be very cautious about that. It's... but the... the question about about looking through energy really is not... is not in front of us right now. We... it hasn't even peaked yet. And I think we'd want to see the back side of that and progress on tariffs before we even thought about about reducing rates. So if I could follow up, the statement today preserves language that has taken on some meaning as it was socialized when the committee was actively lowering rates. Why is that easing bias still ripe given how different the inflation outlook is now versus a meeting or two ago, and what more would have to happen for it to get evicted? So that was... as you'll recall we we had a discussion about that at the last meeting and we talked about it in the press conference after the March meeting. We had the same today, we had quite a vigorous discussion about that very issue and the guidance and is it still appropriate and that kind of thing. And I would say that the, you know, number of people on the committee who either could support that language change, changing to a more neutral stance so that a hike is as likely as as a cut, that number has increased over the intervening period. And it's easy to see why, I mean it's... it's a good question, right? You see inflation has moved up over the interim a bit, core inflation is 3.2 now, moving albeit just a little bit in the wrong direction. And we we know that there will be... that there's headline inflation coming out of the Gulf and we don't know how much that will be. We're just going to need to see. So it makes all the sense in the world that people would look at that and and we'd have a vigorous discussion about that. You saw that three people dissented over the language. I think all of those people agreed with the with the rate decision. So the majority of the committee did not want to do that and and I... I didn't think we needed to do it at this meeting. It really was just a question of what's... why do we need to do that now? You know, we have so much to learn, there's so much uncertainty about the path ahead, there doesn't need to be any rush to make that decision now because, you know, what what happens in the next 30, 60 days even by the next meeting could really change the picture around that... around that language. So, you know, it was a... it's a close... it's a much closer thing on the committee than it was in March, and you know, that makes all the sense in the world, it seems to me. Claire Jones, Financial Times. Just... just going back to this issue of the... the easing bias. We've now got oil approaching 120 dollars a barrel when it comes to the benchmark Brent crude. If it stays around those levels six weeks from now, what would be your guess, best guess as to whether the easing bias will still be in the statement? Thank you. I I wouldn't want to guess. You know, well, first of all we're going to have new leadership in all likelihood by then. And and new leadership is going to have a very important role to play in that. So I I won't be standing here at this podium to answer your questions. So I don't know. As I mentioned, that's all I can really say is that we had a great discussion about that today. It's a, you know, it's got... it's gotten to be a better question in the interim period. We had the discussion, a majority are still on the page of of not feeling the need to move to that level, and I... that's where I am. I get it though, you know, at a certain point you would move and that... that conceivably could come as soon as the next meeting. Thank you. And just to follow on, the new leadership also seem rather lukewarm on press conferences and on the dot plots. What would your advice to him be on these communication tools? So I'm not going to give him any advice through you today here, but I've, you know, I think communications generally is... I think every incoming chair takes a look at communications and it's a very healthy thing. I mean communications is... it's very complex and you know, you can always be... be looking at new things. And and if that happens, feels like it's going to happen, that's a completely appropriate thing. Hi, Chair Powell. Neil Irwin with Axios. Can you tell us if you've been in touch with incoming Chairman Warsh, any... to what extent is this a normal transition process versus all the things swirling around, something unusual, and... and what can we expect when he takes... takes that podium in a few weeks? I haven't seen him since seeing him at a dinner in January where I congratulated him and had a nice... nice chat with him. Haven't seen him. I don't know what a normal process is. You know, the... the last process was with Janet Yellen, with whom I had worked for six years, and so it was you know, we were sitting down the hall from each other, so it was a very different thing. I think this is and will be a very normal standard kind of a transition process. So that's what I expect. I have every reason to think it will be. Quick follow-up. Is the Supreme Court ruling on Governor Cook a factor in when you may leave as a Governor? I wasn't thinking of it as such, but no, not really. I mean, I'm thinking more of the other things I mentioned. Hi, thanks for taking our question. I wanted to ask a question about your tenure, and Chris Rugaber at Associated Press. During your tenure as chair, you often spoke about how disadvantaged Americans benefited from extended periods of low unemployment. In the new framework the Fed adopted in 2020, some economists say elevated the Fed's employment mandate. Are you worried that the... the pandemic inflation spike that followed will make future Fed chairs more reluctant to pursue a hot jobs market and should they be? I don't know the answer to that. I mean, what we so what we experienced in the... in the teens, the mid-teens, was really low levels of unemployment for a long period of time and no reaction from inflation. And we all took very much took notice of that. We also noticed that the biggest wage gains were going to people at the bottom end of the income spectrum. And we had many, many reports of... I mean it felt like a fairly stable equilibrium, and a lot of benefits were flowing to people at the bottom end of the income spectrum, including companies were, you know, setting up in... in, you know, people who were confined and like training them before they got out, and it was a very healthy sort of set of societal dynamics. So, of course, I think anybody would love to get back to that. I don't think that anything that happened to create the global pandemic inflation was in any in any way related to overweighting the employment market. I mean it was a it was a global shock that happened essentially very, very similarly all over the world that had to do with closing, reopening, stimulus, and all that. And I mean you could look at it... a graph of 10 big economies on the page and not know which was the US and which was Germany, France, and things like that. So I don't think that that insight was in any way responsible for the high inflation that we experienced. So, I mean I think it's always been a balance. You've you've got to be strong on both of our dual mandates. And we for example now we don't feel that the labor market is at all a source of inflation. So we don't need to be worrying about that, it's been a long time since we have had to worry about that. Well, actually during the... during the pandemic recovery, the labor market was super overheated and tight and that's when we had to worry about it, but... but not now. And just on the other issue, are you... would you need, do you need more assurance from the Justice Department before stepping down? Is that what you're waiting for or what else is going to affect... I'm waiting, you know, for the for the investigation to be well and truly over, with finality and transparency. And I'm waiting for that, and I will leave when I think it's appropriate to do so. Michael McKee from Bloomberg Television and Radio. I'd like to ask you if you could explain a little more or characterize a little more the discussion about the two-sided view and interest rates, because there were some members of the Open Market Committee who've been suggesting that we may need to raise interest rates even absent the war because inflation was not coming down fast enough. Is there any sense that interest rates might have to go up, or was this just a setup to sort of warn people that you're worried about the war impacts? So nobody was... all the three dissenters and and others who could have supported that, and others who were, you know, who were voters and preferred... non-voters who preferred it, they all supported the right decision, right? So people are not saying we need to hike now. It's more a question of, you know, don't we kind of feel that we should be neutral, and markets are... markets are... what are markets doing? People argue that this is consistent with what markets are doing. And again, it's a... it's a very fair question, but you know, these changes, they are, you know, it's a form of forward guidance, and you want to make... you don't want to make them in a way that that will be sustained and continue to make sense and not something you need to take back, you know, fairly quickly. So I think we just... a group of us, including me, didn't feel like we needed to be in a hurry on that, that that markets are not confused about our reaction function, we don't have a problem to solve on that. But the other side of the argument is a good argument too, as as I mentioned. I mean it's a perfectly good argument to be having, good discussion to be having, so and it came out the way it came out. Well, you've got three dissents in favor of a two-sided warning, you've got yourself staying on the Board, you've got the criticism that does come from elected officials, and you've got a lot of critics who have faulted the Fed for being too slow in 2021 with inflation. Are you worried about Fed credibility under all of this? Is that one reason that you want to stay on? Not... not driving my my thinking now. I mean, monetary policy is going to get made by, you know, 19 people. There's a lot of stability there. I mean if you think about it any... every new fair Fed chair has the same situation, which is you've got 18 colleagues on the FOMC, 11 of them vote during any year, and you have to, your job is to create consensus, is to talk to them, understand them, you know, be inside their thinking and be able to pull them together and get consensus and move. And that's... that's what every Fed chair has to do. And I I think Kevin Warsh is actually quite well... he has the capabilities, skills, to be to be very good at that, I would think. So I think I'm not so worried about that process. You know, I think that'll work itself out. Thank you, Chair Powell. Howard Schneider with Reuters. You mentioned that staying on as a Governor you intend to keep a low profile. I'm just wondering if you could give us a little more detail on what that looks like and how you can... (Powell almost walks off, laughter). Touché. what that... yeah, walk down the steps... what that looks like and particularly around the policy discussion, how you're able to to have your intervention and not be a shadow chair and not have kind of an outsized influence over the process. Yeah, you know that's just something I would never do, you know the shadow chair thing. No, it's... you know, I I don't know what the exact specifics of it will be, but I'm going back to being a Governor, I respect the role of Chair, I... you know, I was a Chair... I was a Governor for six years and I know what that's like and I had a... you know, pretty front-row seat with particularly with Chair Yellen, to whom I I was close when I... I worked with Chairman Bernanke for two years, but you know, I was brand new at that time. So I got a sense of what it was and I had real sympathy for how hard it is to get that group to consensus and I always felt like I... you know, I don't want to add to that unnecessarily, and that means try to support the chair where the chair... the direction the chair wants to go if you can. If you can't, you can't. And I think that's the attitude that people generally take, and that's the attitude that I'll take. And in your view as a soon-to-be Governor, how do you see the risks of oil prices bleeding into core inflation in coming weeks because that was, it seems like, the commentary that was coming from particularly some of the Reserve Bank presidents, they were elevated concerns about the bleed into core and, you know, here we are with three dissents now. What do you see as the prospect of of a core inflation... You know, those those prospects are real. Remember, though, our... and they're real and the real thing is we're going to have to wait and see. We're going to need to see. And the good news is we think our policy stance is just... is in a very good place for us to wait and see. We, you know, we're right kind of at the high end of neutral or perhaps mildly restrictive. The labor market shows more and more signs of stability, whereas inflation is kind of misbehaving, and so maybe a little bit of of restriction or the high end of neutral is just the right place to be. So we can wait here and see and see how things work out before we act. And we'll see how much that how much does come through into core. You see it already in airfares, of course, but you may see it in many other places you know, we just don't know yet and it's so unknowable because how long will the strait be closed? You can develop any number of scenarios that you want, but we really won't know until we know. So, fortunately, we're in a good place to to wait and let things develop. Thank you. Colby Smith with the New York Times. If I could follow up on Mike's question about hikes, are we right to assume that the hawkish outcome for the Fed is still one in which the committee just extends the pause in rate cuts? And to what extent is there a growing sense within the committee that monetary policy really isn't just restrictive at all right now? The economy is holding up relatively well despite this major energy shock, the unemployment rate has ticked lower, inflation was moving sideways even before the war and is now moving higher. So so where is the committee at on that debate? You know, where we're at is we think our policy really we think our policy rate is in a good place. If we need to hike we will certainly signal that and we will and we will certainly do it. And if we need to to cut then if it's appropriate to cut then we'll signal the opposite. I think because we feel like we're we're in a good place to move in either direction, nobody's calling for a hike right now. So it really is going to depend on how things evolve. And you know, that's really where it is. And as I mentioned, close... you know, much closer question this cycle on changing the guidance, but but ultimately we didn't. And as it relates to the war, at what point do you think the risks to growth will be larger than the risks to inflation as this conflict drags on? You know, you just have to find that out empirically. You know with... given our the fact that we're, you know, a big exporter of energy and that our economy is far less energy-intensive, oil-intensive than it was during the 70s, the effects on the United States are really substantially less than those of Western Europe or or Asia. We're feeling much greater effects from these things, our the effects we're feeling in, you know, in the current situation, currently. And in sort of what's priced in, which is you know, a relatively quick outcome. If if this goes on for much longer and prices go much higher, then we'll feel that much more. And of course, I'm talking about aggregate inflation numbers. We know, we're very well aware that people are experiencing higher gas prices all over the country now and that hurts, and these those hikes may continue to happen. And other things are going to start to reflect airline fares as I mentioned and other other products and services that are dependent upon petroleum and derivatives of petroleum, people are going to start to feel that. Thank you, thank you, Chair Powell. Edward Lawrence with Fox Business. So I guess I'll just ask you directly on this, the markets don't see a rate cut at all this year is what they're predicting. Do you think that we are at the neutral rate? Why or why not? You know, the neutral rate is a... we cannot know it with with certainty. I think pretty close to the neutral rate, yeah. I I always had it, you know, between 3 and 4 percent. We're a little north of 3-1/2, so that's well in the range of what I would consider a reasonable... but at the higher end of the range of what I would consider a reasonable neutral rate. You know I think the labor market is still probably cooling off just a little bit, and I... I don't think there's much of a case, for any case really for for policy looking, you know, meaningfully restrictive, maybe mildly restrictive or neutral, I would say. And I want to follow up on some of the other questions about your future a little bit. The... the first time we've seen four dissents now since October of 1992. Are you handing off a divided Fed? You know, the thing to remember is we have always had vigorous debates and and they're excellent debates. I have to say they're they're really good, and we're we're in an unusually difficult situation. So we've really had four supply shocks, you can actually you can say more than four but at a minimum we've had the pandemic, we had the invasion of the of Ukraine, we had tariffs, and now we have have Iran and the oil you know the oil spike. So those every every supply shock has the capability of driving inflation up and unemployment up and and what do you do? You know you're it's a central bank has a really hard time knowing what to do. So the right thing to do is to try to balance the achievement of those two goals and that's what our framework calls for us to do. But these are really tough, difficult judgments. You've got to have a forecast for each variable, you've got to think how long it's going to take to get back to target, you got to think how how restrictive or not is policy. So it's only natural that you have a range of views on the committee. People are going to see it different ways, they're going to have different risk tolerances and that kind of thing. I mean if everybody agreed that would be that would be surprising, and and I think it's only a part... it's a function of the extraordinarily challenging set of supply shocks that we've been dealing with now for five, six years. Thank you so much, Chair Powell. Selena Wang with ABC News. Are you confident that Kevin Warsh will stand up to political pressure from President Trump? So he he testified very strongly to that effect in his hearing and and I'll take him at his word. And when it comes to gas, right now it's over four dollars a gallon, inflation just hit a two-year high, should Americans expect to be paying higher gas prices for the rest of this year and in your view does that take a rate cut off the table? And secondly, by staying on as Fed Governor, what message do you think you're sending to the President? I I don't know what gas prices are going to do for the rest of the year. And it will depend on how long the strait remains closed and how quickly it can be reopened and that kind of thing. But remember when gas prices go up, that's disposable income coming out of people's pockets, so they're going to spend less on other things, so there will be a hit to GDP. So it's a, you know, it's a it's a question whether spending you know, goes down to offset the inflationary effects. So it's not... the answer isn't obvious ex-ante whether you whether you should move your rate over because of that. We'll have to see how it evolves. And the message it sends to President Trump, I'll stand on what I said earlier. Hi, Victoria Guida with Politico. During your tenure Fed independence has come under pressure in a lot of different ways and I was just wondering, practically speaking, where do you see Fed independence as coming from? Is it the law, is it political support from Congress, is it the actions of the Fed? What what sustains Fed independence? Well, it's, you know, it's to to a significant extent it is the law and you know we've had to go to court to successfully so far to defend it. But you know the the law does create a setting in which the Fed can and is directed to make monetary policy without consideration of political factors. And so so part of it is the law. But it goes beyond that though. There's a set of customs, there's a there's a boundary line between the Fed and the administration, between the Fed and the Treasury Department, and we need to respect those continue to respect those boundaries about what the Fed is responsible for and what the Treasury is responsible for and what the rest of the administration is responsible for. So some of it is legal. In fact, it's all legal at the end of the day but there it's more than just monetary policy. We, you know, we don't want to use our tools to we haven't wanted to use our tools to achieve goals that are really clearly outside our mandate. Every administration looks at our tools and thinks that would be good to repurpose those to serve other purposes. But that gets that drags us into politics and into fiscal policy. So we've resisted that. Well, and maybe another way of asking it too is do you think that Fed independence is as strong now as when you became chair, and if so, why? Look, I think it's at risk. I mean, I think these these, you know, these legal assaults, if you will, as I mentioned, you know, we're the institution is being battered over these things, we're having to resort to, you know, the courts to to enforce our legal, you know, not even so much independence, it's it's really the ability to do monetary to make monetary policy without political considerations. That's what we're talking about. And we've had to do that and we've been successful so far, but that's not over. None of that is concluded yet. And you know, it's really important. It's not about it's not about people who work at the Fed or the institution, it's about the benefits of a central bank that makes decisions based on analysis and our best thinking rather than, you know, trying to help or hurt politicians. It's, you know, that there's a bright line between central banks who do one and do the other and successful countries have uniformly, successful advanced economy countries have a really strong set of protections around their central bank just for that reason. So that's what it's all about. I you know, I think I am confident as I said in my remarks that the Fed will continue to make its decisions based on analysis, rigorous analysis, and not on political considerations, but we've had to fight for it. And you know, I'd like to think I'd like to think we can get out of that era and go back to respecting, you know, what the law says and what custom has been, which is to, you know, let the Fed do our thing. We're it's an institution full of human beings who work super hard to get things right for the benefit of the public. We're all human, don't expect per perfection, but do expect us to make, you know, decisions without political considerations and the very best analysis we can bring. Thanks, Chair Powell. Matt Egan with CNN. You've made many tough decisions in your time at the Fed, and as your time as chair comes to a close and you think about your tenure and perhaps your legacy, are there any decisions that stand out as ones that you're particularly proud of, and are there any that with the benefit of hindsight you would take a mulligan on? Yeah, I it's hard I wouldn't want to sing single out individual things at this point. You know, I'll just say you know, we all of us together have consistently tried to do what we think is best for the American people based on our tools and our objectives that that Congress has given us. It's been very challenging because we've been in a situation of supply shock supply shocks really for six years and it's that's just a very different situation than for very long time what the Fed and other central banks were doing all the time was demand management. And you know, and and there was always the inflation mandate, but inflation was low for 25 years. So this is a very different world and a much more challenging one where you have to balance the two objectives. And by the way, central banks that have a an inflation mandate have to do exactly the same thing because they're balancing economic activity. So that's been challenging and we've, you know, we've tried to do our very best through these really challenging times and I'm really proud of of the work that I've done that that my colleagues and I have done during these these years. Thanks, Chair Powell. Christine Romans, NBC News. I want to ask about legacy. When the history books are written, how do you think your stewardship at the Fed will be remembered for the past eight years? You know, I'm going to just say that that's for that's for someone else to say. I'll give you a mulligan on that. All right, I'm going to ask you about misbehaving inflation then. You talked about those four big shocks, supply shocks over the past five years, and inflation still misbehaving. What's your message to American families who feel like inflation has not been under control for them really since since the Covid reopening? You know, we're committed to bring inflation back down to 2 percent and and sustainably. That's that's our goal and we will we will stick at it until that happens. We keep getting these events keep happening which keep driving up costs and you know the best thing we can do is to use our tools to guide inflation back down to 2 percent. I think trying to get there really quickly could be very costly in terms of of of job loss and things like that, but we try to get there over time in a way that does the least damage possible and, you know, our commitment to that is never-ending and unshakable. How would you describe the economy outside of the misbehaving inflation? I mean, it's still offly resilient given all of the blows. I don't know that you can be offly resilient. So it's actually quite resilient, I would say, because it's a positive thing if I can if I can have that amendment. Yeah, the, you know, growth is really solid across our economy. Some of that is that consumer spending is hanging in pretty well, the the most recent data are good.
