We just talked about farming and agriculture. Let's talk about protecting communities in the world. Think about the last wildfire you saw in the news, right? By the time it made the headlines and people found out, it was too late. A lot of damage had already been done, neighborhoods wiped out. And think about the world we live in today, right? Our population is increasing, our cities are getting more dense and complex, the weather's unpredictable. And so we're at risk more so than ever for these threats. And it's not just wildfires; it's storms, floods, other urban emergencies, right? These are not rare events anymore, right? In fact, the UNEP says that extreme wildfires are projected to increase by up to 50 percent by the end of the century. Think about that, up to 50 percent. So these problems are going to get harder, not easier. Now in most cases, the early signs are there. We just don't detect them early enough to do something about them. And therein lies the challenge: how do we shorten the gap between when one of these threats occurs and we can do something about it? Let's see how AI helps us do that. So it turns out that there are signals of potential wildfires in lots of different places. In some cases, right? The signal is very much at the ground level, right? Especially the micro-signals. Being able to see kind of individual patches of a forest burning or starting to burn, right? And so that's what we're seeing on the left here, is how do we put ground-based sensors empowered by compute at the edge, empowered by models that are driven by training not at the edge, right? And then use that to shorten the time between occurrence of some threat, right? And detection of that threat. Be able to get out ahead of it. And it turns out that the other direction here is if we think of one sensor looking horizontally, like looking out over a forest. Over can be a very different word if we say over in a satellite sense. And so it turns out you can put GPUs on satellites. It's a pretty good place to do your advanced computing, so you're not beaming down all of your images at scale, right? These huge images that you have if you want to cover huge tracts of land, right? It's much more efficient if you can process it onboard the satellite and beam down just the detections or the zoomed-in parts of what you need to be looking at, right? So having these two very different approaches to what is fundamentally a major similar problem, right? This is a huge part of how conceptually we should think about what startups are able to bring to AI, right? That it's not about one solution to a problem, right? It's about a thousand solutions to the problem. And then figuring out which of them is most successful in doing the thing that we need it to do. There's an interesting follow-up to that, right? If we think of the left side here, maybe even the right side, as adding new sensors, right? Into the world. I love when startups have a major part of their business being collecting more data about the physical world. And so that's where, you know, it's not enough just to have kind of a sensor, right? Or to rely on legacy sensors that weren't, we should remember, weren't laid out for AI processing. Traditional weather sensors weren't laid out for how I maximize the fidelity of my AI models. But if you start from the premise, right? That I need to make as good of a detection chance as I can have for a particular kind of AI, then putting up radars might make sense for you. That's what we're seeing on the left with ClimaVision here. They are literally putting up a radar network kind of across the southeastern United States that then helps augment places where otherwise traditional weather forecasting methods may have had gaps, or been less able to predict the kinds of disasters that threaten particularly heavy population centers, right? The kind of things that have caused so much damage as extreme climate has proliferated, as you described from the UN. Another way to think of this though is to go from sensor processing, how do we instead of just taking images or from traditional sensors, how do we really get a detailed picture? In some sense, this is about how do we shape our understanding of the world. The other side of this, on the right here, is how do we instead go from traffic cameras to a 3D understanding of what's happening in our cities, right? To be able to say hey, this problem isn't just about counting cars, but about being able to track flows of people and of things, right? Through cities so that we can treat them as integrated systems rather than as individual points on a map. And so it's really cool to think from, you know again, two very different angles on a similar problem, right? If we have challenges affecting our cities, adding new sensors and interpreting those sensors in different ways really provides some powerful leverage to adapt to those problems. So we just talked about protecting communities and the key there was early detection, right? The theme was urgency. Let's talk about the environment where problems build up gradually, slowly, right? When you think about waste management systems, recycling, water systems, forests, the problems in these areas are slow and gradual, and we don't often find out about them until it's too late, right? But the problem is that if we cannot see these problems, we cannot measure them, right? We don't do a good job measuring them. And as the UN says, 68% of environment-related Sustainable Development Goals lack sufficient data to assess progress. Think about that, we've set our goals, and for 68% of them we don't even have the data to measure and see if we're making progress, right? We're just flying blind. And so the opportunity here is how can we use AI to get more visibility into these systems? So one, we can start measuring them, and then two, we can start tracking and see if we're making progress against our goals. Let's look at a few examples. So look, it is very easy to get excited about the shiniest version of AI, right? The version of AI that talks about building the sci-fi glass and steel version of the future, right? But I'll be honest, I get just as excited about the really low-level, micro, "you know it's there and we try not to think about it" parts of our world. And that's what we're seeing here with these companies that are dealing with our waste streams. In some sense, the challenges that we're solving by having denser cities, smaller transportation costs, and more interconnected communities, they come at a different kind of cost, right? Now we have to deal with the waste or the recycling, right? That we need to do from those human populations. And so if we can instead apply AI to analyzing, getting data on, as you can see on the left here, right? Scanning to understand what's in our waste flows, right? So that instead of just saying there is a lot of it, right? We can do something with that. We can turn this into the kind of optimizable cycle that we're so used to in tech and bring that to industries that have lots and lots of potential for becoming, you know, really applying 21st century tools to these problems. And then once you have the data, just like you were saying, right? Once we have the data, then we have the ability to make progress on these goals. And so that lets us then, for instance, add robots into the process to help us sort and pick from those waste streams, right? To bring out of those waste streams things that don't have to be there, again at scale, right? It's not just another dirty job, this is something that now we can kind of solve problems at city scales, which is not trivial. But it's exciting that startups are taking that on. And it's really exciting to me personally that the market has appreciated that, right? That these startups are able to access the kinds of people and technologies that they need to make this meaningful contribution to the world. If we go kind of hard the other direction and you think about, okay that was thinking about kind of urban environments, right? If we're reading the signals of the planet on a more geophysical scale, right? Looking at systems that again, on this theme of what sensors do we need to make to tell if we are making progress, and if we are, how much progress towards our macro goals as a society? Right? Putting up wind, sorry, putting up weather balloons. Again, it's not the kind of thing that's like making the shiniest possible headlines. It's super important. Right? Otherwise we can do all of the things that we want in the world but we don't know if they're having the real impact. And I care a great deal about that. The other side of this, if we think of collection as the left side, we can think of on the right, how do we make the data that we collect accessible, a common operating picture of the planet. Right? That's not easy. There have been lots of cases where people have started trying, but bringing that together in a way that people have a common operating picture of their business, right? Or an industry. If we take that same mindset and we're able to say hey, it's just as important to have a common understanding of the planet. We can bring data together, make that data available to consumers of it downstream, right? In a way that has not necessarily been available. And to use AI to both accelerate and improve the precision of that kind of processing so that then it is available planet scale. I'm excited for that world and a lot of people very close to me who are benefiting that way. Let's talk about something that everything else depends on: the power grid. Now, most of us don't think about the power grid until our lights go out, right? But it is something that everything else depends on: hospitals, transportation systems, communication, and of course, data centers. But the grid was built decades ago. It was built for another world, right? It was built for a world where power flowed in one direction: from the utility plant to people's homes and businesses. Think about how that's changed now. We now have solar rooftops pushing electricity back into the grid. We have EVs charging and discharging rapidly at unpredictable times, right? Now all of this, plus the fact that one, the grid is old, and two, it has to deal with extreme weather means that there is more strain than ever on our grid, right? And inevitably, when the grid fails, not everyone is impacted equally. The Council on Foreign Relations says that when severe storms strike, lower-income communities wait the longest for power to return. Think about that: the people who need power the most have to wait the longest to get it back. Now, one solution is to just rebuild the grid from scratch. Well, that's not practical. So how can AI help us make our grid more resilient, flexible, and smart? Let's look at a few examples. Yeah, absolutely. You know, look, one of the challenges of living in an environment where we are sharing the planet, thankfully, with lots of plants, is that they don't always have a purely positive impact on your access to electricity, right? If you've had a tree or branch fall on power lines, you've certainly experienced what I'm talking about. And so it turns out that there are of course startups dealing with those problems too. Here, the trick is satellite imagery, right? Being able to look down at a fine-grained understanding of local environments, figure out that there is foliage putting at risk parts of the grid, right? And then be able to proactively address that in a way that so far has very much been, like you said, unevenly distributed. Historically, we have had human-based patrols looking for those kinds of risks. Right? Being able to do that again at scale, all the time, on kind of a persistent defense strategy, is a huge part of how we make grids more resilient with the systems that we already have, right? Without doing a wholesale kind of ground-up revision of the grids. The kind of if one version of this is my usual story, you'll hopefully you don't get too tired of, we need more sensors, more data processing, and then more action based on the data, right? That's kind of the theme of how AI works. Here's a specific kind of a flavor that we haven't talked about yet, but is very cool and is something that NVIDIA's played a significant role in, right? Digital twins. Being able to have in the computer a kind of physically driven, sensor-informed model of how the grid works. Being able to use that model to simulate different kinds of stress on it, gives us the ability to again proactively kind of move forward the action point and not just be waiting for it to fail and responding to that, right? Because if we do the latter, again you get these kind of inequities in how those resources are applied. Or if we can get out ahead of things, right? We have the ability to prevent rather than react. And then you, like Joshi said, right? If we say hey, the grid was for a hundred years, 150 years, right? Has been electricity coming from generation sources and flowing out to people, right? If we invert that paradigm and we think hey, there are lots of generation sources now. There are lots of, kind of have to do this two-way street and that just, it doesn't just increase the complexity, right? It kind of squares the complexity. Right? Because now everybody is a consumer and a producer in this simplified model of the world. And so now you've got all of the flows going from everywhere to everywhere, right? It's much more complicated, and so it doesn't really work with the old models. And so then having particularly sensors at the edge that let us have an always-on understanding of the whole back-and-forth of the grid, right? Or as we're preparing for data centers, right? And the impact as you described that they're having on the environment, to use AI to mitigate the impact of AI, right? And that's what we're seeing on the right side here. Only in some sense, the only solution that we're going to have for how do we create a sustainable growth pattern in these industrial areas, right? Is going to be the kind of modeling, the kind of data analytics, the kind of forecasting, the kind of simulation, and then building around that that is made possible by AI. So that's for the new grid, right? For a grid that is people-centric, you have to really change the origins of where you're thinking. All right, we're halfway there. So far we've been talking about big systems, like planet-level stuff. We've talked about farms, we've talked about cities, talked about the environment, talked about the power grid, of course. Let's zoom all the way in down to an individual. Now, some of the most important moments in people's lives also happen to be the most private and quiet. Let me give you an example. There's probably an elderly person right now that might have a fall, but because they live alone, it might be hours before someone finds out, right? Because these moments are private and quiet, not everybody is watching, right? Not everyone has access to a caregiver, not everyone has access to a specialist, not everyone has someone or something watching them at those important moments when they need it the most. And I think this is where AI can help, right? The ITU Focus Group says that AI-based technologies hold great potential in improving the accessibility, quality, and value of healthcare outcomes. In this case, this is as much an accessibility problem as much as a tech problem, right? How can AI help us reach more people in the moments they need it the most? Let's look at a few examples. One of the biggest challenges worldwide in health is vision impairment. There are low-cost interventions, right? Glasses that still need to be proliferated around the world. There are sophisticated interventions, right? Dogs as companions for visually impaired folks. But those are frankly quite expensive and very unevenly distributed. And so if we can bring down the cost, if we can bring in the learning from people around the world as to what they need to navigate their environments, we have the ability now to build technologies. Like this one, they give freedom back to people who need it, right? This headset here has sensors, onboard processing, again, that let you see without sight. That let you see in the same way that cars do. And so you can see the demonstration here is being able to reach out and grab an apple with no visual data going to your eyes. That's remarkable. And again, I think the thing that I'd hopefully leave you with here is that making that available, making that accessible, making that widely distributed, right? This is in some sense the thing that is most important to me about how these technologies are changing lives. It's less directly about how cool it is, and it is very cool, but it's really about how this can be replicated more easily than many of the alternatives have been historically. If we think, you know kind of hard the other direction, right? If this is "let's make everybody's day-to-day more accessible," right? In the worst and the hardest and the most stressful moments, like you're saying, we also need people watching our backs. Emergency first responders need people watching their backs as they try to provide care under, again, some of the hardest times that you can experience. And the last thing we want to do is have people making their best judgments under significant stress and having to monitor lots of different things all at the same time. And so one of the things that AI can do, it turns out, is understand how what people are hearing on emergency dispatcher calls, right? 911 calls here in the US, how people's voice patterns and stresses and other signals can tell us about their individual situation, right? And it can take off some of that cognitive load from the dispatchers and the clinicians who are being asked to provide diagnosis and response. While still keeping them in charge, right? Acting as kind of a supplement to their expert judgment rather than something else. We also, as Joshi said, in the quietest moments, right? In the moments where you least want people constantly monitoring, right? We have AI to provide that impersonal, privacy-protecting support. Right? Being able to say hey, this senior needs help, right? Without having to have a person monitoring that. To be able to understand early in your vocal patterns, right? If there's something cognitively that is impairing or an early sign of something happening in your body. This is incredibly important as we're going to, you know, deal with the challenges of aging populations and unequal access to healthcare. And so again, in all of these cases, the AI is serving the role of spreading and of making broader the kinds of healthcare that otherwise would be expensive and limited. I'm excited for that world and I know a lot of people very close to me who are benefiting that way. All right. In all the sections we've covered so far, the theme has been seeing things early and more clearly. That's true in this one as well. Let's talk about science and medicine, where some of the most important decisions also happen at the very beginning. Now, we have the most amazing people working in science. But science itself has gotten too complex. 200 years ago, you could argue that one smart individual could probably keep track with everything going on in the field of science. But that's just not true anymore. We've made so much progress and there is so much information out there, right? It's impossible for one person to keep track. We now have thousands of teams working on tens of thousands of projects all solving small parts of the big puzzle that is science. Now if you think about it, before a trial or a treatment begins, these smart people have to make some early but consequential decisions. Which path to take for a given trial, which direction to take for a treatment. And these early decisions end up shaping years of research after. The challenge is that these folks are making these decisions often without having the full picture. Therein lies the opportunity, right? AI is enabling the analysis of extensive data sets, uncovering hidden patterns, and accelerating the pace of discovery. So if you think about it, if we can augment our amazing scientists and researchers with the complete information, if we can augment their intuition with the full picture, right at the beginning when those decisions really matter, then we can accelerate the pace of discovery. Let's look at a couple AI startups that are helping us do that. You know it's like you said Joshi, it's really challenging, right? When you have to pick paths without being able to for very good in this case privacy protective reasons, right? Aggregating kind of all of the healthcare data of patients with a particular condition or in a particular family of research, that would be enormously helpful to the model but a significant risk. And so one of the things that is I think underappreciated about the modern kind of deep learning training solutions is what we'll call federated learning. This is where I can take a model and move it to the hospital, right? Move it to where the patient data is stored rather than moving the data to where the model training is happening. Right? And so that means that every medical facility, right? All of these kind of protected enclaves of data, the model comes in, gets a little bit of training, and then moves on. Right? And then if you do that, turns out you're training on the data, right? You are still building the model, you're just doing that in a way that then doesn't aggregate kind of everybody's medical and health information to one place. And so that's really exciting, right? You can facilitate, you can power a whole new generation of models without having the kind of risks and concerns that you might have otherwise. But then you can build on top of that, right? You can say okay, we're going to simulate, right? We're going to just like we were with the kind of digital twins for the power grid, right? We can simulate people's biology and as the caption here observes, test drive, right? Try out a treatment in this model in this "in-silico," right? This is done in a way that then gets to that high leverage point at the very beginning of treatment. Or if we're designing new ways to test, as you can see on the right here, designing new ways to test and pipeline through interventions without having to limit ourselves to traditional ways of assessing the data that you're getting, right? Be able to test them on realistic surrogates, analogs for the actual place that you want to deploy a therapy. And so being able to again create from the ground up these very novel places to make the decisions, assisted by AI, that's an incredibly powerful way to do medicine. I'm excited for that. And then here I'll give you kind of the, in some sense, you know, if I've got drugs, pharmaceuticals, and I've got my genes, right? The hope is that I can have some kind of personalized therapy that goes along with that. If you think of kind of going from drugs to outcomes, being able to inject the response that my particular genetic makeup will have to those therapies is incredibly powerful. And it's something that when we talk about personalized medicine, right? There are lots of ways to personalize medicine but one of the most fundamental is to understand how I might personally respond to something, to a particular treatment. Again, being able to intervene at that high leverage point early on and not have to wait until I'm seeing the reaction in as a patient. All right. So we have everything you saw here today is about how AI is helping solve hard problems in the physical world. We just covered a lot. We just covered 25 startups across six themes. Now I don't want to recap everything, but I did want to leave you with a few, right? We saw AI in farming, how it is helping farmers treat at the individual plant level instead of the farm level. We saw satellites detecting wildfires from space in a matter of minutes. We saw robots helping sort recyclables so less of it ends up in landfills. We saw how AI can help us create a digital twin of our power grid so we can spot weaknesses and be better prepared for emergencies. You saw a pair of glasses that can help the visually impaired navigate the world independently. We saw how AI can help us create a digital tumor to help doctors test treatments or test which treatment would work for a patient before the actual treatment begins. And like I said before, all of these companies are solving hard problems and all of them are building on NVIDIA's stack. The other thing that's common for all 25 is they're all part of NVIDIA's Inception program. Jensen says that Inception is our commitment to partnering with the world's most innovative AI startups. It's how we support these startups by giving them access to free compute, technical resources, go-to-market support, and connecting them with the broader NVIDIA ecosystem. We help them from zero to one and then one to 100, we're there throughout the way. Best of all, it's free. We work with startups in all industries, including the non-obvious ones as you saw today. And we have startups from over 120 countries that are in Inception. In fact, some of the startups that you saw today are here at GTC. If you want to learn more about Inception or want to learn more about the startups that we talked about today, please come talk to us at the Inception info desk in the Expo Hall. And then lastly, if you're building a startup or you know someone that's building a startup, please join Inception. The link is right there. We'd love to work with you and be supportive of your journey. Thank you. Thank you so much.

WATCH LIVE: BOE Governor Andrew Bailey to speak after rate decision

CNBC International Live(482.0K)·94:20·1.2K views·2026-04-29
Andrew BaileyGovernor, Bank of England
TL;DRThe Bank of England held its benchmark interest rate steady at 3.75% in an 8-1 vote, taking previous expectations of rate cuts off the table due to Middle East conflict-driven energy price volatility.
TL;DR The Bank of England held its benchmark interest rate steady at 3.75% in an 8-1 vote, taking previous expectations of rate cuts off the table due to Middle East conflict-driven energy price volatility. To navigate this uncertainty, the bank introduced three distinct...

TL;DR

The Bank of England held its benchmark interest rate steady at 3.75% in an 8-1 vote, taking previous expectations of rate cuts off the table due to Middle East conflict-driven energy price volatility. To navigate this uncertainty, the bank introduced three distinct economic scenarios ranging from a rapid return to its 2% inflation target to a severe 6% inflation spike that would require aggressive interest rate hikes.

The Rate Decision and the "Active Hold"

  • The 8-1 Rate Vote: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) voted to maintain the bank rate at 3.75%.
      • The Dissenting Voice: Chief Economist Huw Pill was the sole hawk, voting instead for a 25 basis point hike to 4.0%.
      • Market Relief: Victoria Clarke (Chief UK Economist at Santander) noted that the 8-1 split came as a sigh of relief for markets that feared a more aggressive hawkish shift.
  • An "Active Hold" Stance: Governor Andrew Bailey emphasized that holding the rate is an active, deliberate policy decision rather than a passive "wait-and-see" approach.
  • Rate Cuts Off the Table: The MPC officially abandoned its previous projections for interest rate cuts this year. Bailey confirmed that the "headroom" previously reserved for cuts will instead be used to absorb the inflationary impact of the geopolitical shock.

Three Economic Scenarios for the UK

Because of the extreme volatility of global oil and gas prices, the Bank of England departed from its traditional single forecast to present three illustrative scenarios:

  • Scenario A (Benign Case): Assumes energy prices follow market futures curves downward and consumer demand drops sharply due to weak sentiment.
      • Inflation Outcome: No second-round wage-price effects occur, and inflation falls below the 2% target by the end of next year without further rate hikes.
  • Scenario B (Moderate/Base Case): Assumes more persistent energy price pressures due to slow repairs of damaged gas infrastructure in the Gulf.
      • Inflation Outcome: Modest second-round effects are felt, but inflation gradually returns to the 2% target within two to three years. Reversing out the previously assumed 55 basis point market rate cuts is expected to accommodate this scenario.
  • Scenario C (Severe Shock Case): Assumes prolonged disruptions pushing oil to $130 a barrel and gas to 210 pence per therm, alongside strong wage-price feedback loops.
      • Inflation Outcome: Inflation peaks above 6% in early 2027 and remains above the 2% target after three years.
      • Policy Response: This scenario would require a materially tighter monetary policy, potentially pushing interest rates to 5.25% or higher, and would likely trigger a mild recession.

Labor Market and Wage Dynamics

  • Labor Market Slack: The MPC noted that the UK labor market has significantly more slack now than during the 2022 energy crisis. This weakness is expected to lower employee bargaining power and prevent aggressive wage-price spirals.
      • Unemployment Outlook: The unemployment rate recently dipped toward 5% but is projected to rise back to 6% under a severe shock scenario.
  • Private vs. Public Sector Pay:
      • Private sector wage growth is moderating toward levels comfortable for the bank.
      • Public sector pay remains elevated and represents a key domestic metric to watch.
  • Indirect Energy Impacts on Food: High energy costs are expected to feed indirectly into food production and distribution. While short-term forecasts project food inflation at 4.6% through September, reports from Bank of England agents suggest it could climb to a range of 6% to 7% by the end of the year.

Market Conditions and Quantitative Tightening

  • Tighter Financial Conditions: Financial markets have already responded to the geopolitical crisis, pushing 10-year gilt yields comfortably above 5%.
  • Commodity Volatility: Bailey highlighted the extreme volatility facing policymakers, pointing out that the Brent crude oil price fluctuated by over $10 a barrel in a single morning.
  • Quantitative Tightening (QT) Here to Stay: Victoria Clarke observed that the Bank of England is highly unlikely to pause its QT program unless the UK experiences an extremely severe episode of gilt market stress, and even then, any pause would only be temporary.

Notable Quotes

"The prospect of cuts which we were after all talking about at this press conference three months ago is not on the table now." — Andrew Bailey, Governor of the Bank of England
"We have really drawn attention... to the continued looseness of the labor market. And that is a very different position for the labor market than we were in in 2022." — Dave Ramsden, Deputy Governor for Markets and Banking
"The MPC's decision to hold bank rate today is based on two key judgments. First, that continued weakness in activity and the labor market is likely to lessen the strength of second-round effects... Second, that uncertainty about the strength of second-round effects means that monetary policy needs to balance the costs of leaning too little against these effects against the costs of responding too much." — Andrew Bailey, Governor of the Bank of England
Welcome to Decision Time. I'm Ian King. The Bank of England is expected to hold interest rates steady today, and according to economists polled by Reuters, will keep rates unchanged through the rest of the year. Predictions for Bank of England cuts in 2026 were reversed when the war in the Middle East began, and UK annual inflation surged to 3.3% in March on the back of rising fuel prices. So with me now is Victoria Clarke. She's chief UK economist at corporate and investment banking at Santander. Victoria, very good to see you this morning. I mean, market consensus is no change and an 8-1 vote in favor of that. Is that your expectation? Yeah, exactly. I don't think there's any real expectation that the bank's going to do anything today. So, eight of the nine MPC are happy with a hold, and the one hawk being Chief Economist Pill, who we think will vote to raise rates as soon as today. But the main thing is, you know, what is the vibe on the MPC? How are they feeling about the risk of inflation versus growth, and what that tells us about the prospect of rate hikes for the next few meetings? And it is a very balanced decision, isn't it? Because if you look at, for example, the flash PMIs for April, I mean, there were some surprising signs of resilience, almost, in the UK economy. That's right. So the PMIs certainly look resilient. They suggest that growth has been okay, but it may be that firms have just been getting things done before the energy price and supply chain disruption arrives. And some of the other signals on growth, I think, are quite weak. I mean, I was looking at petrol volumes—petrol sales—this morning. That's very weak. So I think there is a growth concern for them to contend with, and they've got to balance that against what's going to be quite a big inflation jump as well. So it's not obvious what the right thing to do is. Yeah, I mean, obviously people will see—you mentioned the petrol price—I mean, people see Brent crude trading at above 120 bucks a barrel. I mean, the MPC really does have to be vigilant about second-round effects here, doesn't it? It has to be vigilant, but I don't think that the threat is quite as big as it was in 2022, and I think that the jobs market is weaker. There isn't the employee bargaining power. So you get the jump in inflation from the energy shock. But whether that passes through to wages and gets cooked in, I'd be less, kind of less, concerned that that happens this time. Not complacent, but I certainly think that there's a little bit less reason to be panicked about that. Yeah, I mean, the labor market, the latest figures there were quite an oddity, weren't they, really? Because, I mean, clearly there's softness in the labor market. The number of vacancies in the economy has really, really fallen away over the last couple of years, and yet the headline jobless rate actually came down last time around. It did. It came down because people were leaving the labor market in the group of people right at the bottom end, the kind of 16-to-17-year-olds. And to me, actually, that's a sign that it's just been really difficult for them to find work, so I don't view that as a sign of strength. It's, you know, to me, this picture shows that the labor market's struggling for momentum, and that, I think, all helps to keep wages down a bit. Yeah, but I mean, yeah, so it's labor force participation, essentially. I mean, you mentioned wages there. I mean, for the public sector, they're still growing appreciably stronger than they are in the private sector. That's true. They certainly are. So, private sector pay growth has been moderating down to rates that are looking more comfortable for the Bank of England. Public sector pay is elevated. I mean, there's no other way to look at that data. It is coming lower, and, you know, one of the key questions as we think about how the inflation may or may not get cooked in is what the public sector pay settlements are going to be for this year. So, I'll be watching that closely. So, a bit of fiscal discipline necessary from the Chancellor then, maybe. I think so. Yeah, I think that's right. Obviously, the Bank of England is still engaging in quantitative tightening as well. I mean, is there anything that might cause them to put that on hold for the time being? I think it'd take a very severe episode of, you know, gilt market stress. The bar for a pause on that seems to be very high. And I think if there were some of that, what they'd do is step out temporarily rather than on a permanent basis. Okay. Well, we're moments away now from the decision. It should be dropping on the screens at any moment now. So, here we go. And there we go. The rate has been maintained at 3 and 3/4%. No surprise there in bank rate. Eight in favor of keeping bank rate unchanged and one in favor of a hike, so exactly as you predicted there. Let me see what sterling's doing there. Sterling currently ahead by 2/5 of 1% against the US dollar. Well, Victoria, no real surprise there, as we say. No, I think, you know, that was very much the sort of consensus view. There were a few in the market looking for a unanimous vote and a few that thought maybe we'll get a few more hawkish dissents. So, I think for the market in a way that's a little bit of a relief outcome against the prospect that the vote itself might have delivered another hawkish shock like we had back at the March MPC. So, you know, for the market, a sigh of relief, probably. Well, here we go. We can—I can tell you the monetary policy summary here. This is what the bank is saying in its meeting today: the MPC voted to maintain bank rate at 3 and 3/4%. One member voted to raise to 4%. The conflict in the Middle East means that prospects for global energy prices are highly uncertain. Monetary policy cannot influence energy prices, but will be set to ensure the economic adjustment to them occurs in such a way that achieves the 2% inflation target sustainably. The bank is not changing any of its forecasts at this moment in time. That's interesting. I mean, I presume that what you've got there is a kind of range of scenario analysis around that baseline rather than a sort of base case that you jump onto. But it's also interesting that they've, you know, they've pointed out quite explicitly that they can't control the energy prices effect. I mean, that's telling us that this is very much about the second-round effects and that data will probably take a while to come. So that may be, you know, an effort to balance the statement, too. Well, what they've done, Victoria, which is quite interesting—it says, due to uncertainty caused by the Iran war, the Bank of England has moved away from a single forecast in its April monetary policy report and instead produced forecasts for three separate scenarios. So, Scenario A is the least inflationary. Oil and gas prices follow the paths implied by futures curves, and household spending falls by more than would be implied by the historical relationship with real incomes. Scenario B, the second scenario, energy prices peak at similar levels to Scenario A but remain higher. Household spending behavior remains unchanged. Inflation peaks at a little over 3.5% at the end of 2026. So, that's the scenario in both those forecasts. Scenario C, the most inflationary scenario, energy prices rise more rapidly than in Scenario B and stay higher for a prolonged period. Inflation peaks at more than 6% in early 2027 and is around 2.5% above its target at the end of the scenario in three years' time. Bank rate would need to be materially higher than financial markets expect in the 15 days to April the 22nd. So the bank—I mean, talk about hedging your bets! Yeah. I mean, it's a sensible thing to do, though. The main thing at the moment is they don't know where oil is, so everybody has the information on how they might respond if oil, you know, moves to different paths. So, you take your judgment on the geopolitical situation, and from that, you've got some information on the reaction function. I think to do anything else—to have, you know, a strong baseline view—would have risked sending a signal that there's some crystal ball that they have that we don't have. So, yeah, I mean, you know, it's a pragmatic way of managing markets and keeping markets from getting carried away. Yes. Obviously, the Governor, Andrew Bailey, and his colleagues will be facing the media soon, and you can watch that on the CNBC YouTube channel, of course, with live and uninterrupted coverage of that. But interestingly, I guess most of the media are going to highlight that third scenario: the notion that the headline rate of inflation remains above 6% and is still above the target rate three years out from now. Yeah, I would have thought so. I mean, it's actually not too dissimilar from my severe scenario, which again has that 6%. And I think if you've got inflation moving through 6%, I mean, it's going to be very difficult to do anything other than that. You wouldn't maintain your credibility without hiking into that scenario. The question in that scenario is how long do you stay at, you know, those higher rates, because of course demand will be weakening quickly and you'd expect them to come forward with cuts at some point thereafter. What do you think they mean by bank rate being materially higher than the current level? I mean, look, how long is a piece of string? I think, you know, obviously 3.75% is a bit restrictive. I mean, you're probably talking about them pushing back up towards, you know, the peak that they had not too long ago. So, certainly, you know, through 4.5% or maybe towards a 5% bank rate—that sort of restrictiveness. But look, the question is: for how long? It may be that you want to send a sharp signal that this is important, get those inflation expectations down, and then hope you can lower rates again quickly. Yeah. So, you've modeled a scenario of 6% inflation. What are the other effects on the economy around that? What happens to the jobless rate, for example? Yeah, so look, in that severe shock scenario, unemployment continues to rise through the end of the year. You know, we've got it—well, on the latest figures, it actually dipped towards 5%—so it's back up towards 6% in that scenario. The economy is, as you would imagine, weak. I mean, look, we've got a sort of mild recession in that scenario. And so what you're seeing is you're doing a sort of aggressive, front-up action to say, "Let's not lock in this inflation," but then you notice the kind of weakening economy, and, you know, you're moving into reverse quite quickly. Yeah. Under your modeling then, with inflation at above 6%, does the UK avoid a recession? What happens to GDP? No, in that scenario, you have a mild recession in our analysis. That's right. And how long would a recession last under your modeling? Yeah. I mean, it's a relatively moderate recession. But, you know, when you've got an energy squeeze and tightening monetary policy, the bank has to deliver the economy that painful medicine. But, you know, the reality is that if they didn't do that, you'd have inflation running higher for longer, and the economy would get squeezed even more. So, it is a very tricky situation. Let's hope we don't end up there. I mean, you're obviously from the investment banking arm of Santander, but your parent company, of course, is the UK's second-largest mortgage lender. What happens to house prices under the scenario you sketched out? Look, in that scenario, you're going to find that the mortgage market, the housing market, is somewhat weaker. But the bank will hope that the medicine doesn't need to stay in too long. I mean, that is about doing the right thing to, you know, manage the inflation squeeze so that they can hopefully bring rates lower and not have to, you know, continue to inflict long-term pain. It's a really challenging situation, but I think that they're trying to balance all of these things out. Okay, Victoria, great to have you with us today. Thank you very much indeed. Well, Ritika is outside the Bank of England. She's been in the lock-in already, and she has a good deal more for us now. So, Ritika, no real surprises, but interesting that they've come up with these three different forecasts. Yeah, absolutely Ian. As you mentioned, the Bank of England voted to leave interest rates steady at 3.75%. So, this was expected. The vote split was 8-1 to hold, with Huw Pill being the one to advocate for that higher interest rate hike of 25 basis points. But the decision to leave interest rates unchanged comes despite the material energy shock stemming from the Middle East war. That has pushed up inflation expectations, even as it slowed the outlook for economic growth in the economy. So, the central bank has acknowledged that it needs to balance the risks of doing too little against reacting too much. Now, the MPC says that it does see a material risk of second-order effects from prices and wage setting, but that continued weakness and slack in the labor market and a weaker economy could help to contain inflation pressures. Of course, a lot will depend on how high oil prices go and the duration as well. It's important to note as well that this report was written before oil prices shot up today. Now, the updated forecasts, as you mentioned there, are the first since the Iran war broke out, so it's really critical to gauge the Bank of England's thinking. The Bank of England has put forward three possible scenarios that could depict a range of outcomes for the UK economy. Compared to the central forecast or projections in February, all three scenarios do point to higher inflation and the need for tighter monetary policy. The question, though, is how much. Now, in the worst-case scenario, they see inflation rising to 2.9% in three years, and the models would then show that they would need a much tighter monetary policy, with a bank rate of about 5 and a quarter percent. The Bank of England has also acknowledged that financial conditions have tightened significantly since then. If you look at markets, they are now pricing in three rate hikes. This is after energy prices shot up today, with that 10-year gilt yield above 5%. So again, it's acknowledged that monetary policy needs to be tighter, but the question is how much the Bank of England itself would need to do, and so that leads to the result of the monetary policy holding rates steady at 3.75% as they assess that and the latest developments in the Middle East, Ian. Yes, you were talking about gilt yields, Ritika. The viewers have been looking at what's happening to 10-year gilts—slightly off, down 6 basis points while we've been talking, but still comfortably ahead of 5%. I mean, obviously, Ritika, the MPC will be haunted by its experience of 2022 when, in common with a lot of other central banks, they were deemed not to have tightened monetary policy rapidly enough. Have they said much in the report about the differences between now and 2022? Yes, Ian. That is one thing that they did refer to, because that has been a common sort of comparison to the last time we had the energy shock back in 2022. The difference that they outlined this time is that financial conditions are already significantly tighter than back then. We saw that the 10-year gilt yield is above 5%, and also the labor market has a lot more slack to it than it did in 2022. So, they believe that will dampen those second-order effects that, you know, are the concern for the Bank of England. And so that's why they're going to wait and assess to see how it develops, of course, on the energy price front. Good. Thanks for that, much appreciated. I'll let you get back into the Bank of England building directly behind you. Thanks for that. Well, the Bank of England Governor, Andrew Bailey, will be speaking to the media at half past 12 London time. You can catch his remarks in full on the CNBC International Live YouTube page, live and uninterrupted. Well, there's plenty more central bank action to come here on CNBC. From 1 o'clock London time, 2 o'clock Central European Time, we will be bringing you live coverage of the European Central Bank's decision. But that's it for Bank of England Decision Time. We'll hand you back now to our colleagues stateside. Hi, everybody. Thanks for being here. Welcome to the April Monetary Policy Report press conference. With us today are Clare Lombardelli, Deputy Governor for Monetary Policy; Dave Ramsden, Deputy Governor for Markets and Banking; and the Governor, Andrew Bailey. Andrew will begin with opening remarks, and then we'll turn to questions. Andrew. Oh, thank you, Katie. Well, today we've held bank rate at 3.75%. The conflict in the Middle East has significantly affected the outlook for UK inflation. The disruption the conflict has caused to energy supplies has led to a sharp rise in global energy prices. CPI inflation increased to 3.3% in March and is likely to be higher later this year. Monetary policy cannot prevent higher global energy prices from affecting the UK economy and inflation. Our job is to set monetary policy to make sure that the effects of the shock do not become embedded into broad-based inflationary pressures, so that inflation falls back to the 2% target and stays there. So, I'll now set out how we expect higher energy prices to affect consumer price inflation, before turning to the appropriate monetary policy response and the scenarios that have informed the Monetary Policy Committee's decision today. As I will explain, where we go from here will depend on the size and duration of the shock to energy prices, itself driven by how the conflict in the Middle East evolves and how those higher energy prices affect consumer prices in the United Kingdom more broadly. Chart 1 shows 12-month inflation in the Consumer Price Index since the start of 2024—that's the blue line—alongside our updated expectations for the rest of the year, which are in the shaded area. Before hostilities broke out in Iran in February, inflation was on track to fall to around the 2% target from April—that's the red line. Recent data releases covering the period immediately before the conflict began suggest this expectation was reasonable. We now project that inflation will rise to a little over 3.5% by the end of the year. This change in the inflation outlook is a direct consequence of the conflict in the Middle East. Chart 2 shows the scale of the increases in global oil and gas prices. Volatility has been high, and the future path of oil and gas prices will depend on how the situation in the Middle East evolves. Uncertainty around any projection of UK inflation is correspondingly high. Higher global oil and gas prices have a direct effect on UK consumer price inflation. Chart 3 shows how these direct effects have changed the outlook for inflation since February. The prices of fuels moved quickly as global oil prices rose, and higher prices for petrol and diesel have already pushed inflation up relative to our expectations before the conflict. Household utility bills will follow, and will mostly be affected when Ofgem's next energy price cap for the third quarter takes effect. At this horizon, higher energy costs will also feed through indirectly to consumer prices as firms pass higher costs through their supply chains. At first, the indirect effects on inflation are likely to be largest for food prices, since food production and distribution are energy-intensive. Further ahead, lower supply and higher prices of fertilizers may also push up on food price inflation through higher costs and lower yields for UK farmers and across the world. There is nothing monetary policy can do to prevent these cost increases from affecting UK businesses and households. The longer the conflict in the Middle East continues, the worse the impact will become. A resolution at the source—reopening the Strait of Hormuz and repairing the damage that has been done to the world's energy infrastructure—can, however, reduce the impact of this negative supply shock to our economy. The task for monetary policy is to ensure that as the adjustment to the supply shock happens, inflation returns sustainably to the 2% target. The 2% target applies at all times. But the Monetary Policy Committee's remit recognizes that attempting to bring inflation back to the target too quickly after a shock like this may cause undesirable volatility in output. Monetary policy must manage a trade-off between the speed with which it brings inflation back to target and the consideration that should be placed on the variability of output. Monetary policy generally looks through the direct effect of energy prices on inflation. It takes time for changes in interest rates to affect the economy and inflation, so the direct effects from an energy price shock may disappear from headline inflation before monetary policy can take effect. In that case, higher interest rates would only push inflation below target once the energy price shock had passed, resulting in undesirable volatility in both inflation and activity. Even if the lags in the effects of interest rate changes were shorter, offsetting the direct effect of an energy price shock would require pushing down core inflation by generating additional slack in the economy. Looking through the direct effects of an energy price shock avoids such output volatility, as the MPC's remit allows for. The remit also indicates that the MPC should manage a trade-off when considering how much to lean against the indirect effects from an energy price shock on inflation. But because they take longer to come through, the argument for looking through the indirect effects is weaker. Monetary policy can look through some of the inflationary impact from indirect effects to avoid undesirable volatility in output. But protracted indirect effects could keep inflation above target for too long unless monetary policy responds. Leaning against indirect effects, monetary policy may also reduce the risk that high inflation becomes embedded through higher inflation expectations and second-round effects. Second-round effects could arise, for example, if a rise in inflation expectations leads workers to bargain more strongly for wage increases and firms raise wages to maintain real pay for their employees, which in turn would increase their costs and could lead them to set higher prices. Monetary policy should not look through such effects, but the size of any second-round effects in addition to the direct and indirect effects is highly uncertain. On the one hand, the larger and more persistent the energy shock is, and the more inflation expectations respond to current inflation, the more likely it is that second-round effects arise. And inflation expectations have risen sharply in response to the current energy price shock. Repeated high inflation episodes over the past five years may have raised people's attentiveness to price increases. On the other hand, the strength of second-round effects will depend on conditions in the labor market and the pricing power of firms given the state of the economy. The UK labor market has loosened, and that trend has continued in recent months. This suggests that workers will be more cautious in wage bargaining. At same time, firms may find it difficult to pass on cost increases to their customers given subdued demand. Higher fuel and utility bills will themselves weigh on consumer demand for other goods and services through lower real incomes and weaker consumer sentiment. The uncertainty of the situation is likely to weigh further on firms' investment intentions. In addition to being highly uncertain, second-round effects build more slowly than direct and indirect effects. That leaves monetary policy with a difficult judgment call. Because interest rate changes take time to take their effect, monetary policy cannot wait for conclusive evidence of the strength of second-round effects. But responding too early may generate undesirable volatility in output, and inflation may drop below the 2% target in the medium term if second-round effects turn out to be smaller than anticipated. To inform its policy deliberations, the MPC has considered three scenarios for possible outcomes for the UK economy and inflation over the next three years. These scenarios illustrate that the path for inflation, conditional on a market-implied path for bank rate in a 15-working-day average up to the 22nd of April, depends crucially on both the size of the energy shock and the extent of second-round effects. Chart 4 illustrates the assumed paths for oil and gas prices in each of the scenarios. In Scenario A, oil and gas prices are assumed to follow market futures curves based on the 15-working-day average to the 22nd of April over the next three years—that's the yellow lines. In Scenario B, oil and gas prices follow market futures for six months and an average of the futures curve and a constant price thereafter—that's the purple line. This assumption reflects a more cautious view of the duration of the conflict and the time it will take to restore energy supplies through the Strait of Hormuz than implied by current market prices. Scenario C goes further to reflect the risk of a continued substantial disruption to supplies through the strait, pushing oil prices to $130 a barrel and gas prices to 210 pence per therm and remaining higher than in Scenarios A and B for the entire projection period. In Scenario A there are no second-round effects, in part because the energy shock is relatively short-lived and in part because it is assumed that workers' bargaining power and firms' pricing power... ...pricing power is too weak for such effects to occur, given slack in the labor market and subdued demand. Moreover, demand falls disproportionately more than would normally be assumed to reflect weak consumer sentiment after another supply shock. In Scenario B, with a more cautious view of how the conflict may play out, the energy price shock is large and persistent enough to lead to modest second-round effects, under the assumption that workers' bargaining and firms' pricing powers are only slightly affected by the slack in the economy. Consumption responds broadly in proportion to what would normally be assumed in projections after an energy price shock in this scenario. Scenario C is more severe. The energy price shock is large and very persistent—and I would emphasize here the persistence in this scenario—and global supply chain cost pressures intensify. Inflation expectations respond strongly to continued elevated inflation, and firms are assumed to have sufficient pricing power for higher wages and costs to feed through more fully to consumer prices, resulting in materially stronger second-round effects. Chart 5 shows the paths of inflation and the output gap—that's a measure of slack in the economy—conditional on a market-implied path for bank rate based on a 15-day average of forward interest rates to the 22nd of April, along with the central projection from February. In Scenario A, that's the yellow lines, inflation falls back quickly once direct and indirect effects have fed through. As the energy shock unwinds and energy prices fall again while economic slack persists, inflation falls below the target at the end of next year. In Scenario B, energy prices fall back more slowly and CPI inflation remains elevated for longer. Inflation then returns gradually to 2% by the second and third year of the projection, but with spare capacity in the economy. In Scenario C, persistently high energy prices and second-round effects combine to push inflation above 6%, with inflation ending the forecast horizon above the 2% target. A materially tighter policy stance than embodied in the market-implied path for bank rate would therefore likely be necessary in Scenario C to counter second-round effects, to ensure that inflation returns sustainably to the 2% target in the medium term. But that is not the case in the other scenarios. As Chart 6 shows, five different illustrative policy rules all point to higher interest rates now than they did in February, given the change in energy prices across all scenarios. But the market-implied path for bank rate has also moved higher by about 55 basis points, measured by the 15-day average of forward interest rates to the 22nd of April compared to the corresponding path to the 26th of January from the February report. Since, in February, market pricing was expecting rates to fall by around 40 basis points this year, the chart shows that for Scenarios A and B, the illustrative interest rate responses would largely be accommodated by reversing out these assumed cuts without further increasing rates. But this would not be the case in Scenario C. Since the end of the 22nd of April, oil prices have risen by more than 20%, gas prices have risen by 8%, and the market-implied path for bank rate has moved higher by up to 35 basis points. But if we think back to early on the 8th of April, we saw similarly sized falls in energy prices and the path for bank rate in the days after the announcement of a US-Iran ceasefire. This volatility illustrates the unpredictability of the current situation, as the markets are trying to assess the implications of the events and commentaries as they unfold. And I would just add, for noting, that the range on the Brent oil price this morning is over $10 a barrel. So, that further illustrates the volatility that we are in. Given the sheer unpredictability and drawing on the evidence from Scenario B, there's a good case for holding rates now. But we must recognize that a prolonged spike in energy prices, as in Scenario C, could lead to a higher bank rate, while also recognizing that a prompt end to the conflict and a reopening of the strait can take us to the more benign scenario, as in Scenario A. So, let me summarize. The MPC's decision to hold bank rate today is based on two key judgments. First, that continued weakness in activity and the labor market is likely to lessen the strength of second-round effects from higher global energy prices. But these effects are likely to be stronger, the larger and more persistent is the rise in global energy prices. Second, that uncertainty about the strength of second-round effects means that monetary policy needs to balance the costs of leaning too little against these effects against the costs of responding too much. The right balance is likely to change depending on how events unfold. The situation remains highly uncertain. We will continue to monitor the situation in the Middle East and how it affects the UK economy and inflation very closely. This includes the size and duration of the energy price shock and its direct effects on UK inflation. It includes the state of the UK economy and how higher costs of energy and other inputs to production pass through to consumer prices. And it includes the potential for second-round effects via wage and price setting, drawing on the full range of sources from economic data, surveys of firms and households, and intelligence from our agents. I'm sorry that's taken a bit longer than usual, but as you could see, there are more scenarios and more to explain. So with that, Clare, Dave, and I will be happy to take your questions. Thank you. Go to Faisal, and then Mehreen, please. Thanks, Governor. Faisal Islam, BBC News. Can you just clarify, in Scenario A or B—so essentially, that the oil price... the uncertainty underlying in the geopolitics—but that it may not necessarily require a rate rise? Is that what you just said? Yeah, so look, obviously, the thing about using policy rules is that there are a whole range of policy rules and you can get a whole range of answers. But as the chart I showed a few minutes ago illustrates, and bearing in mind this point that there was 55 basis points of cuts assumed before this set of events started, the conclusion from that analysis is that there is a good deal of room to accommodate the type of tightening that would be needed in A or B within that range of 55 basis points. And, of course, the curve has moved to reflect that. So, I can't give you a cast-iron assurance that, therefore, there will be no increase in any of those scenarios, sorry. But what I can say to you is that there was a good deal of space available to accommodate that. Mehreen, and then Paul. Mehreen Khan from The Times. Just on the flip side, it looks like, you know, this week, Scenario C—which is your most extreme, severe scenario—looks much closer to where we might be at. There has been a huge swing in oil prices, but we hit the 126 mark today. If in June we are in a world where 126 doesn't look so extreme, do you then have to go into what you say is forceful action, and does that include putting a 50-basis-point rate hike on the table? Well, can I make three points on that, Mehreen, and then Clare may want to come in. And the three points are these. First of all, Scenario C, as I mentioned earlier, is a very persistent scenario, so the oil price remains above $100 a barrel through to the end of next year. That's a key part of Scenario C; it's not just where the price is today. Second point is that gas prices don't respond as much as oil prices. The third part is just to reiterate what I said slightly ad lib a few moments ago. I mean, if we'd had this discussion at 9 o'clock this morning, we were looking at a Brent price well over $120 a barrel. Well, I don't know what it is now, but when I walked into the room, it was over $10 a barrel lower than that. So, that illustrates a) the volatility of prices, and b) that as the MPC, we just cannot stop the music at a particular point in time and say that's it for all time, because it is so volatile. Yeah, I mean, the only thing I'd add is just to make explicit that point that we've set out three illustrative scenarios. They're not exhaustive. They're there to explain, basically, and show how the economy may evolve and how policy might need to react to those. But it's quite likely that the world won't be precisely like any of those, so this is to give a sense of, as I say, how it will flow through the economy potentially and our reaction function. Paul, and then Sue. Paul Kelso, Sky News. Governor, a question about Scenario C, what you might call the worst-case scenario you set out today. Consumers will see potential inflation at 6.2%, interest rates at above 5% again, and they have very recent memories of what that feels like. We've had extraordinarily high inflation, from which many households haven't recovered. I suppose the question many will have is—what they're looking for is a sense of—how likely is that to come to pass? Well, going back to the point I made in response to Mehreen's question, the sheer volatility of what we're looking at makes it, I think, very hard to put probabilities on things. This is one of the problems we're dealing with, so you know, we've been very careful not to put probabilities on these scenarios because it moves around too much. I think the point I would make though, and I think this reiterates something I said earlier, is very important: the longer this general state goes on, the more difficult it becomes. I'm afraid that is something we can fairly easily deduce. So, look through the volatility. The longer this problem goes on and the longer the disruption to energy supplies goes on, other things equal, it's likely that energy prices will stay higher, and the more difficult the scenario—the scenario we're in. But the one thing I would add is that the numbers in that scenario show what happens under the market path, so they assume the market path. If we were in a scenario where we saw much higher inflationary pressures like that, we would of course have to take policy action to lean against them, and so that would bring inflation down. Sue in the middle, and then Joel up the top there. Thank you, Sue Chan from The Telegraph. Just following on from what Mehreen asked: if we are in a situation where oil prices remain persistently high, what does forceful look like? Are we back in, you know, a 2022 situation where we saw 50- or 75-basis-point rate hikes? And given your comments just now, when will you have enough info to help you decide that rate hikes might be needed? Well, I'm not going to put numbers on what forceful looks like, other than to say I think the point was made very deliberately that if we see that type of very difficult scenario emerging, then it's important that we obviously do respond quickly to it. But I don't think it's wise to put numbers on it. Your question is a very good question: when will we see it? And it goes back to something I said in my opening remarks. I mean, the challenge for us is the second-round effect point, because judging the second rounds is crucial here. Now, of course, the problem is that the second-round effects emerge much more slowly, and that's in the nature of it, really, because they're the sort of follow-on, if you like. It would be a mistake to wait to see the second-round effects before acting, because then it would be too late. So, it's a judgment that we will have to make at every meeting, and this is why I stressed right at the end of my remarks that we will be looking at a very broad range of evidence. Some of this will be quantitative, but some of it will be qualitative, based particularly on what our agents are telling us as to how it is affecting the economy. And, you know, I would say from my visits around the country—and I'll be out and about tomorrow—we do start in a position where firms very typically say to me they have been experiencing difficulty in passing costs on into prices. But obviously, as this shock is a new feature, that was the sort of ex-ante state, if you like. Obviously, they are now faced with an abrupt energy price shock, and so we will have to then judge carefully how they both can and do respond to it. And just on that, if I can just add in terms of one way of thinking of the scenarios—there are two dimensions to them. One, as Andrew's been emphasizing, is the persistence of energy prices. And the second dimension is the strength of second-round effects. The persistence of energy prices will obviously be more and more apparent as time goes past. The extent of second-round effects will be much more of a judgment as the evidence emerges over time. Joel up at the back there, and then Sam. Joel Hills from ITV News. Given that a key judgment is on higher prices and how they feed through and create second-round effects, what exactly will you be watching over the weeks ahead to form that judgment? And with people feeling worse off, isn't it natural that they'll ask for higher pay? Well, let me take that in two parts. On the first part, I mean what I did was set out very deliberately a sort of sequence of things that you look for. So, you start obviously with the first-round effects, which are energy prices themselves. Then we talked about indirect effects, which is how it passes through into the cost of production of other things. And I did put quite an emphasis on food because I think one of the things we learned four years ago was what a considerable share of the cost of final food is energy—I mean, processed food is energy, so that's important. I also emphasize food because energy and food are the two most salient things in the consumption basket for people, because they're the things we buy most often and most regularly, and we can compare prices more easily from time to time, therefore. And then you get the second-round effects. So, in terms of what we will be watching, those will be the things we'll be watching. On wage setting, wages are set in labor markets. Let me make an important point here: labor markets at the moment are functioning efficiently. There's no reason in any of the very considerable analysis that our staff do on the labor markets to think that pay is being set as anything other than efficiently. Now, it will, I should say, be some time before we really do get a read on pay, and the reason is the seasonal cycle of pay setting, because a large part of the pay settlements are done in spring. So, we've actually passed through that period for this year. You're probably immediately going to compare that back to things I said four years ago, and I would just make the following point: coming out of COVID, the labor market was much less efficient. It was not functioning as well. A lot of work our staff did on job matching was showing there had been a real increase in the inefficiency of the labor market in that sense, but we're not in that place today. You know, wages will be set in markets. Sam, right there, and Josiah. Thanks very much. Governor, you put in your statement some weight on Scenario B, I think. In terms of the inflation forecast based on the market curve going into the meeting, that puts inflation on target at 2% along the inflation horizon. But that implied market curve had at least a couple of rate increases. That was the curve at the time which generated that forecast. So, is it reasonable, therefore, to expect some tightening from here under Scenario B? And the minutes themselves put a lot of weight on higher, tighter market conditions taking some insurance out in terms of adverse inflation outcomes. But at what point do you need to actually validate the tightening in the financial markets by raising interest rates? Thanks. Well, I'll let Clare come in as well on the final point. Let me start on Scenario B. One of the reasons that I think one element of Scenario B is more plausible than Scenario A is the energy price profile, and particularly, probably, the gas price profile. Because Scenario A—the market curve—I think assumes that, let's be very optimistic for a moment and say the conflict is over tomorrow, it assumes quite a rapid return to pre-existing supply conditions. Now, that's not really consistent with what we hear, and I'm sure you hear from people who have looked at the damage, for instance, that was done to the gas production infrastructure in Qatar, where I think the view we hear is, look, it is going to take quite a while to repair that—some pretty key parts were damaged. So, Scenario B, in part, is motivated by building that into the assumption. Now, on the point you raise about interest rates: look, I think the right decision today is to hold. But it's an active hold in that sense. It's not a passive wait-and-see hold; it's a deliberately active hold. Because, as I said earlier in response to the earlier question, there is a range of scenarios in our view where the headroom we've got—because, as I say, it's quite clear that the prospect of cuts, which we were after all talking about at this press conference three months ago, is not on the table now—so that headroom could take it up, but we can't give you any assurance on that front. Clare, did you want to...? No, only to add, I mean, as we've mentioned, we haven't put probabilities on these scenarios. You couldn't, I mean, for obvious reasons. So, different members will think differently about how they want to weigh the risks of a higher-inflation, more persistent scenario, and whether or not they want to adjust policy in response to it. Josiah, who is right in the middle there. Yep. And then Suban. Josiah Popovic, Central Banking. So my question is just with regard to Alan Taylor's comment that given neutral at 3%... [Andrew Bailey:] Sorry, can you just talk up a bit? The acoustics are terrible in this room for when you're sitting here, anyway. Just talk up a bit, that would be really helpful. Thank you. [Josiah Popovic:] I'll start again. Can you hear me now? Great. So, Josiah Popovic, Central Banking. I just wanted to ask about Alan Taylor's comment that, given neutral at 3%, it makes sense to hold. I just wanted to ask where that figure comes from? Is that a current staff assessment or a hypothetical, and what are the Governor's thoughts? I think that's Alan's estimate of neutral. It's in his paragraph, so it's his estimate of neutral. In the past, we've published a range of estimates. Clare and I have talked about them extensively at these press conferences. But on different models and different approaches, you can get anything from 2% to 4%. He's been very clear and consistent that he sees neutral as being around 3%, so that's very much his estimate. I think we've said before, some members of the MPC are more confident in their estimates of the neutral rate, and some of us—some of the rest of us, and I include myself in this group—are less confident about precise estimates. That's fine; reasonable people can disagree on that. Suban, who is right behind you. Thank you, Governor. What evidence would you need to see to vote for a rate hike, and are you close to voting for one? You said you've put some weight on Scenario C, even if not as much as on Scenario B. What would you need to see that pushes you to Scenario C, and is that evidence likely to come before the next MPC meeting? What would push us to Scenario C, or give greater weight to Scenario C, I think—well, look, I think a lot of it does hang on what happens in the Gulf. Because Scenario C is, in the first instance, motivated by more persistent first-round effects. It also has stronger second-round effects in it, but as I was saying earlier, I think in response to Sue's question, those take longer to appear on the scene, as it were. So, in the first instance, there's a difficult judgment in there about how long this is going to go on for, and that's because persistence is an important element of Scenario C. What I would emphasize, though—the reason this persistence point is important—is that if you look back to four years ago, energy prices went up, and particularly gas prices went up, but they came down quite quickly, actually. Scenario C does not look like that; it's actually a different shape to the profile, and it's got a much longer downslope on it. Heather, and then John-Paul. Heather Stewart from The Guardian. Governor, you were at the Prime Minister's COBRA meeting, I think earlier this week, about managing the impact of the crisis. I just wonder, what is the role of the Bank in that? Are you advising about the inflationary impact of potential policies, for example, or should we infer that there are concerns about the financial stability impact of what's going on? What's your role there? Well, my role—and obviously, I have to be quite careful about my role given it's an interest-rate-setting week—is to really, I think, outline the macroeconomic framework. There was a very short record or note of the meeting issued, and it was merely to, in a sense, outline that framework. By the way, the meeting was confidential, so I'm not going to talk in any detail. But you probably won't be surprised to know that I laid out first-round effects, indirect effects, and second-round effects in terms of how it works in principle. John-Paul. Hello, John-Paul Ford Rojas from the Daily Mail. Can you hear me all right? Yeah. A couple of scenarios: Scenario A, which you've just sort of implied is rather unlikely given the energy profile, seems to be the only one under which it's likely that interest rates may not need to rise. Therefore, does it look almost certain—should people who are watching and listening to this take it as the message—to get ready for rate hikes? And, sorry, one further thing in relation to that: Scenario C, at 5.25%, the report suggests that that would raise the risk of a recession. Is a recession a price that people will have to pay? So, to answer your first question: no. Scenario A... you're right, I, for my own part, think that the market curve on energy prices, particularly on gas, is too optimistic in terms of the time it would take to restore supply. But Scenario A has other features to it, and in particular, this question about the second-round effects. The second-round effects are to do, obviously, with the length, duration, and impact of the energy price shock.
