So, the shorter this whole thing is, the less we would expect the second-round effects to be. So, Scenario A is not there for decoration; it's very much a part of the scene, in that sense. And as I said earlier, a good part of Scenario B would be accommodated by the headroom that we already had. So, I would very much give the message: no, it is not the case that we're giving some sort of slightly clandestine message that interest rates are going to go up, notwithstanding what we've decided today. As I said earlier, today is an active hold in that sense. Do I think, therefore, that the interest rate curve is in the wrong place? No, I don't, because there are risks around this. And as Andrew emphasized up top in his opening remarks, and as we emphasize again in the monetary policy statement, you know, we will act as necessary to ensure that inflation does get back to the 2% target sustainably in the medium term. So, that's the key message. But this, you know, we have really drawn attention—and Andrew has consistently in recent weeks—to the continued looseness of the labor market. And that that is a very different position for the labor market than we were in in 2022. As for a recession, what I would say is when you look at the analysis that we presented using market curves, scenario C does not deliver a recession. Now, as I said earlier, there's a whole range of policy rules you can attach to these things, and you can get a whole range of results. There are some policy rules which will deliver you some short periods of negative growth, but the basic message is this actually delivers a profile of slower growth. Tom and Dearbail. Tom Rees from Bloomberg News. As you've suggested, what we've seen in the first two months of this conflict is actually just a lot of volatility in prices, you know, crude going from $80 a barrel to 126. If that extreme volatility lasts much longer, do any of these scenarios capture something where prices just fluctuate quite a lot? And I was just also wondering, how much has your thinking changed in the last week? Have things—has the outlook got a lot worse over the last week from what we've seen in energy markets? Well, two things there. Thanks, Tom. First of all, of course, volatility is hard for all sorts of reasons. It's hard, you know, when you're taking decisions. But you've also got to think about how volatility feeds through into people's expectations. This is a, you know, this is a more problematic element to it in a sense because obviously people are quite rightly saying, well, I don't know what the—I mean, the first starting point is: what is the price today? As I said earlier, you know, if you checked into the Brent oil price at some point this morning, you would get very different numbers depending on what time you checked in at. So that's a further complication as to how people form their expectations. On your point about the last week or so, I mean, you look at the oil price curve. You had a—you had a, obviously it went up, then you had a period when it was coming back down again, and now it's gone back up again. All of that hangs off, frankly, what's going on in the Gulf and what's being said about—even more so actually, sometimes, what's being said about what's going on in the Gulf. So, you know, I think certainly for myself, I think we have to be quite careful here. I don't—I don't sort of pin my reading of the world on one particular period of time because, frankly, we've seen such changes over the period since this whole conflict began. Dearbail and then Maurizio. Hello, can you hear me? Yes. Okay, sorry, never usually can. I just wanted to raise a point in the minutes. It talks about the indirect effects of high energy prices on production costs, likely to affect food prices particularly. How much do you think that is going to affect food prices? And it says inflation is expected to rise somewhat further in Q4, so does that mean that Christmas dinner is going to be more expensive? Well, of course, unfortunately, a lot can happen between now and Christmas. I mean, just to go back—go back to, as I said, go back four years ago. Obviously you can't generalize because different foods get made in different ways. But, you know, when we looked at it four years ago, there were quite a few processed foods where 30% of the cost is energy. So obviously that's significant. But then let's consider, as I say, the pass-through. I mean, the question of course in the current conditions—current conditions for demand, current conditions in the labor market, and what firms are telling us about their ability to pass costs on into prices, which has definitely become more difficult. There's no question about that, we get that all around the country. You know, I think it remains an open question, therefore, what the impact on final food prices is going to be. I mean, that remains to be seen. And I would just re-emphasize also the point I did allude to in the opening remarks about the other effect on food, which is the impact of the problem in fertilizer supply, which again will of course come through over time. Although, you know, I know farmers are already telling us of course that they're having to make decisions on what they think fertilizer availability is going to be as to what, you know, what they plant. We put a bit more detail than in the minutes in the monetary policy report on food prices. So if you look on page 14, you'll see that we say that our short-term forecasts out to September '26 takes them up to 4.6%. But then the reports that our agents are getting suggest that they might go up further to a range of 6 to 7% by the end of the year. But that's based on reports as of now, late April, and as Andrew says, a lot can happen. Maurizio here and then Ritika up at the back there. Hi, Maurizio Arancorn from City AM. Governor, after the last meeting you said that markets were getting ahead of themselves. And then now you're sort of saying it's an active hold, and I think the minutes report also say that tighter financial markets have become an insurance to sort of inflation. Do you risk sort of actually confusing the traders out there in the city about what the Bank's next move is? And I suppose, what's your response? Let me—that's a very fair question. Let me give a little bit of sort of how we got from the last meeting to here, I think that's helpful. We obviously saw quite a move in markets at the time of our last announcement. And I think it comes back to earlier questions, actually. You know, we were very conscious—I was very conscious—that we were expected to cut rates this year. And indeed, look, it wasn't—you know, I go back to the last press conference, I said it a number of times that I thought that was my central expectation. And effectively, in the last minutes of the last meeting, we took that off the table. And I think, you know, we had to do that because frankly, as you'll see from the material we've presented this time, it's just not realistic. Sadly, it is not realistic. Now, we know that markets, and this is totally understandable, were pretty substantially positioned based on what we had been saying. That's understandable, totally understandable. And the consequence of taking those cuts off the table was quite a sharp move in the curve. We were—I was not surprised by that because, as I say, that in a sense was sadly a fact that followed on the outbreak of the conflict. We did get to a point, I remember, when four or five increases were being priced in for this year, I think. And I did make this point: I think you're getting ahead of yourselves at this point, and I would stick to that point. And what you see this mor... look, what you see in the much larger amount of material we've presented today is, I think, a very broad analysis. As we've all said a number of times in response to questions, the scenarios show you that there are paths through this where we don't have to increase rates, and there are scenarios, I'm afraid, where we do have to increase rates. And that's where we are today. So look, as I think we've said and I've said in things I've said today, it's an active hold today. Do I think, therefore, that the interest rate curve is in the wrong place? No, I don't, because there are risks around this. Ritika up at the back and then Francine. Ritika Gupta, CNBC. So there's questions about if you wait for the hard data whether that means you're going to be behind the curve. So if you did go ahead with an interest rate hike but then you wanted to reverse that—say there was a quicker resolution to the war than you anticipated—how would you then communicate that to the public? Would you see that as a high cost to reverse that decision? Well, it's a good question. First of all, I mean, we are in such an uncertain world. I mean, you're right to say that you don't want to be the people who increase interest rates and then the next week this whole thing gets settled and everything gets back to normal, and you end up sort of thinking, "Why did we do that?" Well, unfortunately, that's the world we're in. It's a very volatile world, so we have to recognize that could happen. I don't think any of us can predict that, but it's a world we could be in. And I think the important thing is that we have to take this decision over, you know, at every meeting. We have to weigh up everything. I think I made this distinction, and I'll make it again, between what I called an active hold and a wait-and-see. Wait-and-see would be sort of speaking to your point really, of saying, "Well, you know, things could happen, let's hang on," as opposed to taking the active decision and saying, "Well, this is our assessment of where we are at the moment." There's a range of possible outcomes; we've tried to capture that in scenarios. What's our best judgment of what we do today, and then we have to stick to it and face the consequences of it. I think all I could say is I think they'd be reasonably easy to explain if that happened. If peace broke out a week later, I think it wouldn't be too difficult to explain. Thank you. Tim Wallace at the Daily Telegraph. Governor, what's your message to businesses when they're considering their prices? Do they need to resist the temptation to put up prices, or the pressure to put up prices in the face of this shock? And also to pick up on what you said about the labour market before, lots of private sector companies have set their wages for this year. They've had the increases already. The public sector is not quite the same. There's still a lot of negotiation going on, strike threats, things like that. Do the public sector wages feed into your forecast? Well, for businesses, it's really the same point, Tim, as I made about wages. I mean, prices are set in markets. So businesses will have to obviously look at the markets they're operating in, they have to look at the costs that they are obviously seeing and receiving, and they'll set their prices in markets. And so, our judgment of second-round effects is really a judgment on how those markets operate. On public sector wages, look, this is an issue for the government. You know, I don't think they need—I think they're very aware of the situation obviously in the world. They're very aware of the impact on energy pricing, on inflation, but those decisions are taken by the government, not by us. Delphine. Thanks. I was interested to see you'd flagged the risk of a much sharper rise in unemployment in some sectors than others, hospitality in particular. Wondered if you could talk a little bit more about how plausible you think that is and what it would mean for your assessment of whether the labour market's working efficiently? Hmm. Yeah, do you want to start? Yeah, sure. So we've done quite a lot of analysis that looks at different sectors of the labour market. I mean, one of the big questions prior to this was: what is going on in the labour market, and how should we think about the different ways to understand what might be going on structurally and cyclically? And so, could we get a handle on it by looking at that sectorally? We do see quite a big dispersion between sectors and, actually, at the moment the difference across sectors is higher than it typically is. And as you say, we've seen particular impacts and particular weakness in hospitality, and in some professional services as well. We've done a lot of analysis of whether or not that is a sort of leading indicator of a bigger problem, and it's not clear that it is in that sense. But obviously there are risks on either side of that, and it's something that we're monitoring really carefully. Have we got everyone? Is that you, Sue? Yeah, go Sue. Just a final one then. Looking at chart 1.10 on page 21, can you just describe how you see the outlook for living standards going forward this year? It's falling. Fair point. Well, I think you can see what it shows, which is we have had periods where we've had positive real income growth and we've had periods where it's gone below the line. I mean, again, what it shows is that it's coming down—it has been coming down for a little while, but from a fairly positive position. Honestly, I think what happens hereafter depends upon a lot of the debate or the discussion we've been having in the last hour, which is the balance of what the picture is on inflation and what the picture is on the increase in wages and incomes. And as I say, that really gets us back to this question about second-round effects. It's also a negative supply shock. Yeah. That's what we're dealing with, and that is negative to the economy, and it is negative to real incomes through the economy. It's negative to the UK terms of trade; that's it. You can see in the scenarios just how the difference happens. The difference in terms of the impact on real incomes will be the fact that obviously the larger the shock is, the greater the impact. But it is, you know, it is going to be painful. Well, it comes back to this point about size and persistence.

WATCH LIVE: European Central Bank Press Conference with President Christine Lagarde

CNBC International Live(482.0K)·54:14·421 views·2026-04-29
Christine LagardePresident, European Central Bank
TL;DRThe ECB Governing Council kept its three key interest rates unchanged, adopting a strict data-dependent, meeting-by-meeting approach to return medium-term inflation to its 2% target amidst intensified geopolitical risks, rising energy prices, and slower economic growth.
TL;DR The ECB Governing Council kept its three key interest rates unchanged, adopting a strict data-dependent, meeting-by-meeting approach to return medium-term inflation to its 2% target amidst intensified geopolitical risks, rising energy prices, and slower economic growth....

TL;DR

The ECB Governing Council kept its three key interest rates unchanged, adopting a strict data-dependent, meeting-by-meeting approach to return medium-term inflation to its 2% target amidst intensified geopolitical risks, rising energy prices, and slower economic growth.

***

Monetary Policy Stance and the June Decision Framework

  • Interest Rate Decision: The Governing Council unanimously decided to keep the three key ECB interest rates unchanged.
      • Policy Rationale: President Christine Lagarde characterized the choice as "an informed decision on the basis of yet insufficient information," noting that while hard data aligns with March projections, extreme geopolitical uncertainty requires the Council to wait for June's updated projections and scenario analyses.
      • Rate Path Flexibility: The ECB is not pre-committing to any specific rate path. Future decisions will remain strictly data-dependent, determined on a meeting-by-meeting basis.
  • Reaction Function: The ECB's policy reaction remains anchored on three core pillars:
      • Maintaining a symmetric 2% medium-term inflation target.
      • Calibrating the forcefulness of policy response based on the size and sustainability of any target deviations.
      • Assessing incoming economic data, underlying inflation dynamics, and the strength of monetary policy transmission.
  • The June Outlook: The next policy meeting in six weeks will leverage fresh data on wage settlements, collective agreements, corporate profit margins, and commodity price trends to reassess whether further tightening or adjustments are necessary.

Macroeconomic Outlook and Risk Profiles

  • Growth Performance and Projections: The euro area's economic growth is slowing but avoids stagnation.
      • Real GDP: Grew by 0.1% in Q1 2026 (preliminary flash estimate), driven primarily by domestic demand and a resilient labor market.
      • Growth Outlook: The ECB projects GDP growth of 0.8% for 2026, rising to 1.2% and 1.3% in subsequent years.
      • Stagflation Dismissed: Lagarde explicitly rejected "stagflation" as an accurate label for the current environment, pointing to low unemployment (6.2% in March) and positive growth forecasts, which contrast sharply with the persistent stagnation and high unemployment of the 1970s.
  • Geopolitical Downside Risks: The ongoing war in the Middle East represents a major drag on economic sentiment, supply chain logistics, and trade. Prolonged closure of key shipping routes could force production cuts due to critical input shortages.
  • Inflation Dynamics and Upside Risks: Inflation rose to 3% in April (up from 2.6% in March and 1.9% in February), driven heavily by energy costs.
      • Energy Price Spike: Energy price inflation jumped to 10.9% in April, compared to 5.1% in March, following Middle East disruptions.
      • Core Inflation: Inflation excluding food and energy decreased slightly to 2.2% (down from 2.3% in March), supported by a decline in services inflation to 3.0%.
      • Second-Round Effects: The ECB is closely monitoring whether high energy costs spill over into broader wage-setting and long-term inflation expectations, though current surveys show no signs of major second-round wage-price spirals.

Financial Sector Dynamics and Credit Conditions

  • Liquidity and Operational Framework: Eurosystem central bank reserves have decreased from their peak to just under €2.6 trillion.
      • Abundance of Liquidity: Despite the decline, Lagarde emphasized that the banking system still possesses significant excess liquidity, and no new liquidity tools are currently under discussion.
      • Framework Review: The Eurosystem is actively preparing a timetable and methodology to calibrate its operational framework, ensuring it remains fit for purpose and supportive of financial stability.
  • Tightening Financial Conditions: Market-based financing costs have risen since the outbreak of Middle East hostilities.
      • Debt and Lending Rates: The cost of market-based debt issuance rose to 3.9% in March (up from 3.5% in February). Prior to the conflict, bank lending rates to firms and mortgage rates stood at 3.5% and 3.4% respectively.
      • Credit Supply and Demand: The Q1 Bank Lending Survey indicates that credit standards for corporate loans have tightened due to banks' heightened risk perceptions. Concurrently, corporate demand for loans—particularly for fixed investment—declined.
  • European Banking Integration: Vice-President Luis de Guindos reiterated the ECB’s support for a single banking jurisdiction within Europe, advocating for the free flow of capital and liquidity across member states to bolster systemic resilience.

Fiscal Policy and Structural Recommendations

  • The "3 Ts" of Fiscal Support: The ECB urges euro area governments to ensure all fiscal measures implemented in response to energy price shocks remain:
      • Temporary: Avoiding permanent deficits.
      • Targeted: Directed exclusively at vulnerable populations.
      • Tailored: Designed to maintain price signals that encourage energy conservation.
      • GDP Impact: Currently committed fiscal support measures across the euro area represent roughly 0.1% of GDP.
  • Structural Reforms: To reduce dependence on fossil fuels and bolster productivity, the Governing Council stressed the importance of accelerating the energy transition, completing the European savings and investment union, and simplifying single market regulations.
  • Strategic Autonomy: Swift adoption of the digital euro regulation is highlighted as vital for Europe's payment system competitiveness, strategic autonomy, and financial integration.

***

Notable Quotes

"If I was to summarize for you what we decided today, I would say that we made an informed decision on the basis of yet insufficient information." — Christine Lagarde, ECB President
"We project growth of 0.8% followed by 1.2% followed by 1.3%. I wouldn't call that stagnation, sorry. It's lower growth, granted, in '26, but we're not in stagnation, let alone recession." — Christine Lagarde, ECB President
"We are in favor of a single jurisdiction for the European banks with free flow of capital and liquidity." — Luis de Guindos, ECB Vice-President
Return inflation to our 2% target in the medium term. Good afternoon and welcome to our press conference with President Lagarde and Vice-President de Guindos. My name is Wolfgang Proissl. As always, we are also joined by journalists who are connected via video. When I give them the floor, they should please unmute and put on their camera. And with that, I hand over to you, President Lagarde. Please. Thank you very much, Wolfgang. And good morning, afternoon, good day to all of you. The Vice-President and I welcome you to our press conference. The Governing Council today decided to keep the three key ECB interest rates unchanged. While the incoming information has been broadly consistent with our previous assessment of the inflation outlook, the upside risks to inflation and the downside risks to growth have intensified. We are committed to setting monetary policy to ensure that inflation stabilizes at our 2% target in the medium term. The war in the Middle East has led to a sharp increase in energy prices, pushing up inflation and weighing on economic sentiment. The implications of the war for medium-term inflation and economic activity will depend on the intensity, the duration of the energy price shock, and the scale of its indirect and second-round effects. The longer the war continues and the longer energy prices remain high, the stronger is the likely impact on broader inflation and the economy. We remain well positioned to navigate the current uncertainty. The euro area entered this period of surging energy prices with inflation at around our 2% target, and the economy has shown resilience over recent quarters. Longer-term inflation expectations remain well anchored, although inflation expectations over shorter horizons have moved up significantly. We will closely monitor the situation and follow a data-dependent and meeting-by-meeting approach to determining the appropriate monetary policy stance. In particular, our interest rate decisions will be based on our assessment of the inflation outlook and the risks surrounding it in light of the incoming economic and financial data, as well as the dynamics of underlying inflation and the strength of monetary policy transmission. We are not pre-committing to a particular rate path. The decisions taken today are set out in a press release that is available on our website. And I will now outline in more detail how we see the economy and inflation developing, and will then explain our assessment of financial and monetary conditions. The euro area economy was showing some momentum when the current turbulence started. Real GDP grew by 0.1% in the first quarter of '26, according to Eurostat's preliminary flash estimate. Domestic demand remains the main driver of growth, supported by a resilient labour market. However, the economic outlook is highly uncertain and will depend on how long the war in the Middle East lasts and how strongly it affects energy and other commodity markets, as well as global supply chains. The incoming information suggests that the conflict is weighing on economic activity. Surveys point to slowing growth, and consumers and businesses have become less confident about the future since the war began. Longer delivery times and rising input prices suggest supply chains are coming under pressure. Looking ahead, high energy costs are expected to continue to weigh on real incomes, making households and firms more reluctant to consume and invest. While unemployment remained close to historical lows in March at 6.2%, labour demand has cooled further. At the same time, households are still benefiting from a solid financial position and investment should continue to be underpinned by governments spending more on defense and infrastructure and by firms increasingly investing in new digital technologies. This favourable starting point provides some cushioning against the fallout from the war. The Governing Council highlights the urgent need to strengthen the euro area economy while maintaining sound public finances. Fiscal responses to the energy price shock should be temporary, targeted and tailored. Reforms to enhance the euro area's growth potential and accelerate the energy transition to reduce reliance on fossil fuels are more vital than ever. Completing the savings and investment union is key to funding innovation, supporting the green and digital transitions, and improving productivity. The digital euro and tokenised wholesale central bank money will enhance Europe's strategic autonomy, competitiveness and financial integration, and will boost innovation in payments. It is thus essential to swiftly adopt the regulation on the establishment of the digital euro. Simplifying and harmonising rules across the EU's single market will help European firms grow faster. Inflation rose to 3% in April from 2.6% in March and 1.9% in February. The rise has been driven by surging energy prices caused by the war in the Middle East. Energy price inflation jumped to 10.9% in April after 5.1% in March. Food price inflation edged up to 2.5% in April. Inflation excluding energy and food decreased to 2.2% from 2.3% in March, reflecting a fall in services inflation, which declined to 3% from 3.2% in March. Goods inflation went up to 0.8% from 0.5% in March. Indicators of underlying inflation have changed little over recent months. For now, the ECB's wage tracker and surveys on wage expectations continue to indicate easing labour costs in the course of '26. At the same time, surveys indicate an increase in other cost components and in selling price expectations. Inflation expectations have moved up significantly over shorter horizons. Most measures of longer-term inflation expectations stand at around 2%, supporting the stabilisation of inflation around target in the medium term. The increase in energy prices will keep inflation well above 2% in the near term. As the period of high energy prices extends, the likely impact on broader inflation through indirect and second-round effects intensifies. We will therefore closely monitor the size and persistence of the energy price surge and how it feeds through to price and wage setting, inflation expectations, and overall economic dynamics. The risks to the growth outlook are to the downside. The war in the Middle East remains a downside risk to the euro area economy, adding to the volatile global policy environment. Prolonged disruption of the supply of energy could increase energy prices further and for longer than currently expected. These factors would erode incomes and make firms and households more reluctant to invest and spend. The drag on growth would intensify if the closure of major shipping routes were to cause acute shortages of key inputs that forced euro area firms to curtail output. A worsening of global financial market sentiment could further dampen demand. Additional frictions in international trade could exacerbate supply chain disruptions, reduce exports and weaken consumption and investment. Other geopolitical tensions, in particular Russia's unjustified war against Ukraine, remain a major source of uncertainty. By contrast, growth could turn out to be higher if the economy proved to be more adaptable to the disruption caused by the war in the Middle East, or if the conflict were resolved more quickly than currently expected. Moreover, planned defense and infrastructure spending, reforms to enhance productivity, and euro area firms adopting new technologies may drive up growth by more than expected. New trade agreements and a deeper integration of the single market could also boost growth beyond current expectations. The risks to the inflation outlook are to the upside. If energy prices were to rise by more than and for longer than currently expected, euro area inflation would increase further. This could be reinforced and become more persistent if higher energy prices were to spill over by more than expected to other prices and to wages, if longer-term inflation expectations were to rise in response, or if global supply chains were disrupted more broadly. Ongoing trade tensions could also give rise to more fragmented global supply chains, curtail the supply of critical raw materials and worsen capacity constraints in the euro area economy. By contrast, inflation could turn out to be lower if the economic effects of the war in the Middle East proved to be more short-lived than currently expected, or if indirect and second-round effects proved less pronounced. More volatile and risk-averse financial markets could weigh on demand and thereby lower inflation as well. The war in the Middle East has caused significant volatility in global financial markets. Overall, financial conditions remain tighter than before the war. The cost of issuing market-based debt rose to 3.9% in March from 3.5% in February. Bank lending rates to firms, based on data recorded prior to the war, edged down to 3.5% in February, while mortgage rates remained at 3.4%. The annual growth rate of bank lending to firms increased to 3.2% in March from 3% in February, while the growth rate of corporate bond issuance fell to 3.9% from 4.5% in February. Credit standards for loans to firms tightened in the first quarter, as reported in our latest bank lending survey for the euro area. This tightening was due to banks becoming more concerned about the economic risks faced by their customers. Demand for loans to firms decreased slightly in the first quarter, especially for fixed investment. Mortgage lending grew by 3% in March after 3.1% in February, amid a small tightening in credit standards and unchanged demand. So, in conclusion, the Governing Council today decided to keep the three key ECB interest rates unchanged. We are committed to setting monetary policy to ensure that inflation stabilises at our 2% target in the medium term. We will follow a data-dependent and meeting-by-meeting approach to determining the appropriate monetary policy stance. Our interest rate decisions will be based on our assessment of the inflation outlook and the risks surrounding it in light of the incoming economic and financial data, as well as the dynamics of underlying inflation and the strength of monetary policy transmission. We are not pre-committing to a particular rate path. In any case, we stand ready to adjust all of our instruments within our mandate to ensure that inflation stabilises sustainably at our medium-term target and to preserve the smooth functioning of monetary policy transmission. And we are now ready to take your questions. And the first question goes to Mark Schroers of Bloomberg. Mark, please. Yes, thank you. President Lagarde, the statement says that the upside risks to inflation and the downside risks to growth have intensified. Where does this leave the ECB? Where does this new risk assessment leave the ECB between its baseline, its adverse and the severe scenario? And what does it mean for monetary policy ahead? And related to that, would you say that a rate hike in June, as expected by markets and most economists, is indeed the most likely outcome unless there is a quick end to the war and energy prices fall back quickly and significantly? Thank you. I think we would all wish that there was a quick end to the war. But looking at our monetary policy and the decision that we made today, we decided to keep the three interest rates unchanged. And to address your question, I'm not going to tell you whether we are closer to the baseline, scenario one, or scenario two, because what we have debated over the course of yesterday and today with the Governing Council is where we are relative to the baseline. The conclusion is that we are certainly moving away from the baseline. Where to exactly, and at what point in between the baseline and other scenarios, I am not sure is the most relevant assessment. We are moving away from the baseline, and what is critically important is the impact that energy prices will have. I said last time in March that it would be a question of the duration, the depth, and the propagation. And this is clearly what we need to better assess. If I was to summarize for you what we decided today, I would say that we made an informed decision on the basis of yet insufficient information. So why do I say it's an informed decision? It's because we debated at length and in depth various options. We debated the decision that we have unanimously taken today, but we also debated at length and in depth a decision to possibly hike. So that was debated amongst all governors. And I'm sure that you will meet some governors who will argue both sides or maybe one side of both proposals. So it was really a deeply discussed monetary policy stance, of which the outcome was a unanimous decision to leave the three interest rates unchanged. And I'm saying that it's an informed decision based on insufficient information. It is caused by the fact that while the hard data is broadly in line with our projection and what we discussed here back in March, there is such uncertainty that we need to understand and revisit all of that at our next monetary policy meeting. And we believe that given the position we're in—we are saying in the monetary policy statement that we started from a good position and we were well positioned to navigate—given that position, we believe that these six weeks will be the right time to assess the developments. We need to understand in particular the outcome, possibly, of the conflict, or if there is no outcome, that in of itself will be informative. This is in order to make an informed decision on verified and revisited information that we will receive in the next six months. That's the clearest way, I hope it was clear, that I can describe the really good discussions that we had both yesterday and today. Next question to Annetta Weissbach of CNBC. Annetta, please. President Lagarde, thank you for taking my questions. I have a question on whether you have also discussed that stagflationary scenario in the euro area and how that discussion went. And also in the US, they are starting to also think about shrinking the balance sheet instead of using interest rates as a monetary policy instrument. Do you look into using other instruments as well instead of just hiking to alleviate the pressure from the price pressure and also help perhaps the economy? Thank you. Well, thank you for your two questions. On the first one, you know, we take the view that the word—I mean, it's quite popular to talk about stagflation, and it flags a lot of anxiety and all the rest of it—but we have determined that stagflation is the right characterization of what happened in the seventies. From our perspective, we think that it's better to park it in the seventies, given the facts that we have at the moment. You know, it's a completely different situation. In the seventies, you had inflation continuing, continuing, continuing at a sustainable and solid pace. You had very high unemployment. You had a monetary and fiscal framework that had nothing to do with what we have at the moment. So we don't apply stagflation, that flashy term, to the circumstances that we have because we really think that it's associated with the seventies situation. On the tools, you know, we have a whole series of tools in our toolbox. But certainly from our perspective, and given the data that we have and the kind of shock that we're facing, we believe that the interest rate is still the best tool that we can use and that we have debated in the course of yesterday and today. Thank you. Francesco Canepa of Reuters. Francesco, please. Thank you very much. So, first question about your reaction function. Could you unpack a little the relative weight that you attach to growth and inflation in your reaction function? And second about the scenarios. Did you update them, and if so, what do they look like now? And why, if I may, do you not think it relevant to say where we are now compared to the baseline and the two scenarios? Thanks. I'm so pleased that you mentioned the reaction function because, you know, we spent quite a lot of time working it out, finding consensus around the reaction function. And as you know, because you are a frequent reader and observer of what we do, the reaction function has been very clearly spelled out in our strategy review that we published in '25. I have tried to elaborate on that and apply it to the current circumstances in both my speech to the Watchers and my speech in Berlin last week. So I think for me, there are sort of three anchors of what our reaction function is. First of all, to remind you, target is 2% medium term, number one. Number two, symmetry. We discussed that today and we reaffirmed it. Number three, our reaction will depend on the type of deviation from target that we observe. Is it large and sustainable? In that case, obviously we have calibrated in our strategy review and in those two speeches that I have committed in the last few days. In that case, we have a forceful or persistent reaction to return inflation to our target of 2% medium term. That's our reaction function. And the data and the process that we use in order to deliver on that reaction function is what you see monetary policy statement after monetary policy statement. This is the inflation outlook with the risks associated with it, and as you will have seen in the MPS, we have a strong chapter on both risks to growth and risks to inflation. That's how we bring in the risks to growth, to your point. We also look at underlying inflation, and we look at the dynamics of monetary policy transmission. So we have the reaction function, the process, and the tools that we use in order to deliver on our target of 2% medium term. They are see-through anyway, and I don't need to repeat my Berlin speech as some of you have read it. You asked me about the scenarios. So we constantly monitor. We constantly take in new data, hard data, soft data, and survey results from all sorts of perspectives, and we verify, adjust, and take stock of all that. In June, six weeks from now, you will be receiving not only the projections that our staff will produce, but you will also see the scenarios as revised and updated as a result of the circumstances. Turning now to the participants via video link, I'd like to give the floor to Isabella Bufacchi of Il Sole 24 Ore. Isabella, please. Thank you for the opportunity. I have two questions on liquidity, which is key to the implementation of monetary policy but also crucial for financial stability in times of crisis like we have now. So President Lagarde, Euro area central bank reserves have almost halved from the peak in 2022 to 2.6 trillion in early 2026. And in the last bank lending survey, we found out that bank access to money market funding has deteriorated. So has the Governing Council been looking at the design of new liquidity tools—as you had a question before on your toolbox—new liquidity tools in the operational framework, for example, new structural longer-term credit operations or a structural portfolio of securities? And my second question is for Vice-President Luis de Guindos. At the end of your eight-year mandate and eight years of multiple crises, what is your assessment of the resilience of the banking system in terms of liquidity? And what about private credit players that do not have access to the ECB liquidity? Is all this enough? Thank you. Well, thank you so much. We miss you here, but it's nice to see you. So I'll take the first question and then I'll give the floor to the Vice-President. We still have an abundance of liquidity. You mentioned 2.6; I think it's a little down from that number, but it's a significant amount of excess liquidity in the system. So there is no shortage of liquidity. And as you know, we have a mechanism that works on the basis of access to liquidity—you know, it's a fixed interest rate full allotment with good collateral. It's working well and we're not seeing any tension in that respect. Did we discuss new tools? The answer is no. Yesterday on the agenda, we discussed the timetable and the methodology that will be adopted in-house by staff as well as within the Eurosystem. We are bringing all the committees into the loop to calibrate our operational framework, making sure that it is fit for purpose. It must be good from a financial stability point of view, as well as for the proper functioning of the provision of liquidity, which is obviously one of the missions that we have. With that, I'll turn the floor over to you, my dear Luis. Thank you. Thank you, President. If you look at our reply—the reply of the Eurosystem to the consultation process that was launched by the European Commission a couple of weeks ago and that we published a couple of weeks ago—you will realize that the main message embedded in that report is that we are in favor of a single jurisdiction for European banks with free flow of capital and liquidity. And I think that, you know, referring to the deal that you have indicated, that you have alluded to, well, I think that this principle applies very clearly. Next question goes to Olaf Storbeck of the Financial Times. Olaf, please. Thanks for taking my questions. My first question is on a scenario that some economists discuss at the moment, which is basically a return to the 2011 scenario where the ECB hiked relatively early on, then had to reverse after finding out that it basically pushed the economy further into stagnation. Is this a scenario which is on the top of the Governing Council's mind, and has it been discussed? What are your thoughts on that? And my second question is also to the Vice President on cross-border banking mergers in the Euro area, which has been, I think, kind of a favourite topic of the ECB in recent years. We now have a situation where the German government is quite openly opposing a potential merger. Do you have any views on those kind of actions of the German government, or at least their position? Thanks. Well, thank you for your first question. You know, we around the table of the Governing Council, and the staff of the ECB and the Eurosystem, are very attentive to our history. We don't have a long history, but we're paying attention to what has been done in the past. And you might argue that we, learning from history, will want to stay away from the risk of being too early—that's your reference to 2011—and the risk of being a little bit too late, which is what some of you would characterize for 2022. I would like to debate that forever, of course, but that's where we are. And we are going to be very attentive to the data, very attentive to inflation expectations, very attentive to supply chain disruptions, and we will look deeply into the depth of the wage agreements and the collective agreements that will be negotiated in the near future. We will also be attentive to selling prices. I mean, there is a whole range of data that we will be paying attention to in the next few weeks, and there will be plenty of it coming in to be certain that, from the good position we're in, we make the right decision. Our determination is to bring inflation to 2% in the medium term. Yes, there is an ocean of uncertainty, and things might change for the good or for the bad. But one thing is certain: we are determined to return inflation to 2% in the medium term. My dear Vice-President. Thank you. Thank you, President. If you look at our reply—the reply of the Eurosystem to the consultation process that was launched by the European Commission a couple of weeks ago and that we published a couple of weeks ago—you will realize that the main message embedded in that report is that we are in favor of a single jurisdiction for European banks with free flow of capital and liquidity. And I think that, you know, referring to the deal that you have indicated, that you have alluded to, well, I think that this principle applies very clearly. Next question now goes to Jean-Philippe Lacour of Agence France-Presse, AFP. Jean-Philippe, please. Thank you, Wolfgang. Bonjour Madame la Présidente. I'm wondering why in the statement there is this sentence: "the Governing Council will closely monitor the situation." We were just speaking about history, and I remember the Trichet times when he said "we are looking very closely at the situation," which was a pre-signal for an upcoming rate step. So I'm wondering why the need to add this in a communiqué, and what was the rationale behind this today? And on the other side of my second question, there is a stagnation in the economy, or quite the stagnation of the Eurozone economy. If the ECB were to raise its rates again, are you not in a dilemma then to change the situation from stagnation maybe to recession, because an aggressive rate hike could hamper the economy? So is this the dilemma you have to solve? Thank you. Well, thank you very much for your two questions. I think, with all due respect to my predecessors, including my dear friend Jean-Claude, I try not to be exceedingly coded. So it's not because I add one adverb or one adjective here or there that it is going to mean necessarily this or that. But, you know, this is on the basis of, number one, the hard data that we're getting. On the basis of the worsening of our risk assessment—you will have noted that the nice word 'tilted' has been removed—we are not saying long term versus short term. We are looking throughout the period and consider that the risk is to the downside for growth and to the upside for inflation. On the basis of that, and in view of the uncertainty that we have around us, we are bound to revisit the situation based on the data, the projections, the sensitivity analysis, and the updated scenarios, and then we will take a decision. I think directionally I know where we're heading, okay? But we shall see, as there might be massive changes taking place. There's one element that is going to have a real impact, and that is the duration of the conflict. So I'm not suggesting that because the conflict stops tomorrow, there will be no negative supply shock and consequences. There will be, we know that, because of the infrastructure, the logistics, the time lags, and all of that. But it would certainly have a significant impact, in particular on oil futures, for instance. So we are watching, and we are watching developments in our economy and the impact that this negative supply shock has on inflation and on activity. We will be looking at both. I think I dealt with the stagflation point, because that's really something that I park in the seventies. Given the projection that we have for March, you know, 0.8% growth followed by 1.2 followed by 1.3, I wouldn't call that stagnation, sorry. It's lower growth, granted, in '26, but we're not in stagnation, let alone recession. Now, you can imagine scenarios where we are heading towards those situations. But this is not what we are seeing for the moment. Thank you. And now Andres Stumpf of L'Expansion. Andres, please. Thank you. Madame Lagarde, with the risk of boring you again... You never bore me. I'll have to ask again about today's decision because I don't really get if the decision is taken, or if we are just waiting to see whether we are in the bad scenario or in the worst scenario. And even the baseline one, which is bad by itself, contemplates or has embedded two interest rate hikes. I don't really get what the ECB could be waiting for. And a second question: some leaders advocated last week in Cyprus for activating the escape clause in the fiscal rules, and I would like to know the ECB's opinion on this. Thank you. Sorry, can you repeat the last, the second question? The fiscal escape clause. Some leaders in Cyprus last week advocated for activating it and I would like to know whether the ECB would push for that or against that. Thank you. Thank you for your first question, and thank you for reminding everyone that in the design of our baseline and of our two scenarios, one adverse and one severe, we had in those situations two rate hikes which were embedded in both the baseline and the scenario given the cutoff date that we took. And as you know, this has changed over the course of time. It goes from two to one, to three. But two was certainly the one, and that means that our reaction function is well understood, right? Second, I would observe that there has been recently some financial tightening, which is also, you know, doing part of the work. But the reason we're not, and we've debated that yesterday and this morning, the reason we are not implementing what is embedded today is that number one, we start from this good position that gives us time to actually revisit all this hard data, the soft data coming in and that will be confirmed by multiple additional information. That's point number one. Point number two, we are not seeing second-round effects. Okay, so we are seeing direct effects, granted. We are seeing some indirect effects, but we are certainly not seeing second-round effects. And that is something that we need to be very attentive to. We are receiving not contrarian, but inconsistent information when we look. And I read the results of the corporate telephone survey, for instance. There is no intention on the part of the corporate leaders to significantly increase wages. There is a labor market that is more fluid than it was. And as I said, you know, the financial tightening is happening. So for all those reasons, we want to give ourselves the time, the depth, and the necessary analysis to determine in June what is necessary in order to reach our 2% target. And now... Oh, fiscal rules, of course. You know, we had the pleasure of welcoming Valdis Dombrovskis for part of our meeting yesterday afternoon and today, and we discussed that. We understand that effectively the escape clause has been mentioned by several countries and that this is under review by the Commission. This is not an area over which we have any kind of jurisdiction or authority. What we can say, because it has an impact on inflation, is that whatever fiscal support is granted, whether it's in the form of additional income or tax exemptions, has to be temporary, targeted, and tailored. We think that the price signal in particular should not be eliminated by the measures that are temporarily and in a targeted way offered to citizens. Stefan Riecher of Handelsblatt, Stefan please. Thank you, Madame President. I have a question, a follow-up on the term stagflation once more, which you called "flashy", I believe. That's why you come back to it. Yes. And I'm asking that because quite some analysts and economists are using that term right now to describe the situation that we are in now. Is there a fundamental misunderstanding in some way of the economic situation by economists using this term, or is this just mincing words? And my second question is on what you just repeated, the three Ts: targeted, temporary, and tailored. Do you have a sense that governments are adhering to these principles, or do you see signs or actions that worry you? Thank you. Yeah. So on your first question, I will not repeat what I said, although I will, actually. So, stagflation is a characterization which in our view is associated with the 70s—an entirely different situation. And we contend that our monetary policy framework is strong enough and our determination is solid enough that we will bring inflation back to 2%. What happened in those days is that inflation was on this running course and continuously without being brought back and tamed. We will tame inflation. So we will not find ourselves in that situation. Added to which, as I said, 0.8% followed by 1.2 followed by 1.3 is not what I would call stagnation. It's lower growth than what we had anticipated before the war, unfortunately. But... So, your second question... The 3 Ts. Are the 3 Ts. Yes. So: targeted, temporary, tailored. Some people add a fourth T, by the way, which is transformational, so that the measures that are taken actually take us in the direction of some structural reform element. Our great staff has done a calculation of how much GDP has already been committed in terms of these Ts. Based on what had been committed by then—and that was, I think, as of April 21st, though I stand to be corrected—it equaled 0.1% of euro area GDP by the various governments. Now some governments had, you know, more fiscal space than others. Certain governments didn't pay attention to one of the Ts. I think the temporary one is holding. But we keep repeating, and I hope it sinks in, that temporary, targeted, and tailored are all necessary in order to navigate through this period. Thank you. For our last question we turn back to video participants and it's for Chelsea Dulany of the Wall Street Journal. Chelsea, please. Don't go, it's not over. Yeah, thanks for taking my question. So you spoke a bit about propagation. I was hoping you could just give a little more clarity on the timeline for when we might expect to see some of these second-round effects. Like, we're two months into this now; is it June, or is it sometime later? Yeah, what is the timeline for when we might expect to see second-round effects? And then just to clarify this point: is there any scenario in which the ECB doesn't react to these higher energy prices? Looking at the baseline, what's already in the pipeline, and the likelihood that getting energy flows restarted takes months, is there any situation in which there's no need to react at all? First of all, the ECB will always react because we will take inflation back to 2%. So whatever the reaction, we will react. As for the time for propagation, we believe that by June we will have a lot more information that will help us revisit, ascertain, and verify to see whether or not we have second-round effects. And as I said, the number of indicators will include many, many elements from wage-setting, hiring, and selling price revisions. We will also monitor commodity prices—I should have mentioned that one first: commodity prices, because it's both oil and gas, but also all the derivatives and everything that is actually channeled through the Strait of Hormuz in particular. We will take all that into account, and we will continuously do that. So, before you all go, I would like to take advantage of my position to actually turn the floor over to my dear Vice President, my friend and colleague Luis de Guindos, and ask him his take from eight years at the helm of the ECB together with a President. Well, thank you, thank you very much, President. Let me say first that it has been a real honor to accompany you and Wolfgang for more than six years to this press conference. I think that is really enlightening and perhaps to say in my view that this press conference, you know, from my standpoint is one of the most useful, relevant instruments that we have to communicate our views, our policy decisions, our, you know, the assessment of financial stability situations. And I think that the dialogue that normally, you know, you respond and you ask questions is something that I think that is very, very, very useful. So you play a role here; you play a very important role, you know, in this press conference. Very often I have found that your questions were the correct ones, that were smart, intelligent, and sometimes a little bit uncomfortable. But I think that, you know, to ask complicated questions is part of the job of a good journalist. You know, sometimes you know on the other side well you can say, well, why they are asking me about that? But I think that is your real, real obligation, and it's your mission, and you have accomplished that over the last eight years—over the last eight years that I have been coming to this press conference. Thank you very much, Vice President. And with that...

WATCH LIVE: Senate Banking Committee votes on Kevin Warsh's nomination for Federal Reserve chair

PBS NewsHour(5.3M)·20:56·3.7K views·2026-04-29
Kevin WarshNominee for Federal Reserve Chair
TL;DRThe Senate Banking Committee voted 13-11 to advance Kevin Warsh’s nomination for Federal Reserve Chair to the full Senate, following a contentious debate regarding central bank independence and allegations of executive interference by the Trump administration.
TL;DR The Senate Banking Committee voted 13-11 to advance Kevin Warsh’s nomination for Federal Reserve Chair to the full Senate, following a contentious debate regarding central bank independence and allegations of executive interference by the Trump administration. The Vote:...

TL;DR

The Senate Banking Committee voted 13-11 to advance Kevin Warsh’s nomination for Federal Reserve Chair to the full Senate, following a contentious debate regarding central bank independence and allegations of executive interference by the Trump administration.

Committee Vote and Procedural Context

  • The Vote: The committee moved in-block to report Kevin Warsh favorably for a seat on the Board of Governors (term expiring 2040) and a four-year term as Chairman.
      • The final tally was 13 in favor and 11 opposed, reflecting a strict party-line split.
  • The Trigger for the Markup: Senator Elizabeth Warren (Ranking Member) noted that the vote only proceeded after US Attorney Jenny Durkan announced the closure of a criminal investigation into current Fed Chair Jerome Powell, a move Warren characterized as a political "trigger" to allow the nomination to move forward.
  • DOJ Assurances: Senator Thom Tillis defended the timing, stating he received personal assurances from the Department of Justice that the investigation into the Fed was being handled appropriately by the Inspector General, effectively resolving his previous concerns about the probe.

Allegations of Executive Overreach

  • Independence Concerns: Democrats, led by Senators Warren and Raphael Warnock, argued that advancing Warsh facilitates an "illegal attempt to seize control" of the Federal Reserve.
      • Threats to Personnel: Critics highlighted President Trump’s repeated threats to fire Chair Powell and his ongoing legal attempts to remove Governor Lisa Cook.
      • Ongoing Investigations: Senator Warren entered statements into the record from the President and DOJ officials suggesting that criminal investigations into current Fed leadership are "not dropped" but merely paused or shifted to different agencies.
  • The "Sock Puppet" Critique: Senator Warren labeled Warsh a "Trump sock puppet," citing his refusal during hearings to acknowledge that Donald Trump lost the 2020 election as evidence that he lacks the independence required for the role.

Candidate Fitness and Financial Transparency

  • Financial Disclosures: Senator Warren raised concerns over Warsh’s personal finances, noting he holds more than $100 million in "secret financial assets."
      • Divestment Plan: Critics argued Warsh has failed to disclose if his plan to divest involves significant financial backing from billionaire investors.
  • Past Performance: Opponents pointed to Warsh's tenure as a Fed Governor during the 2008 financial crisis, describing his record as "catastrophic" for American families while favoring Wall Street.
  • Refusal to Answer "Hypotheticals": Senator Warnock expressed deep dissatisfaction with Warsh’s written responses to questions.
      • Firing Scenario: When asked if he would leave the Fed if the President attempted to unlawfully fire him, Warsh responded that he would "not answer hypothetical questions," a stance Warnock called "woefully insufficient" given the President's actual attempts to fire sitting governors.

Economic Outlook and "Bidenomics"

  • Republican Perspective: Republican members argued that Warsh is "battle-tested" and uniquely qualified to lead the country out of the "bind of Bidenomics."
      • Inflation Control: Proponents blamed the previous administration for 9% inflation and argued that Warsh would provide a more consistent, less political focus on the needs of American households.
  • Democratic Perspective: Senator Warnock countered that the current "Trump economy" is failing working families, particularly in Georgia, and that the Fed must remain independent to protect jobs and affordability rather than serve the President’s "poll numbers."

Notable Quotes

"A vote today by this committee to advance Mr. Warsh will bring the President one step closer to completing his illegal attempt to seize control of the Fed and to artificially juice the economy."

— Senator Elizabeth Warren, Ranking Member

"Kevin Warsh's leadership is absolutely essential now at the Federal Reserve than ever before. Frankly, his time as a former governor of the Federal Reserve during the crisis makes him battle-tested and ready to serve and not only serve, but to lead."

— Senate Banking Committee Republican (In support of the nomination)

"I asked, 'If President Trump or any future president attempts to unlawfully fire you without cause, would you leave?'... His response: 'I will not answer hypothetical questions of this nature.' Well, this isn't a hypothetical question."

— Senator Raphael Warnock

"I’ve got confidence that this investigation is over... [Senator Warren] is flatly wrong on every point she just tried to make."

— Senator Thom Tillis

Comments, I'm going to turn to our ranking member for some opening comments. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Mr. Chairman, we should not be voting today on Kevin Warsh's nomination to lead the Federal Reserve. The Trump economy is in real trouble. Inflation is up, job creation is down, the stink of stagflation is in the air, and President Trump is getting desperate. A vote today by this committee to advance Mr. Warsh will bring the President one step closer to completing his illegal attempt to seize control of the Fed and to artificially juice the economy. Trump has not been subtle about his takeover. He's threatened to fire the Fed chair, he's actually tried to fire a Fed governor, and he started not one but two bogus criminal investigations to push them out. When this committee refused to move on his new nominee under such circumstances, US Attorney Jenny Durkan announced that she would, quote, "close the bogus criminal investigation into Fed chair Powell while the Federal Reserve inspector takes up the inquiry." This was the trigger for getting to today's markup. Now, I understand wanting to believe that there is finally an end to the President's demands to take over the Fed. But no one is fooled. Trump is still going after control of the Fed and he is keeping the threat of bogus criminal charges alive until he gets what he wants. Don't take it from me; take it from President Trump, who said the investigation is, quote, "not dropped," or take it from the President's spokeswoman who said, quote, "the investigation still continues," or take it from US Attorney Durkan who said that she, quote, "will not hesitate to restart a criminal investigation." Take it from acting Attorney General Todd Blanche who said, quote, "this is an investigation, so I'm not going to prejudge what the investigation will find and say there's absolutely going to be charges or absolutely not going to be charges." Mr. Chairman, I ask that these statements be entered into the record. Without objection? President Trump is also still trying to fire Fed Governor Lisa Cook, taking it all the way to the United States Supreme Court, and the DOJ's reported criminal probe against her is ongoing. These are uniquely dangerous circumstances to advance any Fed Chair nominee. But Mr. Warsh is uniquely an unfit candidate. He had a catastrophic tenure as a Fed governor before, during and after the 2008 financial crisis. Despite bailing out Wall Street and selling out American families, he has said repeatedly he has no regrets. If confirmed, he will enter the office with more than $100 million in secret financial assets. He won't even disclose whether his plan to divest relies on a billionaire investor cutting him a massive check. At his hearing last week, Mr. Warsh dug the hole deeper. He had a chance to demonstrate just a tiny bit of independence from Mr. Trump, and he just couldn't do it. Mr. Warsh is a Trump sock puppet who is so cowed by the President that he could not even say that Donald Trump lost the 2020 election. Members of this committee who vote for Mr. Warsh and help facilitate President Trump's takeover of the Central Bank will come to regret it. Unfortunately, it will be American families that will pay the price. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Thank you. This committee will come to order. We now have a quorum. I call the committee into executive session. We will now move to an en bloc on Mr. Kevin Warsh to be a member of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve for a term expiring in 2040 and chairman for a term of four years. The clerk will call the roll. Mr. Chairman. Aye. Mr. Crapo. Aye. Mr. Rounds. Aye. Mr. Tillis. Aye. Mr. Kennedy. Aye. Mr. Hagerty. Hagerty's Aye. Miss Lummis. Aye. Mrs. Britt. Aye. Mr. Ricketts. Aye. Mr. Banks. Aye. Mr. Cramer. Aye. Mr. Moreno. Aye. Mr. McCormick. Aye. Miss Warren. Sheila. Miss Warren. No by proxy. Mr. Reed. No by proxy. Mr. Warner. No by proxy. Mr. Van Hollen. No by proxy. Miss Cortez Masto. No by proxy. Miss Smith. No by proxy. Mr. Warnock. No. Mr. Kim. No by proxy. Mr. Gago. No by proxy. Miss Blunt Rochester. No by proxy. Miss Ossoff Brooks. No by proxy. Mr. Chairman, the vote is 13 in favor and 11 opposed. Senator Tillis is down. Recognized? I don't want to delay things. Look, I I just disagree with everything the ranking member had to say. Everybody, it's no secret that the reason that Mr. Warsh's nomination could have been held up is because of my concern with the investigation. I want to thank the Department of Justice for the assurances that they gave me. They are going through with the appeal. The appeal has no impact on reopening the investigative investigation. They have a matter that they want to settle, which is fine with me. I've got confidence that this investigation is over, that Horowitz, who I believe is one of the best inspector generals in the whole of government, is going to study this. If he has referral power, if he believes that there was criminal activity, then he would refer it. But the fact of the matter is, this was based on two minutes of testimony that was not criminal. Seven witnesses on this committee agreed with it. It's done and I think that Senator Warren, I get that she wants to score some political points, but she is flatly wrong on every point she just tried to make. Thank you. Yes, Senator. I reserved the right to provide some comments and perspective as well as color on the comments as well. Kevin Warsh's leadership is absolutely essential now at the Federal Reserve than ever before. Frankly, his time as a former governor of the Federal Reserve during the crisis makes him battle tested and ready to serve and not only serve, but to lead. I think it's incredibly important that we break the bind of Bidenomics in the households of Americans all across this nation. Let us never forget that it was the Biden administration that took inflation to 9% and interest rates followed. And as a result, we find ourselves in an economy that is actually improving because the American people have the good conscience to change leadership of this nation to President Trump. And as a result, we find ourselves now in an economy that is improving. But let's not be cute about it. Digging out of the hole created by Bidenomics will take time, and that's what we need. We need a Federal Reserve that is responsive to the needs of the American families more than the political will of whoever is in the White House. I believe that the Federal Reserve will be more focused, more consistent with those needs than we've seen in the last several years. Thank you. That's before you close but the Senator wants to make... Senator Warnock, thank you very much, brother chair. I oppose Mr. Warsh's nomination today, and it comes at a critical time in our nation. I respectfully disagree with the chair that the economy is improving. This, this is Trump's economy, and I don't think the folks back at home in Georgia, a year and a half into Mr. Trump's administration, believe that this is somebody body else's economy other than Donald Trump. Thank you, Bill. Appreciate it. Which is why today's nomination is critically important. It is a nomination that has been tainted by the real and persistent threats that President Trump has made to members of the Federal Reserve Board of Governors. The independence of the Fed is foundational to our system. Mr. Warsh is correct that presidents and political leaders have previously called on the Federal Reserve to lower interest rates, but this is not just about interest rates. President Trump's actions, including vicious attacks on current chair Jerome Powell, as well as his illegal attempt to fire Governor Lisa Cook Lisa Cook should give all of us pause and deep concern. And yes, the President continues to threaten to illegally fire chair Powell. This is not normal, and any effort to characterize it as such is blatantly false. We are not dealing with business as usual. These are not normal times. During last week's hearing, we were limited to just five minutes of questioning, and the chair encouraged me and others to submit additional written questions for the record, which I did, with an open mind to this nomination and with the hope that Mr. Warsh would use this opportunity to provide us and the American people with more clarity into how he views the Fed's dual mandate, Fed independence, and the state of the American economy. This is particularly important when it comes to maximizing employment and making sure folks can afford to provide for their families. The written answers I and others on this committee got back were so woefully insufficient that I was left wondering if Mr. Warsh even read our questions. Did he take this process seriously? I want to read just one example. I asked, quote, "if President Trump or any future president attempts to unlawfully fire you without cause, would you leave? Would you leave the Federal Reserve?" His response, quote, "I will not answer hypothetical questions of this nature." That was his answer. Well, this isn't a hypothetical question. In fact, the President attempted to fire Governor Cook in the past year, and the President has repeatedly mused about firing Chair Powell because he won't bend to his interest rate demands, doing so as recently as two weeks ago. And Mr. Warsh says he won't answer hypothetical questions. These are the sorts of answers that matter when looking at the future of the Fed and whether the body remains an independent steward of the American economy. This matters to millions of families all across this country, even as they're trying to make their lives work in this tough Trump economy. America's markets are the envy of the world. Investors flock to the United States in large part because they trust that the Federal Reserve board is free from political influence and political pressure. And working families in Georgia and across the country struggling in this Trump economy deserve to know that the Fed is on their side, on their side, maximizing their chances to keep a good paying job and keeping their lives affordable, not on the side of the President's poll numbers or his political concerns as we approach the midterm. Fed independence is not theoretical. It matters to the everyday lives of working families. And for that reason, I vote no. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Yes, sir, thank you. The ayes have it. The nominations are hereby ordered reported favorably to the full Senate without any further statements. The executive session is now adjourned.
